meaningMnemonic: Lying on the <radical>ground</radical> is something that looks just like the ground, the number <kanji>One</kanji>. Why is this One lying down? It's been shot by the number two. It's lying there, bleeding out and dying. The number One doesn't have long to live.
readingMnemonic: As you're sitting there next to <kanji>One</kanji>, holding him up, you start feeling a weird sensation all over your skin. From the wound comes a fine powder (obviously coming from the special bullet used to kill One) that causes the person it touches to get extremely <reading>itchy</reading> (いち).

meaningMnemonic: The kanji <kanji>two</kanji> and the radical <radical>two</radical> are the exact same things. Therefore, you know the kanji for two already!
readingMnemonic: How do you count to <vocabulary>two</vocabulary>? Just use someone's <reading>knee</reading> (に), and then their other knee. There — two knees!

meaningMnemonic: Nice! This kanji is the same as the radical that looks just like it (down to the meaning, even!). The radical is <radical>nine</radical> and the kanji is <kanji>nine</kanji>, making the meaning of this kanji really easy to remember as long as you know the radical first.
readingMnemonic:
Although this kanji has two on'yomi to learn, the pronunciations of those two on'yomi are very similar, so that should make them easier to remember.

When you were <kanji>nine</kanji> your favorite foods were <reading>coo</reading>kies (く) and <reading>cu</reading>cumbers (きゅう). Yum yum!

meaningMnemonic: Lucky for you (as is the case with many of the lower-stroke kanji), this kanji is the same as the radical you've already learned. The radical that makes up this kanji is <radical>seven</radical> and the kanji (which is exactly the same) is also <kanji>seven</kanji>. So, if you know the radical, you know the meaning of the kanji as well.
readingMnemonic:
You know this kanji means <kanji>seven</kanji>, so let's use that to remember the reading too. <kanji>Seven</kanji> is a lucky number, usually, but this time it isn't so good: 

<kanji>Seven</kanji> percent is all she got on her test after <reading>she chea</reading>ted (しち) on it.

meaningMnemonic: Remember how the radical version of this kanji meant <radical>person</radical> because it looks like a person with their arms down and their legs spread out? Well this is a <kanji>person</kanji> too!
readingMnemonic: Only one <kanji>person</kanji> at <reading>Nin</reading>tendo (にん) complained about the new <reading>jean</reading>s (じん) policy, and that person was you.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the radical are the same, so remembering the meaning of this kanji is as simple as making sure you know the radical for <radical>enter</radical> first! If you know the radical, you know this kanji means <kanji>enter</kanji> as well.
readingMnemonic: Turns out, after you <kanji>enter</kanji> the tent (remember how the person in the radical is entering one?), you're given a <reading>new</reading> (にゅう) one! Why? No idea, but hey — it's new!

meaningMnemonic: This isn't exactly the same as the <radical>fins</radical> radical, but it's close. Sometimes this will happen (where we have to use a slightly "modified radical" version of the original), so it's good to understand that now, early on. In this case, we have fins. How many fins? <kanji>Eight</kanji> fins. How do you know that? Because you caught four fish with two fins each.
readingMnemonic: After you count and smell all <kanji>eight</kanji> of your fins, it's time to put them away. You open a large <reading>hatch</reading> (はち) and put them in. This is where you keep your fins, always putting in eight at a time because that's what the boss wants.

meaningMnemonic: The radical and the meaning of this kanji are the same. Both are <kanji>power</kanji>.
readingMnemonic: Who has the most power of anyone? It's the <kanji>Power</kanji> Rangers. They are battling their arch enemy <reading>Ricky</reading> (りき). They defeat him and <reading>lock</reading> (りょく) him up, so that he can't hurt anyone ever again.

meaningMnemonic: This <radical>cross</radical> is the symbol for the number <kanji>ten</kanji>. A good way to remember this is to think about all the religions that have crosses and also important sets of tens.
readingMnemonic: This cross in particular has <kanji>ten</kanji> <reading>jew</reading>els (じゅう) on it. Two on each arm and two in the middle. That's ten jewels!

meaningMnemonic: This kanji is made up of the ground radical (which also happens to be the kanji for "one") and the radical for two. Combine <radical>one</radical> and <radical>two</radical> together and what do you get? <kanji>Three</kanji>!
readingMnemonic: <kanji>Three</kanji> is the number of chances you get with <reading>San</reading>ta-san (さん). He has a list, and when you get to three "naughty things" lines, that's it. You're on Santa-san's three strikes and you're out list.

meaningMnemonic: You find a <radical>toe</radical> on the <radical>ground</radical>. It's weird, because it's <kanji>above</kanji> the ground, not where toes belong.
readingMnemonic: Of course when you find a toe <kanji>above</kanji> the ground, you want to know where the toe came from. When you examine it, you see a name written on there. This toe belongs to the local clumsy farmhand, <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう).

meaningMnemonic: There's the <radical>ground</radical>, and there's a <radical>toe</radical> just <kanji>below</kanji> it. Yep, you know this means "below" because the ground has a toe below it in this kanji.
readingMnemonic: You keep digging in the ground for the toe <kanji>below</kanji>, but instead you hit something hard and metallic. You try to dig around it, but it's huge. Turns out it's a <reading>car</reading> (か). Keep digging until you unearth the car below. (Inside, you find the toe you were looking for.)

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>mouth</radical> radical and the <kanji>mouth</kanji> kanji are exactly the same!
readingMnemonic:
Do you remember seeing the construction worker こういち earlier in the 工 mnemonic? If you haven't yet, not to worry!

For kanji with こう readings (there are a lot of them!) we will use the character こういち every time. He's one of the people who started this website, so we'll do our best in the mnemonics to embarrass him as much as possible. Anyways, remember that whenever you see こう, think こういち.

Everyone agrees, the <kanji>mouth</kanji> is <reading>こう</reading>いち's best feature. You stare at it lovingly as he whispers sweet kanji readings to you.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>big</radical> radical and the <kanji>big</kanji> kanji are exactly the same!
readingMnemonic: There's this big guy (that's how you remember the radical, remember?), and he's wearing a huge <reading>Tie Dye</reading> (たい, だい) shirt. Because he's so huge, the tie dye shirt is also super huge. Everything about him is pretty big, but the tie dye stands out more than anything.

meaningMnemonic: The radical for <radical>woman</radical> and the kanji for <kanji>woman</kanji> are exactly the same.
readingMnemonic: When you think of a <kanji>woman</kanji>, think of <reading>Jo</reading>-Anne (じょ), though she usually goes by just "Jo."

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>mountain</radical> radical and the <kanji>mountain</kanji> kanji are the same.
readingMnemonic: Think about <kanji>mountains</kanji> talking to each other, calling each other by their names and adding the Japanese name-ender <reading>san</reading> (さん) to each of their names. "Hello, Everest-<reading>san</reading>." "Oh hi, Fuji-<reading>san</reading>."

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>river</radical> radical and the <kanji>river</kanji> kanji are the same as each other!
readingMnemonic: You're standing next to a <kanji>river</kanji> that's actually used as a <reading>ca</reading>r <reading>wa</reading>sh (かわ).

meaningMnemonic:
The <radical>construction</radical> radical and the <kanji>construction</kanji> kanji are the same!

This kanji also means <kanji>industry</kanji>, which is what construction is, if you think about it. It's all part of the construction industry.
readingMnemonic:
Every time a kanji uses the こう reading (there are a lot!), we'll use the character こういち. He's one of the people who started this website, so we'll do our best in the mnemonics to embarrass him as much as possible. Like this:

You see some <kanji>construction</kanji> in front of you. There, standing shirtless (only suspenders and a hard hat) is <reading>こう</reading>いち, looking back at you.

meaningMnemonic: This radical consists of a single, horizontal stroke. What's the biggest, single, horizontal stroke? That's the <radical>ground</radical>. Look at the ground, look at this radical, now look at the ground again. Kind of the same, right?

This radical kind of looks like a <radical>toe</radical>. See the one weird toe coming off the side of it? This also looks like the katakana character ト (to), which sounds like the word "toe." So whenever you see ト, just think toe.

If you look at this radical in just the right way, you'll notice that it kind of looks like the letters "n" and "i" (the "i" comes from the little flippy-up stroke on the right side). What do the letters "n" and "i" start spelling? Why, the word <radical>nine</radical>.

Watch out! This isn't the person radical, it's something else. What's the difference? The right side is longer than the left. This radical also has that extra part sticking out at the top. See it? It kind of looks like a person lowering their head to go inside a tent or something. That's why this radical is <radical>enter</radical>.
Imagine a guy flexing his arm downward to show off his steroid-pumped arm. It's huge. It's so full of <radical>power</radical>. RARRRWWWRRR!!

This looks like an enclosure of sorts — that's why it's a <radical>prison</radical>. Imagine a tiny prisoner curled up inside, staring off into space, completely convinced they're trapped forever. Ironically this prison is actually wide open. They're just so consumed with self-pity that they haven't realized they've been free to leave all along.
This radical looks like a <radical>cross</radical>. I mean, just look at it. Two bars going through each other = a cross!
A big, gaping, square hole represents someone opening their giant <radical>mouth</radical> to tell you something stupid.

This radical looks just like a stick figure—more specifically, a really <radical>big</radical> guy with his arms stretched out nice and big. When you see this radical, just think big, because this guy is really BIG!
This radical looks like a curtsying <radical>woman</radical>. Another way to think of it could be as two stacked Xs, since women generally have XX chromosomes. That's why this is the woman radical.
These look like <radical>fins</radical>. What's a pair of disembodied fins doing here?! Some fish must have left them here by accident. Bet they're really missing these fins right about now.
This is a <radical>mountain</radical>. See the big peak in the middle and the smaller ones on either side? This totally looks like a mountain.

This radical already looks like a <radical>river</radical>. See how the lines represent the flow of the river? There's even a slight bend in this river. So when you see this radical, you should think "river."

Since there are no circles in Japanese characters, sometimes rectangles or squares have to make do. In this case, this big rectangle is the <radical>sun</radical>. The middle line is a cloud, moving across the sun. Picture it, but don't actually look at the sun and burn your eyes!
Do you see the <radical>tree</radical> in here? It has branches, and then it has the foliage coming down diagonally from it, spreading out like a tree would. That's why this radical is a tree.

You know those big steel girders that are used for the frame of big buildings? This is like the end of one of those, and has a very distinct shape, just like this radical. These girders are used in construction, which is why this radical means <radical>construction</radical>. Funny thing is, you use these girders to put together buildings just like you're going to use this radical to put together kanji!

This little radical is a <radical>drop</radical>, maybe of water or some other liquid. Notice how it's only one drop? Sometimes radicals and kanji will have more than one drop in them, but don't let that throw you off.

What does this radical look like? Hmm, nothing much. What about if you turn it upside down? Now does it look like a 7 with a line through it? Close enough to help you to remember that this radical is <radical>seven</radical>, right? Just remember, when you see this radical, flip it over and see what number it looks like. If you can do that, you can remember the meaning!

Close your eyes and imagine you're a kid again. Now open them and... look! It's a <radical>slide</radical>! Imagine little you sliding down this slide over and over, having a great time. Imagination + emotion makes for good memorization!

This radical is shaped like a <radical>barb</radical>. Kind of like the barb you'd see on a barbed fishing hook. Imagine that thing getting stuck in your arm or something. Not exactly pleasant. Say out loud, "Oh dang, I got a barb stuck in me!" Alright, relax, it's only a little barb.
One line plus another line equals <radical>two</radical> lines. This radical represents the number two. Pretty simple, right? Can you guess what three looks like? I bet you can...
This looks like something you'd put on a kettle or a pot, right? It even has a little handle to make sure you don't burn yourself. This is some kind of <radical>lid</radical>.
This is a <radical>stick</radical>, sticking straight up. It even looks like a stick.
This looks a lot like a <radical>gun</radical> pointing to the right. You have a handle on the left, and the barrel pointing to the right. Imagine it hanging in a case on the wall in a museum.

This is a <radical>person</radical>. They're standing with their arms down at their sides and their legs spread out, which kind of makes them look like a torso with a pair of legs. Think of 人 as highlighting the bipedal nature of people. Walking on two legs? Probably a person.
As is the case with most vocab words that consist of a single kanji, this vocab word has the same meaning as the kanji it parallels, which is <vocabulary>one</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: When a vocab is a single kanji and all alone like this, it's usually going to use the kun'yomi. Numbers are an exception, however. When a number is all alone, with no kanji or okurigana, it is going to be the on'yomi reading, which you learned with the kanji.  Just remember this exception for alone numbers and you'll be able to read future number-related vocab to come.

meaningMnemonic: This word follows the "number of things" pattern where there's a kanji for a number plus つ on the end. Whenever you see this, you know the word means "____ things." Knowing that, as long as you know the kanji (which you do) you can figure out what number of things it is. For this one, it's the kanji for <kanji>one</kanji> plus つ. So, this one is <vocabulary>one thing</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
Since all of the "number of things" words all follow the same pattern (number plus つ), you really just have to remember the part before the つ (and then remember that the つ is in all of these). All of the readings are the kun'yomi reading, which means we'll have to use a mnemonic to remember them (you know the on'yomi reading of the kanji portion).

So, you have <vocabulary>one thing</vocabulary>. What one thing do you have? You have one of He-Man's Toes, aka a single <reading>He-Toe</reading> (ひと). So what's the thing you have only one of? You have one He-Toe. Imagine He-Man walking around with one missing toe, trying to find you so he can get it back. At least you'll be able to run faster than him now.

meaningMnemonic: When a vocab is alone with no okurigana (that's hiragana attached to a kanji) it usually has the same meaning as the kanji too. That goes for this word as well. So, if you know the kanji's meaning (<kanji>seven</kanji>) you'll know the vocab's meaning (<vocabulary>seven</vocabulary>).
readingMnemonic: Most single-kanji all-alone vocab words like this use the kun'yomi reading. Numbers are an exception to this rule. That being said, four and seven are exceptions to this exception to the rule (because the on'yomi readings sound too much like the word for "death"). You should learn both readings for this word, though we're going to go with なな for the main one here. You can remember that because you had seven <reading>nana</reading>s after you.

meaningMnemonic: This word follows the "number of things" pattern where there's a kanji for a number plus つ on the end. Whenever you see this, you know the word means "____ things." Knowing that, as long as you know the kanji (which you do) you can figure out what number of things it is. For this one, it's the kanji for <kanji>seven</kanji> plus つ. So, this one is <vocabulary>seven things</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
Since all of the "number of things" words all follow the same pattern (number plus つ), you really just have to remember the part before the つ (and then remember that the つ is in all of these). All of the readings are the kun'yomi reading, which means we'll have to use a mnemonic to remember them (you know the on'yomi reading of the kanji portion).

What thing do you have seven of? The <vocabulary>seven things</vocabulary> that you have are seven <reading>Nana</reading>s (なな). These are your grandmas, cloned. Just think, "It's lucky to have seven nanas. They can all cook my favorite foods for me!" What's more, you have one nana for every day of the week, so you'll get your favorite food every day.

meaningMnemonic: When a vocab is made up of a single kanji with no okurigana (hiragana attached to the kanji), it often has the same meaning as the kanji it's based off of. Same goes for the word <vocabulary>九</vocabulary>, as well. It has the same meaning as its kanji counterpart.
readingMnemonic: When a vocab is a single kanji and all alone like this, it's usually going to use the kun'yomi. Numbers are the exception, as you might already know. When they're alone with no okurigana they're the on'yomi reading, which you should already know from learning the kanji that makes up this word.

meaningMnemonic: This word follows the "number of things" pattern where there's a kanji for a number plus つ on the end. Whenever you see this, you know the word means "____ things." Knowing that, as long as you know the kanji (which you do) you can figure out what number of things it is. For this one, it's the kanji for <kanji>nine</kanji> plus つ. So, this one is <vocabulary>nine things</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: Since all of the "number of things" words all follow the same pattern (number plus つ), you really just have to remember the part before the つ (and then remember that the つ is in all of these). All of the readings are the kun'yomi reading, which means we'll have to use a mnemonic to remember them (you know the on'yomi reading of the kanji portion). What thing do you have nine of? The nine things you have are nine coconots (ここの). As you can guess from the spelling, coconots are not coconuts. Think of them as… fake coconuts. Imagine yourself juggling nine coconots in the air. Try counting them as you do, arriving at nine things. Then arrange them into a square on the ground. Three coconots by three. Now you should remember how to read 九つ.

meaningMnemonic: When a vocab word is a single kanji without okurigana (hiragana attached to the kanji) it usually has the same meaning as the kanji it's spawned from. If you know the kanji meaning you'll also know the vocab meaning. It's <vocabulary>two</vocabulary>!
readingMnemonic: When a vocab is a single kanji and all alone like this, it's usually going to use the kun'yomi. Numbers are an exception to this though. Numbers use the on'yomi reading, which you've already learned by learning the kanji's reading! How nice!

meaningMnemonic: This word follows the "number of things" pattern where there's a kanji for a number plus つ on the end. Whenever you see this, you know the word means "____ things." Knowing that, as long as you know the kanji (which you do) you can figure out what number of things it is. For this one, it's the kanji for <kanji>two</kanji> plus つ. So, this one is <vocabulary>two things</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
Since all of the "number of things" words all follow the same pattern (number plus つ), you really just have to remember the part before the つ (and then remember that the つ is in all of these). All of the readings are the kun'yomi reading, which means we'll have to use a mnemonic to remember them (you know the on'yomi reading of the kanji portion). 

So what thing do you have two of? The <vocabulary>two things</vocabulary> you have are 2-<reading>foo</reading>t <reading>ta</reading>cos (ふた). How lucky are you, that your two things are 2-foot tacos? Other people only have two foot-tacos... And trust me, you don't want to eat those.

meaningMnemonic: When a vocab is a single kanji all alone, it usually has the same meaning as its kanji parent. That goes for this vocab word as well. <kanji>Person</kanji> is <vocabulary>person</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
When a vocab is a single kanji and all alone like this, it's usually going to use the kun'yomi. Since you don't know the kun'yomi reading for this kanji yet, let's use a mnemonic to learn it.

What does a <vocabulary>person</vocabulary> hate more than anything? They hate extreme <reading>heat</reading> (ひと). You can always put on more clothes to stay warm, but when the heat gets too high, you can only take off so much.

meaningMnemonic: When you have <kanji>two</kanji> and <kanji>person</kanji> and then put them together, what do you get? You get a <vocabulary>pair</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>two people</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: The reading for this word is quite strange. If you know 二つ's reading, you can use that to remember the 二 part (ふた). But, the り that is the 人 is a total exception, something you won't see too often (though you may have seen it in 一人). If you can use the reading of 一人 (aka if you've learned it already) then definitely use that. If not, do your best to remember the reading on your own. It's a strange one that doesn't connect to much else.

meaningMnemonic: When there's <kanji>one</kanji> <kanji>person</kanji>, what are they? Well, they're either just going to be simply <vocabulary>one person</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>alone</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
The reading for this vocab doesn't follow any rules you learned previously. It's an exception, which means you have to learn the reading separately too. Spend a few moments trying to remember this word, look away for 10 seconds, and then try to recall its reading. Could you do it? Try again, this time in thirty seconds. Did you do it again? Okay, go ahead and move on. You may know it now, though you'll need to recall it again in the next 5-10 minutes if you want to remember it for good.

Alternatively, if you know the reading for the vocab word 一つ (in the same level as this word), you can know that it uses the same reading!

meaningMnemonic: This vocab word has okurigana, which is the hiragana part that comes after the kanji. When a vocab's okurigana makes an "う/oo" sound (in this case, we have a る), it's often going to be a verb. So in this case, the kanji 入 is <kanji>enter</kanji>. The vocab 入る is <vocabulary>to enter</vocabulary>. We're just taking the kanji and using it in a verb!
readingMnemonic:
When a vocab is a single kanji with okurigana, chances are it's going to be the kun'yomi reading, which you don't usually learn in the kanji (you usually learn the on'yomi reading). So, let's learn the kun'yomi reading for this word using its meaning.

When you <vocabulary>enter</vocabulary> the room, what do you do? You say "<reading>Hi</reading>" (はい) to everyone to the point of absurdity. "Hi there. Oh hi Frank. Oh hi there Susan. Oh hi there lamp."

meaningMnemonic: When a vocab is a single kanji it usually steals its meaning from its kanji as well. Same goes for this vocab word. It means <vocabulary>eight</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: When a vocab is a single kanji and all alone like this, it's usually going to use the kun'yomi. But, because this is a number (exception) it uses the on'yomi instead, meaning you already know the reading from when you learned the kanji!

meaningMnemonic: This word follows the "number of things" pattern where there's a kanji for a number plus つ on the end. Whenever you see this, you know the word means "____ things." Knowing that, as long as you know the kanji (which you do) you can figure out what number of things it is. For this one, it's the kanji for <kanji>eight</kanji> plus つ. So, this one is <vocabulary>eight things</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: Since all the "number of things" words follow the same pattern (number + つ), you just have to remember the part before the つ. All their readings use kun'yomi. So far you've only seen the on'yomi reading for this kanji though, so here's a mnemonic to help you remember it: Can you think of eight things that start with the sound "ya"? Yah (や), you can. Try listing them out loud. Even if you don't manage it, you'll remember the connection between eight and "ya." Oh, and see that small っ in the middle of the reading (やっつ)? It puts a little emphasis on the consonant that follows it, like yattttttsu. OK, not that much emphasis. More like yattsu.

meaningMnemonic: When a vocab word is made up of a single kanji and that's it, it almost always takes the meaning from its parent kanji. So, this vocab word also means <vocabulary>power</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
When a vocab is a single kanji and all alone like this, it usually uses the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn the kun'yomi reading with the kanji, so for this vocab word, we'll use a mnemonic:

The martial art that requires the most <vocabulary>power</vocabulary> is <reading>chee</reading>se <reading>kara</reading>te (ちから). Practitioners of cheese karate channel all their power into karate chopping large blocks of cheese. Cheese doesn't break as easily as bricks or tiles, so this actually requires a lot of <vocabulary>strength</vocabulary> and <vocabulary>ability</vocabulary> to do well.

meaningMnemonic: When a vocab is alone like this with no okurigana (hiragana attached to the kanji) it tends to steal its meaning from the kanji it came from. That means this vocab word also means <vocabulary>ten</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: When a vocab is alone and consists of a single kanji like this, usually it uses the kun'yomi reading for the kanji. But, numbers are an exception and they use the on'yomi reading (which you learned when you learned the kanji). That means you know that this word's reading is <reading>じゅう</reading> already.

meaningMnemonic: When a vocab is a single kanji and alone like this, it usually steals its meaning from the parent kanji. That goes for this word too, making the meaning of this word <vocabulary>three</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: When a vocab is a single kanji and all alone like this, it's usually going to use the kun'yomi. Numbers are an exception, though, and use the on'yomi reading. You learned the on'yomi reading when you learned this kanji, so you already know that the reading for this vocab is <reading>さん</reading>.

meaningMnemonic: This word follows the "number of things" pattern where there's a kanji for a number plus つ on the end. Whenever you see this, you know the word means "____ things." Knowing that, as long as you know the kanji (which you do) you can figure out what number of things it is. For this one, it's the kanji for <kanji>three</kanji> plus つ. So, this one is <vocabulary>three things</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
Since all of the "number of things" words all follow the same pattern (number plus つ), you really just have to remember the part before the つ (and then remember that the つ is in all of these). All of the readings are the kun'yomi reading, which means we'll have to use a mnemonic to remember them (you know the on'yomi reading of the kanji portion).

What thing do you have three of? The <vocabulary>three things</vocabulary> that you have are actually three versions of yourself. "Who is the real one?" you ask yourself. "It's <reading>me</reading>!" "Wait, no, it's <reading>me</reading>!" Just remember that there are three of you, and they're all saying "me me me me me" to try and prove that they're the real you.

meaningMnemonic: You have the kanji for <kanji>three</kanji> and <kanji>person</kanji>. Common sense will tell you what this word is. It's <vocabulary>three people</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo (kanji compound) word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Since 人 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use:

Apparently, there are <vocabulary>three people</vocabulary> working as <reading>San</reading>ta-san at <reading>Nin</reading>tendo (さんにん)! It's a big company, so obviously they need three Santas.

meaningMnemonic: When a vocab word is a single kanji and alone, it tends to steal the meaning from the kanji. Same goes for this one too. It means <vocabulary>above</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>up</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
When a vocab is a single kanji and all alone like this, it's usually going to use the kun'yomi. Since you learned the on'yomi reading of the kanji, we'll need to use a mnemonic to learn the reading of this vocabulary word. 

<vocabulary>Above</vocabulary> you is a huge weight. You're holding it up and struggling (it's heavy!). You look up and try to crane your neck to see how much it <reading>weigh</reading>s (うえ), but you can't see the numbers on the side of it. How long can you hold it above your head like this?

meaningMnemonic: This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>above</kanji> and this verb is related to that meaning. It means <vocabulary>to lift something</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>to raise something</vocabulary>. It's a transitive verb, meaning that you do something to something else. You raise something. Like your hands!
readingMnemonic:
Since this word has okurigana (hiragana attached to the kanji) you know that it's probably going to use the kun'yomi reading, which you didn't learn with the kanji. Here's a mnemonic to help you:

When you want <vocabulary>to lift something</vocabulary>, you always shout "<reading>Ah</reading> (あ)!" to get the extra power you need. Especially when you want to lift something over your head. Go ahead, try it now. Lift something up and shout "Ah!" Makes you feel powerful, right?

meaningMnemonic: When a vocab is a single kanji with no okurigana (that's hiragana attached to the kanji) it tends to steal the meaning of the kanji it comes from. So, this one is <vocabulary>below</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>under</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
When a vocab is a single kanji and is alone it tends to use the kun'yomi reading. You learned the on'yomi reading for this kanji, so now it's time to learn the kun'yomi reading to learn this vocabulary word.

What's <vocabulary>below</vocabulary> you? What's below your foot? Oh no. The worst thing possible: It's <reading>した</reading>.

meaningMnemonic: This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means below so the verb version of that is when you put something below you. It's to lower something. It can also mean to hang something, such as a lamp from the ceiling. This is a transitive verb, which means that the verb is done by one entity onto another. In other words, you lower something. Like your impossibly high expectations for yourself. Take it easy!
readingMnemonic:
Since this word has okurigana (hiragana attached to the kanji) you know that it's probably going to be the kun'yomi reading, which you didn't learn with the kanji. Here's a mnemonic to help you:

You want <vocabulary>to lower something</vocabulary>. In this case, it's a tree, so you need a <reading>saw</reading> (さ). Lower the tree to the ground gently while you slowly saw it down!

meaningMnemonic:
You know that the kanji 下 means <kanji>down</kanji>, and さい sounds like the word "sigh." You need to ask someone for something, so you look down, sigh, and say <vocabulary>please give me</vocabulary> that thing. You're ashamed that you have to ask. But you still need it. <vocabulary>Please</vocabulary>, please give it to me!

You only use this kanji version of ください when asking for something, which is why this is "please give me." If you're just saying "please," you don't need the kanji at all!
readingMnemonic:
The reading for 下 here is one you haven't learned. Just think about how it kind of sounds like you're saying <vocabulary>please</vocabulary>, <reading>could I</reading> (くだ) have this thing? Pretty please, could I have it? 

Alternatively, if you already knew the word ください beforehand, this shouldn't be too hard!

meaningMnemonic: When a vocab is made up of a single kanji with no okurigana (hiragana attached to the kanji) it's generally going to have the same meaning as the kanji it came from. From that, you know this word's meaning is <vocabulary>mouth</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
When a vocab is a single kanji and all alone like this, it's usually going to use the kun'yomi, which you did not learn from learning the kanji. To remember this word, you should use a mnemonic. With your <vocabulary>mouth</vocabulary>, make a "<reading>kuchi</reading> kuchi kou" (くち) sound. Go ahead, make it. Focus on your mouth and try to imagine each shape it makes while zooming in on it. You're talking to a baby, which is why you're making all these ridiculous noises. Also, instead of "coo" at the end, you're saying "kou," because that's the reading of the kanji (こう).

くちくちこう！くちくちこう！ Sounds like something from Arrested Development, actually.

meaningMnemonic: A <kanji>mouth</kanji> that you <kanji>enter</kanji> is an <vocabulary>entrance</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
This word is really two separate words put together to form one, so let's look at it in two parts:

The kanji 入 has り attached to it as okurigana (hiragana that comes after a kanji), so you can bet that 入り will use a kun'yomi reading for the kanji. You haven't learned this reading yet, so here's a mnemonic to help you:

The <vocabulary>entrance</vocabulary> you're going through is really <reading>eerie</reading> (いり). It has cobwebs and creepy figures carved into it, and it just gives you a really eerie feeling.

Since 入り uses the kun'yomi, you can guess that 口 will follow suit. You learned this reading with the vocabulary, but the く in くち changes to ぐ due to something called rendaku. Think of it as an eerie entrance into the <reading>Gucci</reading> (ぐち) store.

meaningMnemonic: This is the adjective form of <kanji>big</kanji>. It has the same meaning, though it is a word for describing. Words that end with い are often adjectives, remember that for future words as well. So, as long as you know the meaning of this kanji, you can transfer that to the meaning of this word. It's the adjective <vocabulary>big</vocabulary>, or <vocabulary>large</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: What do you do when you see something really, really <vocabulary>big</vocabulary>? You say <reading>おお</reading> because you can't believe your eyes. Imagine something really large and say おお out loud to help seal the memory in that brain of yours.

meaningMnemonic: In Japanese, さ on the end of a word is often the equivalent of "-ness" in English. In other words, if you replace the い on the end of an い-adjective with さ, you have a noun. In this case, the adjective is 大きい (big) and the noun 大きさ means "bigness", aka <vocabulary>size</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
When a vocab has okurigana (hiragana attached to the kanji) it is probably going to be the kun'yomi reading. Since you learned the on'yomi reading with the kanji 大, we'll have to use a mnemonic to remember this word.

When you saw the size of (pick something surprising), you said "<reading>おお</reading>*#$@!" Really imagine something surprising. Say the おお part out loud, as well. Think about the size of whatever you're looking at, too, and how <vocabulary>big</vocabulary> it is.

meaningMnemonic: した is the past tense form of する (which means "to do"). So, essentially this word is "did big." What is something that "did big"? It's something that's <vocabulary>great</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>considerable</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
The reading for the 大 part is actually the on'yomi reading you learned when learning this kanji. Since 大 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use:

When something's <vocabulary>great</vocabulary> and <vocabulary>important</vocabulary>, you'd better wear a <reading>tie</reading> (たい) for it. No showing up without a tie to anything that's a <vocabulary>big deal</vocabulary>, okay?

meaningMnemonic: This word combines <kanji>big</kanji> and <kanji>person</kanji>. If you're a child, how do you think of adults? You just think of them like big people. That's why this combination of kanji means <vocabulary>adult</vocabulary>, and when used like an adjective, it means <vocabulary>mature</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
Sometimes kanji have exceptional readings that are neither on’yomi, nor kun’yomi, and this is one of them. So here’s a mnemonic to help you remember this reading:

"I'm an <vocabulary>adult</vocabulary>, which means I have adult responsibilities, like… <reading>oh, toner</reading> (おとな)!" Are you an adult with your own toner printer? Ohhhh, toner…

meaningMnemonic:
When a kanji is alone and doesn't have any okurigana (hiragana attached to the kanji) it's usually going to take the same meaning as the kanji it comes from. So in this case, you know that this word means <vocabulary>woman</vocabulary>.

But watch out! Using 女 on its own can often come off as blunt and disrespectful, especially in casual chats. We’ll introduce you to neutral equivalents soon!
readingMnemonic:
When a vocab is a single kanji and all alone like this, it's usually going to use the kun'yomi. You didn't learn the kun'yomi reading when you learned the kanji itself, so let's use a mnemonic to remember it:

Think about how this <vocabulary>woman</vocabulary> is <reading>onna</reading> (おんな) roll. She's winning left, right and center. She's unstoppable. Yep, she's truly onna roll!

meaningMnemonic: A vocab made of a single kanji all alone with no okurigana (hiragana attached to the kanji) usually has the same exact meaning as the kanji it's made from. So by that you know that this vocab's meaning is <vocabulary>mountain</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
When a vocab is a single kanji and all alone like this, it's usually going to use the kun'yomi, which you didn't learn when you learned the kanji. To remember this word's reading we'll use a mnemonic.

What are you doing going up into the <vocabulary>mountain</vocabulary>s? You're hunting for <reading>yam</reading>s (やま). Go ahead, imagine yourself doing just that, climbing up the mountain (it's hard work!) and then digging in the ground with your hands, then pulling up that sweet, delicious yam! Mmm!

meaningMnemonic: Just look at this word. What do you think it means? That's right - it's a name (Fuji) plus the kanji for <kanji>mountain</kanji>. Put those together, and you have <vocabulary>Mount Fuji</vocabulary>. In English the "Mount(ain)" part comes first. In Japanese, it comes at the end, sort of like the name ender さん, ちゃん, and so on. You can add 山 to the end of any mountain's name too, not just Mount Fuji.
readingMnemonic: Normally ふじ would be in kanji, but I wanted you to see this kanji use in action since it's more useful than just being used on Mount Fuji. This is treated like a jukugo word (combo kanji word). In the case of jukugo, usually the on'yomi reading is used, meaning you just have to remember the on'yomi reading of 山. Luckily, you learned this when you were learning the kanji, so you should already know it!

meaningMnemonic: When a vocab is made up of a single kanji with no okurigana (hiragana attached to the kanji) it will usually take the same meaning as the kanji it comes from. So, from that you know that this word means <vocabulary>river</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: You're in luck — the reading you learned with the kanji is the same as the one you have to learn for this vocab!

meaningMnemonic: The amount of people with mouths (that you have to feed) is the <vocabulary>population</vocabulary> that you have to feed.
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Since 人 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use:

Here at Tofugu, we measure <vocabulary>population</vocabulary> in <reading>jean</reading>s (じん). Each of us has just one pair of jeans, so if you know the jean count you know the population.

meaningMnemonic: A <kanji>person</kanji> of <kanji>industry</kanji> makes a lot of things by hand, so everything they make is <vocabulary>artificial</vocabulary>, and also means it's <vocabulary>man made</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Since 人 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use:

Remember this by thinking about how <reading>jean</reading>s (じん) are <vocabulary>artificial</vocabulary>. You've never seen jeans growing on trees, right? Nope, jeans are 100% <vocabulary>man made</vocabulary>.

meanings: As Much As One Can Muster
meaningMnemonic:
You've hopefully already learned that 力 means "power." You might have also heard the word いっぱい, because it's used a lot in conversation. It has a few meanings (you'll learn more of them later) but the important one here is "full" or "a lot." Put these two words together and you have <vocabulary>full power</vocabulary>, <vocabulary>with all one's strength</vocabulary>, and <vocabulary>as hard as one can</vocabulary>. Remember this one with all your strength!

Just a side note: いっぱい can also be written in kanji, which you'll learn later on! However, in simple words like this one it's usually written in kana.
readingMnemonic: 力 uses the same reading as you learned with the vocabulary. That's because this is basically two separate words stuck together.

meaningMnemonic: The radical and the meaning of this kanji are almost the same! The radical is <radical>sword</radical>, but we're learning Japanese, so can you name a Japanese <kanji>sword</kanji>? That's right, the most common is a <kanji>katana</kanji>, which happens to be the meaning of this kanji as well.
readingMnemonic: So now you have this sweet <kanji>katana</kanji> in your hands and you're swinging it around like an idiot. Feel free to make whooshing noises as you swing it around. To become a master katana wielder, you decide to travel to <reading>とう</reading>きょう (Tokyo). とうきょう has the best sword and katana schools in the world, so it makes sense to travel to とうきょう.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>dirt</radical> radical and the <kanji>dirt</kanji> kanji are the same!
readingMnemonic: Who spends a lot of time in <kanji>dirt</kanji>? <reading>Do</reading>gs (ど) do! Dogs dig in dirt, they roll in dirt, sometimes they try to eat dirt (which isn't so great).

meaningMnemonic: This kanji is the same as the radical you learned, so it means <kanji>thousand</kanji>.
readingMnemonic: Remember the thousand people going down the slide on a cross in the radical 千? Turns out each one of them was carrying a <kanji>thousand</kanji> <reading>cen</reading>ts (せん). Go ahead and try to calculate what a thousand times a thousand cents would come out to. It's quite a lot, actually.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>evening</radical> radical and the <kanji>evening</kanji> kanji are the same. If you know the radical you know the meaning of the kanji.
readingMnemonic: The <kanji>evening</kanji> is when all the <reading>you</reading>ths (ゆう) come outside to play.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>child</radical> radical and the <kanji>child</kanji> kanji are exactly the same.
readingMnemonic:
The main reading here is し, so to remember it we'll use this mnemonic:

There is a <kanji>child</kanji> standing in front of you. You look closely at the child but something seems wrong. You rub your eyes and when you open them again, there is a <reading>shee</reading>p (し) in front of you. A sheep?! You yelp, blink again, and it's back to being a child. Are… are all children really just sheep?

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>small</radical> radical and the <kanji>small</kanji> kanji are exactly the same.
readingMnemonic: This <kanji>small</kanji> kanji would be the perfect gift to give to the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう). The Shougun loves little things, and the <kanji>little</kanji> kanji represents all of that.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the radical that look like this are exactly the same. They are both <kanji>street</kanji>.
readingMnemonic: So you're walking down this <kanji>street</kanji>, and off in the distance you see the silhouette of somebody. A lady. Big, black curly hair and red eyes. It is the street-woman, Mrs. <reading>Chou</reading> (ちょう).

meaningMnemonic: What's something with <radical>nine</radical> sides? Just add a <radical>drop</radical> and you'll have something that's almost a <kanji>circle</kanji>.
readingMnemonic: Do you know about the adorable cat Maru? If not, you should check it out. May seem like a distraction, but if you come back knowing the reading for this kanji, then it's totally worthwhile. When you think about a circle, think about Maru all rolled up in a little circle, being super adorable. Don't tell me it doesn't melt your heart just thinking about it. Alternatively, you could also look at this kanji and think of Maru inside of a box, with his little tail sticking out of it. Whatever works best for you, just get yourself to "Maru."

meaningMnemonic: On the <radical>ground</radical> you put <radical>barb</radical>s at the bottom of a <radical>slide</radical>. This is your special <kanji>talent</kanji>. When you go down the slide, you fly right past the barbs like some kind of daredevil. Any normal kid would land on the barbs and get hurt, but you have this talent.
readingMnemonic: But then people start to say that no human could have so much <kanji>talent</kanji>, that you must be a <reading>cy</reading>borg (さい). Are you really a talented cyborg?

meaningMnemonic: The radical <reading>middle</reading> and the kanji <kanji>middle</kanji> are both the same. So if you know one, you know the other.
readingMnemonic:
To remember the reading for this kanji, we use the word Chewbacca to pull up ちゅう in our memory.

If you remember back to the radical 中, a stick was stuck in the middle of the mouth of something. Let's say it was your mouth, and when you look up to see who did it, there stands <reading>Chew</reading>bacca. He's even doing his Chewbacca yell. And it isn't a stick in your mouth, it's an arrow from his bowcaster. It just so happens Chewbacca's bowcaster looks just like this kanji too. Go figure.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>five</radical> radical and the <kanji>five</kanji> kanji are the same.
readingMnemonic: <kanji>Five</kanji> is the number of buildings <reading>Go</reading>dzilla (ご) will knock down. Oh, look! There he goes now.

meaningMnemonic: Just imagine a beast with a <radical>lid</radical> for a head and <radical>fins</radical> for feet, running around looking as insane as possible (really imagine it!). When it runs up to you, it shows you the top of its lid-head. On it is written the numbers 666. Why? Because it's a spawn of the devil, and the number <kanji>six</kanji> is the devil's number.
readingMnemonic:
Whenever we need to remember the reading ろく we just use the word <reading>rock</reading>. 

So, think about the devil and the devil's number. What is often associated with those things? Why, <reading>rock</reading> (ろく) and roll is. Imagine the lid-head fin-legged beast suddenly blasting out rock music in the middle of his destructive mayhem.

meaningMnemonic: Here we have a <radical>head</radical> with a <radical>lid</radical> in it, near the top. Imagine the top of someone's head being closed by a lid. Open that lid up and peer inside... it's full of <kanji>yen</kanji> coins, the currency of Japan. Jackpot! And all those yen coins have a nice <kanji>round</kanji> shape, naturally.
readingMnemonic: What does the word <kanji>yen</kanji> sound like? It sounds like <reading>えん</reading>. Why? because back in the day, Japanese used to have a "ye" sound, but now it doesn't. Instead, it's an "e" sound, which is why yen = えん!

meaningMnemonic: The radical <radical>heaven</radical> is the same as the kanji <kanji>heaven</kanji>, making this an easy kanji to memorize.
readingMnemonic: <kanji>Heaven</kanji>... nowadays, people aren't all that good. Things have gotten bad. That's why the acceptance rate to get into heaven is only <reading>ten</reading> (てん) percent!

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>hand</radical> radical and the <kanji>hand</kanji> kanji are identical in looks and meaning! That should make things easy!
readingMnemonic: To remember the reading て we will use the word "television." You have great hands. Go ahead and look at them. OH MY GOD. You have little <reading>te</reading>levisions (て) on your hands. They are playing the dumbest show you can think of, too, both hands at the same time.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the radical are the same, so remembering the meaning of this kanji is as simple as making sure you know the radical for <radical>writing</radical> first! If you know the radical, you know this kanji means <kanji>writing</kanji> as well.
readingMnemonic:
This kanji has more than one important reading you need to learn! But don't worry, here's a mnemonic to help you:

You always put <kanji>writing</kanji> on your <reading>bun</reading>s (ぶん) to keep them from getting eaten by the bun <reading>mon</reading>ster (もん). What kind of writing? Usually it's just an X to show the monster these buns are off-limits. (In fact, it kind of looks like the X part in 文. Hmm…)

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>sun</radical> radical and <kanji>sun</kanji> kanji are the same, huzzah! Also, if you want to remember <kanji>day</kanji> as well, you can just think about how a day is the time it takes for the sun to cross the sky.
readingMnemonic:
There are so many on'yomi for this kanji, but since this kanji is so important and common, you'll end up learning them all eventually whether you want to or not. For now, you're only going to have to learn one of the readings, and that one is にち. 

The <kanji>sun</kanji> is so hot. It focused its rays to take out one man... and that man is Friedrich <reading>Nietzsche</reading> (にち), famous philosopher. He philosophized one too many things about the sun, so the sun decided it was time he paid.

meaningMnemonic: The <kanji>moon</kanji> kanji is made up of the <radical>moon</radical> radical. This kanji is also used to mean <kanji>month</kanji>.
readingMnemonic: The <kanji>moon</kanji> really <reading>gets</reading> (げつ) to your <reading>guts</reading> (がつ). Staring at the moon for too long will give you terrible indigestion, so be careful!

meaningMnemonic: This is another kanji that is the same as the radical. Both <radical>tree</radical> and <kanji>tree</kanji> are the same in looks and meaning.
readingMnemonic: You’re walking through the trees, looking at them. They look ominous. Suddenly, you trip and fall. The trees start to <reading>mock</reading> (もく) you. Even the mocking birds mock you. It’s terrible. It’s painful. You’re being mocked as you pull yourself up off the ground.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>water</radical> radical and the <kanji>water</kanji> kanji are the same.
readingMnemonic: Imagine a lake (i.e. a whole bunch of <kanji>water</kanji>). When you think of water, you should try to think of this beautiful lake. It’s sunny, the trees smell nice (imagine a tree smell if you can – the more senses you use the better!) and on that tree there is an awesome tire <reading>swi</reading>ng (すい). You get onto the tire swing and you are swinging back and forth. The water is right in front of you, and you are able to use the swing to throw yourself into the water. It’s a lot colder than you expected, and you come back to the surface, gasping for breath. The water gets into your mouth and you taste it. How strange, it’s very, very <reading>swee</reading>t. Imagine the sweetness of the water and how weird it is (really really sweet, this water is).

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>fire</radical> radical and the <kanji>fire</kanji> kanji are the same as each other.
readingMnemonic:
Whenever we need to remember か, we use the word <reading>car</reading>.

For this kanji, when we think <kanji>fire</kanji>, we ought to think of something on fire. What's on fire? It's obviously your very own <reading>car</reading> (か)! Oh Goddd NOOO!

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>dog</radical> radical and <kanji>dog</kanji> kanji are exactly the same!
readingMnemonic: Every <kanji>dog</kanji> likes to <reading>ea</reading>t <reading>noo</reading>dles (いぬ). Like that movie where the dogs share a noodle and end up kissing, right? So when you see 犬, just imagine how those two eat noodles.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>king</radical> radical and the <kanji>king</kanji> kanji are the same!
readingMnemonic: When you see the <kanji>king</kanji> you need to say, "<reading>Oh</reading> (おう) King! Oh King, oh no, oh why are you looking at me like that? Oh King... you aren't going to chop off my head are you? OH NO!"

meaningMnemonic: There are two <radical>mountains</radical> in front of you... and probably a bunch more around you too. You are in a pass, and don't know how to get out. You are frantically looking for the <kanji>exit</kanji> before you freeze to death. The most important thing is to find the exit from this mountain range - your life depends on it.
readingMnemonic: As you're trying to <kanji>exit</kanji> this mountain range, you suddenly realize that someone is actually hunting you. What? Make sure to feel that sudden realization. It's crazy. Then, you find the exit and run towards it. Sadly, the hunter is waiting there, and he <reading>shoots you</reading> (しゅつ) through the leg, knocking you down.

meaningMnemonic:
<radical>Narwhal</radical> <radical>mouth</radical>s are on the <kanji>right</kanji> side of their head. In fact, some people didn't think they had mouths at all because they weren't looking from the right. Rookie mistake.

See how the "mouth" radical is to the right of the "narwhal" radical? That should help you remember that this means <kanji>right</kanji>.
readingMnemonic: How did we know to look from the <kanji>right</kanji>, though? Because the <reading>you</reading>ths (ゆう) figured it out. Youths think in ways that older people usually don't. That's why they can see the swirls on cereal, hear the music behind noise, and of course, look at things from the right side.

meaningMnemonic: Imagine contorting yourself in such a way that your <radical>mouth</radical> now has your <radical>legs</radical> inside it. You've bent your body in all the wrong ways to achieve this. In fact, if you look at yourself, you look like a square now... You're just a person with <kanji>four</kanji> sides.
readingMnemonic: Now that you have <kanji>four</kanji> sides, you look like a four-sided animal: the <reading>shee</reading>p (し). Look at yourself in the mirror and see an adorable four-sided sheep looking back at you. Imagine yourself with perfectly square wool. (You can only see four sides because mirrors aren't 3D. So you're a flat, cartoonish sheep.)

meaningMnemonic: <radical>Narwhal</radical>s do <radical>construction</radical> with their <kanji>left</kanji> hands (or flippers, I guess). That's because all narwhals are left-handed!
readingMnemonic: In their <kanji>left</kanji> hands, the narwhals all hold <reading>saw</reading>s (さ). That's because the ice for their homes is already there, so they just need to saw away at them to make the shapes they want. Narwhal construction is actually very simple.

meaningMnemonic:
The kanji and the radical are the same, so remembering the meaning of this kanji is as simple as making sure you know the radical for <radical>book</radical> first! If you know the radical, you know this kanji means <kanji>book</kanji> as well.

Take note that the second, third, and fourth meanings of this kanji are <kanji>origin</kanji>, <kanji>real</kanji>, and <kanji>main</kanji>. You can figure this out by thinking about how books mainly have their origins in real life.
readingMnemonic: <kanji>Book</kanji>s are one of the best ways to <reading>hone</reading> (ほん) your intellect, you know?

meaningMnemonic: There is a line on the <radical>ground</radical> so you <radical>stop</radical>, making a giant sign pop up that says <kanji>CORRECT</kanji>! You obeyed the rules of the road and did the correct thing. Good on you.
readingMnemonic: You did the <kanji>correct</kanji> thing, so here's your reward: a <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい) from the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう) himself!

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>king</radical> has a big <radical>drop</radical> of liquid in his hand... but then he bounces it! The drop was really a <kanji>ball</kanji>.
readingMnemonic: When he's done playing with it, the king gives his <kanji>ball</kanji> to <reading>Tama</reading>ra (たま), the resident ball girl, for safekeeping.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>rice paddy</radical> radical and the <kanji>rice paddy</kanji> kanji are the same, making this one super easy to remember, at least meaning-wise.
readingMnemonic:
To remember the reading た, we use the word "taco."

You're in the rice field... but when you lean down to examine the rice more closely, you see that it's not rice that's growing, it's a bunch of little <reading>ta</reading>cos (た), blossoming out of the ground.

meaningMnemonic: The radical <radical>white</radical> and the kanji <kanji>white</kanji> are the same.
readingMnemonic: The <kanji>white</kanji> team was the first to be able to <reading>hack</reading> (はく) the <kanji>white</kanji> iPhone.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>eye</radical> radical and <kanji>eye</kanji> kanji are the same as each other, so now you know them both.
readingMnemonic: Your <kanji>eyes</kanji> are important to you, right? Sadly, one of your eyes is missing right now, because a sheep grabbed it and pulled it out, and is now running away from you, yelling his sheep call.... "<reading>Mehhhhhh</reading> (め)!"

meaningMnemonic: The radical <radical>stand</radical> and the kanji <kanji>stand</kanji> are the same thing, so now you know them both.
readingMnemonic: Think of someone who has to <kanji>stand</kanji> up straight for you. These people are pros in hospitality. These are the doormen at the <reading>Ritz</reading> (りつ).

meaningMnemonic:
This is a strange kanji because it doesn't really mean anything at all. In fact, what it does is it repeats the kanji that comes before it. For example: 人々 is actually 人人. This kanji shows up whenever there's a repeating kanji, because it's often easier to write than writing a more complicated kanji twice. That's why this kanji is the <kanji>repeater</kanji>. It repeats what comes before it.

To remember that this kanji is the repeater, though, think of a <radical>prison</radical> with <radical>drop</radical>s of water dripping over and over in it. Drip drop drip drop. It does that over and over again, driving the prisoners nuts. But it never stops. It just repeats over and over.
readingMnemonic:
This symbol doesn't really have a name, but it's sometimes called the のま because it looks like the katakana characters ノ and マ put together. If you know katakana then this will be easy to read. If you don't, think of it this way:

Your mom is the <kanji>repeater</kanji>. She is trying to force feed you over and over again until you explode (literally). You keep yelling "<reading>No ma</reading> (のま)!" but she doesn't stop. She's on a repeating track, never stopping, repeating forever.

meaningMnemonic:
This is the <radical>cow</radical> radical. It kind of looks like the long face of a cow, right? You can think of that top horizontal line as its horns. Seeing it yet? It's got a little tuft of hair on its head, and some kind of accessory on the horn to your left, so this is a very stylish cow.

(Did you know both male and female cows can have horns? I didn't either, but it's true. So please don't write in telling us we can't call this thing a cow — we don't want any beef.)

This is a little triangle that's almost completely closed off. When you want to be alone, you hide in a place where you can be in <radical>private</radical>. That's why this is the private radical. You're closing yourself off to the rest of the world, with just a little peephole.

At first this looks like a cross in the ground, but it's actually something growing in the <radical>dirt</radical>. It's a little sprout, already rocking three leaves. And it's all thanks to that dirt! When you see this radical, think about how amazing it is that things just, like, grow in the dirt.

This is the <radical>sword</radical> radical. The ノ part is the curvy sword, while the rest is a samurai bowing deeply, like 90 degrees, as he holds that sword — probably before a sword fight. By the way, don't confuse this with the power radical (力). See the difference? In the sword radical (刀), the ノ doesn't pierce through the bowing samurai. (Lucky for him.)

This radical looks like a <radical>narwhal</radical>. See the tail at the bottom, and the majestic narwhal horn poking out at the top? It also looks like the katakana character ナ (na), so you can remember it that way too. Na-na-na-narwhal! Just be careful not to confuse this with the treasure radical (メ). The sideways line for that one is at more of a downward angle, like an "X." But the sideways line for narwhal is horizontal (ナ).
Imagine viewing this one from above — looks like a couple of streets, right? Maybe a three-way intersection at the top there? Well, that's why this is the radical for <radical>street</radical>.
This radical already looks like a pair of <radical>legs</radical>. There's even feet on these legs. That's how you know these are legs and not fins (or something else).

This is the outline of a <radical>head</radical>. A really square head, admittedly, but try to imagine it facing to the right. The left side is the back of the head, and the barb-like part on the right shows the curve of the chin. See the head?
This radical is <radical>treasure</radical> because "X marks the spot," as they say. Doesn't it look like the X on a pirate treasure map? You want to go find that treasure now, right?!

You have a slide supported by a big cross, which makes it so strong it can hold a lot of people at once. How many? A <radical>THOUSAND</radical>! Imagine a thousand people going down this slide. You can hear a voice trying to count them as they go: "ONE! TWO! THREE HUNDRED! FIVE HUNDRED!" and so on. Finally the last person slides down and the voice calls out: "A THOUSAND!" You think it's over, but just as that group finishes, in come another thousand…

This is the top half of a moon peeking out as it begins to rise. When does that happen? In the <radical>evening</radical>! Don't confuse this with the moon radical 月. 夕 is for the evening, when the moon's still low enough to be partly covered (by a mountain, for example).
This is the radical for <radical>child</radical>. Aww, see how they're wearing a cap and have their little arms stretched out? You can almost hear them saying "pick me up!" A very child-like child.

You see two fins around a barb, but when you look closer, you realize those fins are actually two tiny fish. Think how small these fins (and fish) would have to be to look so much smaller than an already small barb. Talk about small fry… Yeah, that's why this is the radical for <radical>small</radical>.

This radical looks like a fancy composite <radical>bow</radical> people would use to shoot at targets and definitely not any normal living animals. See how the middle part is being pulled back? Imagine holding this bow and firing it at a target shaped like a giant alligator-crab monster.
See the <radical>flag</radical> in this radical? There's a pole with a flag on the end of it, blowing in the wind. Basically, this radical looks just like a flag.
These three streaks look like the flowing locks of someone's <radical>hair</radical>. Pretty easy to see and remember that this is the hair radical, right?

If you use your imagination, this radical actually looks like the number "5." You just have to remove the vertical line in the bottom left, plus a few extra ends that stick out here and there. Once you do that, you have an easy way to remember that this radical means <radical>five</radical>.

Remember the guy from the big radical? This is him again, but this time he's so big his head is touching <radical>heaven</radical>. He's looking up and has his arms stretched open toward heaven, and his big hair is poking into those heavenly clouds.

This is the <radical>hand</radical> radical. If you look at the palm of your right hand, the lines in it probably kinda look like the lines in 手. Sure it's not a perfect match, and everyone's got different hands, but basically this radical looks like the lines in your hand. 

You could also think of the horizontal lines in 手 as fingers. That would make for a pretty alien-looking hand, but still a hand… of sorts…

This looks like a stool with a lid on it, but it's actually a desk for <radical>writing</radical>. Do you see it? You can even see the pen sticking up in the middle. Think of all the beautiful writing that's been produced here. Letters, novels, essays — any kind of writing you can think of.

This is the <radical>moon</radical> radical. See how it looks like a crescent moon with a couple of little clouds floating by? Try not to mix this up with the evening radical 夕 — you can see the whole crescent moon this time, so obviously this should be the radical for moon!

There's a stick and a giant toe stuck in the ground. Imagine walking along and seeing these things in your path. What would you do? Most likely come to a complete <radical>stop</radical>. You know, because of the toe. I guess a normal stop sign wasn't good enough, so they also put up this big toe to really get people to stop.

This is the radical for <radical>water</radical>. It's the water radical because it kind of looks like water coming out of a faucet and splashing everywhere. See how it flows off in different directions too? Very water-like! As water-like as a radical can be, anyway…

This is the radical for <radical>fire</radical>. It's got three little flames on top and even a couple logs underneath.

But if it helps you can also focus on the person radical here. See it? It's got things coming off of it... What are those thi— OH MY GOD IT'S FIRE!!! Help, help! Put the fire out! Wait, it's fine… It's just the fire radical.

Remember the big guy who helped you with that big radical? Now you've got his big <radical>DOG</radical>! You know it's a dog because he's lying down, legs all spread out, with his favorite bone right beside his head.

This is the <radical>king</radical> radical. Picture this king sitting on his solid, squarish throne. The top stroke is the high back of the throne. The cross in the middle is his body, with his arms spread out on the arm rests. The bottom stroke is the ground. See him sitting there now, looking all dignified. Talk about a kingly king.

This is a stick going through the <radical>middle</radical> of a mouth. What kind of mouth? Mouth of a cylinder, mouth of a box, a human mouth — whatever kind you want to imagine, as long as the stick is going through the middle of it, since this is the radical for "middle."

What's something useful you can get out of a tree? A <radical>book</radical>! Just cut that book right out of the tree trunk. If you think about it, every tree contains unlimited book potential. It's just a shame they have to be cut down to make the books…

In Japan rice paddies are made up of square fields with irrigation lines going between them. See the four rice paddies with water flowing between and around them? That's why this radical is <radical>rice paddy</radical>.

Add a drop of the sun to something and it will turn it <radical>white</radical>. The sun is really strong, so even a single drop will burn anything white. Why do you think so many beaches are white? Even the drops of sunblock you use to stop the sun's rays is white. So when you think of sun drops, think white.

See the iris in the middle of the eyeball? Remember that there are no round things in kanji, so square things take their place. That makes this a pretty realistic, square <radical>eye</radical>. You just have to squint with your real eyes a bit.

This looks like someone in a standing position with legs planted firmly on the ground, spreading their arms out in a T-pose to maintain balance. So that makes this the radical for <radical>stand</radical>.
There's a leaf on top and a stem going down out of it, making this the <radical>leaf</radical> radical. It's a pretty wide and flat leaf, so if it helps you can imagine it as a lily pad leaf.

From <reading>Ohio</reading> (おはよう), you shout <vocabulary>good morning</vocabulary>. Why Ohio? Because that's where you woke up this morning. Imagine yourself waking up in Ohio, and wishing everyone "good morning" at the top of your lungs.

おはよう is a casual way to say "good morning." It can also be written in kanji, but hiragana is much more common.

ホテル comes from the English word <vocabulary>hotel</vocabulary> and the meaning is exactly the same!

The only thing to watch out for is that the "l" on the end becomes ル, because that's the closest sound in Japanese.

This word means <vocabulary>to climb</vocabulary>. You can tell this word is a verb because it ends with an う sound (る, in this case). Its meaning is similar to the meaning of the 上 kanji, which is "above." So this word is all about moving "above" something (in other words, climbing!)
readingMnemonic:
上る consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, so it will use the kun'yomi reading of the kanji. For 上, there are multiple kun'yomi readings, and you didn't learn this one yet, so here's a mnemonic to help you: 

You want <vocabulary>to climb</vocabulary> the mountain, but a gruff old man grabs your shoulder and says "<reading>No bo</reading>y, you ain't climbing that thing, not today." If you're not a boy, think about how weird it is that he called you a boy. If you are a grown man, think how you wish he called you "dude" or something, not "boy." If you're a boy, well... think about how appropriate it is that he said "no boy."

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well!
readingMnemonic: Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it uses the kun'yomi reading. I'm hoping you already know this word - that will make everything much easier. It's <reading>かたな</reading> (you know what a katana is, right?).

meaningMnemonic:
The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.

土 generally refers to <vocabulary>dirt</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>soil</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>earth</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it uses the kun'yomi reading. Here's a mnemonic to help you to remember it: 

You look through the soil. You're trying to find something. As you dig and sift, you come across not one but <reading>two chee</reading>ses (つち). Pull them out of the ground and put them in your mouth. How do the two cheeses taste and compare?

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: Normally a single kanji making up a word like this means it will use the kun'yomi readings. Numbers are generally the exception, though, which means this word uses the on'yomi reading. If you learned the kanji, then you should already know this!

meaningMnemonic: I spent all <kanji>evening</kanji> in <reading>be</reading>(べ)d  <vocabulary>last night</vocabulary>. It was a really chill <vocabulary>evening</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: When hiragana is attached to a kanji, you can usually assume that that kanji is going to be read using the kun'yomi reading, which is exactly what's going on with this word as well. By learning this word's kanji you've also learned the reading for this word.

meaningMnemonic:
A <kanji>woman</kanji> <kanji>child</kanji> is a <vocabulary>girl</vocabulary>.

This word has two kanji with hiragana stuck in the middle. That hiragana is the particle の. This time, it just means the noun before の is modifying the noun after it.

女の子 is a casual word for young girls, like kids or teenagers. Sometimes it can also refer to adult women, but only to playfully or affectionately highlight their youthful or girlish qualities.
readingMnemonic:
Since there's hiragana in here, you can bet that these kanji will use the kun'yomi readings. You already know the kun'yomi reading for 女. You might even remember the one for 子, but here's a mnemonic just in case:

When you need to remember the kun'yomi reading for 子, just think about how a child stole your <reading>co</reading>at (こ). You gave it to her because she looked cold, but then she ran off down the street and left you shouting, "Coat! Coat! That 子 has my coat!"

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it uses the kun'yomi reading. When learning the kanji, you didn't learn that reading, so here's a mnemonic to help you with this word:

You saw a <vocabulary>child</vocabulary> and gave her your <reading>co</reading>at (こ). The kid looked all cold, so you gave her your coat. Children never want to wear proper warm clothing when they should, then they end up shivering their butts off and needing to wear a parent's oversized coat. Fortunately you were there to give this 子 your coat.

meaningMnemonic:
A <kanji>woman</kanji> <kanji>child</kanji> is just a <vocabulary>girl</vocabulary>!

女子 is a relatively formal word commonly used in contexts where classification or categorization by gender is used, such as in sports or school settings.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: This kanji has hiragana attached and ends with an い sound. That means it's probably an adjective (and it is!). So, if you take the kanji 小 and its meaning (small), you can figure out the adjective version of it. What is the adjective version of "small?" Actually, it's the same thing: <vocabulary>small</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn that reading with this kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you to remember the ちい portion:

When you think of the word <vocabulary>small</vocabulary>, try to think of this <reading>chee</reading>se <reading>ea</reading>tery (ちい). You go inside, and all of their cheeses are so small. They hand you a magnifying glass to see all the cheeses. You look at them and exclaim: "Why are these cheeses so small???"

meaningMnemonic: This vocab word and the kanji that makes it have the same meaning: <vocabulary>Circle</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: The reading you learned for this kanji is the kun'yomi one. Since this vocab word is made up of a single kanji, it uses the kun'yomi reading. Guess what? That means you already know it!

meaningMnemonic: With an い on the end, you can guess that this word is an い-adjective. What is the adjective version of the kanji <kanji>丸</kanji>? "Circle" becomes <vocabulary>circular</vocabulary>, <vocabulary>spherical</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>round</vocabulary>. This, compared to another similar word (円), is more general purpose and can be used with 3D spherical objects as well.
readingMnemonic: This word uses the reading you learned for the kanji <kanji>丸</kanji>, though you'll need to tack the い onto the end too.

meaningMnemonic:
Although this word consists of one kanji that is all alone, it doesn't share its meaning with the parent kanji. But the meanings are similar in a way. This word means <vocabulary>years old</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>age</vocabulary>. With more years comes more <kanji>talent</kanji>, right? That's why this word means "years old" and not "talent," like the parent kanji.

You can stick this word onto numbers to say how old someone is!

Special note for all of you advanced learners out there. When discussing age, 〜歳 is the original and correct kanji to use. Because 〜才 is easier to write and has the same reading, it became mainstream. You'll see it used in writing today, especially in elementary schools. In formal writing though, you'll always want to use 〜歳, which you'll learn later on.
readingMnemonic: The reading is the same as the one you learned for the kanji, meaning you should already know it! Don't try to type the 〜 part though.

meaningMnemonic: When the <kanji>middle</kanji> kanji is used as a word, it means <vocabulary>inside</vocabulary>. When you're in the middle of something, it means you're <vocabulary>in</vocabulary> that thing!
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it uses the kun'yomi reading. When learning the kanji, you didn't learn that reading, so here's a mnemonic to help you with this word:

There's a sound coming from <kanji>inside</kanji> the wall. It sounds like a knock, knock, knock noise. You break down the wall, and there's some creepy dude standing inside. He is the <reading>knocker</reading> (なか).

meaningMnemonic:
The 々 symbol just repeats the kanji that comes before it. In other words, 中々 is the same as 中中. Easy! So with 中々, you have <kanji>middle</kanji> x2. When something is more middle than middle itself, it's <vocabulary>rather</vocabulary>, <vocabulary>fairly</vocabulary>, or <vocabulary>quite</vocabulary> something. Why? I guess because those are all good middle-level intensifiers.

中々 can be used in positive or negative ways. In a positive way, it usually means something is better than expected, like 中々おいしい (quite delicious). But it's really versatile and could also mean "a little," "very," or "completely" depending on the situation. It might even sound condescending, so be careful how you use it! In a negative sense, you can use it when something is more difficult than expected, like in 中々できない (can't be done easily).
readingMnemonic:
The reading for this word is the kun'yomi reading, since it's like putting two single kanji together rather than a true jukugo word. To learn the kun'yomi reading, take a look at this mnemonic: 

From the middle of the middle comes a sound. It is a knocking sound. Knock. Knock. Knock. You open up the walls around this thing. Then you open up the second set of walls (middle of the middle).
