Inside is an old man, who identifies himself as "the knocker." You go inside of him and find another knocker as well. That's why it's a <reading>knocker knocker</reading> (なかなか).

meaningMnemonic: The moon kanji also represents a month, so when you have five of these months, what month of the year is it? That would be <vocabulary>May</vocabulary>, the greatest month of the year (because it has towel day in it!).
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. 月 has two on'yomi readings, but since this is a specific month name, it's going to use the がつ reading. Here's a mnemonic to help you remember that:

<vocabulary>May</vocabulary> is when you and your buddies get together to eat <reading>Go</reading>dzilla <reading>guts</reading> (ごがつ). The weather's always great in May, so it's the perfect time to go hunting for Godzilla and have a Godzilla-guts barbecue outside with your friends.

meaningMnemonic: <vocabulary>Day five</vocabulary> or the <vocabulary>fifth day</vocabulary>. No matter how you look at this word, it makes a lot of sense.
readingMnemonic:
Words that count days use an exceptional reading. Even though this is a jukugo word, it uses kun'yomi readings for both the kanji. Think about this word this way:

On the <vocabulary>fifth day</vocabulary> I have to <reading>eat</reading> (いつ) five <reading>ca</reading>rs (か). Now imagine yourself eating five cars. How did you manage to get past the first one? I'm impressed.

meaningMnemonic: What happens when you have ten five times? You get <vocabulary>fifty</vocabulary>!
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. That's the case for this word too, which means you should already know how to read this word.

meaningMnemonic: From previous number + つ words, you can probably guess that this word means <vocabulary>five things</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
The reading of this word is the kun'yomi reading of the kanji <kanji>五</kanji>, meaning you didn't learn it when you learned the parent kanji reading. To remember this word, think of it this way:

You have <vocabulary>five things</vocabulary>. What do you have to do with them? You need to <reading>eat</reading> (いつ) them. Just imagine having five delicious things to eat, sticking each on one finger of your hand, and then eating them one after another. And make sure to count down from five while doing it!

meaningMnemonic: This vocab is made from a single kanji, which means it shares the same meaning as the parent kanji: <vocabulary>Five</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: Since numbers are an exception, this word is read using the on'yomi reading even though it's made from a single kanji all alone. Luckily for you, you learned the reading for this word when you learned the parent kanji.

meaningMnemonic: Any number with a つ attached to it means you are counting that number of things. In this case, it's <vocabulary>six things</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can guess that it uses the kun'yomi reading. Here's a mnemonic to help you do that:

What are the <vocabulary>six things</vocabulary> that you need to count? This time you are counting six cows. The reason it's six is because you wanted exactly six <reading>moo</reading>s (むっ). No more, no less. You're very particular about your moos.

meaningMnemonic: Since the kanji 月 means both "moon" and "month," what is the sixth month of the year? January, February, March, April, May, <vocabulary>June</vocabulary>!
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. 月 has two on'yomi readings, but since this is a specific month name, it's going to use the がつ reading. Here's a mnemonic to help you remember that:

<vocabulary>June</vocabulary> always gives you <reading>rock</reading> <reading>guts</reading> (ろくがつ). June is the start of the rainy season in Japan, so your guts turn rock solid to avoid being dissolved by all the precipitation. Rock guts in June is a cool survival mechanism, but it makes for terrible indigestion.

meaningMnemonic: It's the kanji for six and day. Put them together and you have the <vocabulary>sixth day</vocabulary> (or <vocabulary>day six</vocabulary>).
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. This is an exception, though. When you're counting days (at least one through ten), the kun'yomi readings are used. So let's think about that sixth day - what does it entail?

On the <vocabulary>sixth day</vocabulary> of your roadtrip, you've finally arrived at your destination. You get out of your car and tell it, "<reading>Muy</reading> (むい) bien <reading>car</reading> (か), muy bien!".

meaningMnemonic: You have ten and six (not to be confused with six tens!). When you have ten and six, you have a total of <vocabulary>sixteen</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well!

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading. Numbers, as you know, are the exception to this rule. They usually use the on'yomi reading, and the on'yomi reading is the one you learned along with this kanji, meaning you should be good to go!

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: The reading is the same as the one you learned with the kanji. Be sure to omit the 〜 in your answer as it is only a placeholder for a number.

meaningMnemonic: A thousand of these yen things makes this word <vocabulary>one thousand yen</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: Since you know the on'yomi readings of these two kanji, you should know the reading for this vocab word as well!

meaningMnemonic:
The kanji and the word are… almost the same. The kanji means <kanji>heaven</kanji>, but this word is more about the <vocabulary>heavens</vocabulary>. You know, the great heavens that stretch out high above us?

天 can sometimes refer to religious kinds of heaven (that place we all want to end up someday), but more often it's about the sky and what lies beyond it, so you might also call it the <vocabulary>firmament</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it would normally use the kun'yomi reading. The kanji that makes this word only has one important reading, though, and that happens to be the on'yomi. Since you learned the on'yomi reading for this kanji, you've already learned the reading for this word.

meaningMnemonic: If <kanji>heaven</kanji> has given you <kanji>talent</kanji>, you must be a <vocabulary>genius</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. As long as you know the readings for these kanji, you should be able to read this word just fine.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji means <kanji>hand</kanji>, and the word means <vocabulary>hand</vocabulary> too! It can also be used to refer to your whole <vocabulary>arm</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it uses the kun'yomi reading. Since this is a body part, the kun'yomi reading usually takes priority. That means you learned the kun'yomi reading already when you learned the kanji.

meaningMnemonic: Think of someone holding out their hand at shoulder level. Anything above that equals "skillful." Everything <kanji>below</kanji> that <kanji>hand</kanji> equals <vocabulary>unskillful</vocabulary>. That's why "below hand" means <vocabulary>unskilled</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>bad at</vocabulary>!
readingMnemonic:
Sometimes kanji have exceptional readings that are neither on'yomi nor kun'yomi, and this is one of them. So here's a mnemonic to help you remember this reading:

If you're <vocabulary>unskillful</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>bad at</vocabulary> something and you complain about it, then you're just a <reading>hater</reading> (へた). Don't be a hater; get better.

meaningMnemonic: Think of someone holding out their hand. This is average. Things rated below the hand are things you're bad at. Things rated <kanji>above</kanji> the <kanji>hand</kanji> are things you're <vocabulary>good at</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
Both of these kanji use on'yomi readings, though 手 uses an on'yomi reading you didn't learn with the kanji. It's a rare reading, so it wasn't included (and we just have to deal with exceptions like this as we go along). To remember the reading for this word, here's a mnemonic:

What are you good at? You're really <vocabulary>good at</vocabulary> taking <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう) to the <reading>zoo</reading> (ず). No one else can take Joe to the zoo—he gets mad and throws them into the animal enclosures. But you? Joe trusts you.

meaningMnemonic:
The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.

By itself, 文 can also just mean <vocabulary>sentence</vocabulary>, especially in grammatical contexts.
readingMnemonic: This word uses one of the readings you learned for the kanji: ぶん. You can remember this by thinking how whenever you're working on your writing, eating a <reading>bun</reading> (ぶん) helps you write better sentences.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well. Take note that this word means both "sun" and "day," though. Why? because the sun takes a day to go around and come back!
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it uses the kun'yomi reading. Here's a mnemonic to help you to remember it: 

What do you feel from the sun? You feel the <reading>hea</reading>t (ひ).

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading. When learning the kanji, you didn't learn that reading, so here's a mnemonic to help you with this word:

Look up at that big, bright moon. You have to open it up and take what's inside. To do that, you are required to have <reading>two key</reading>s (つき), and they have to be turned at the same time. Imagine you and a friend both turning your keys on opposite sides of the moon to open up the center.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji for "moon" and "month" are the same, probably because a moon cycle takes approximately a month to occur. So, what is the tenth moon cycle of the year? That would be the awesome month <vocabulary>October</vocabulary>!
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. 月 has two on'yomi readings, but since this is a specific month name, it's going to use the がつ reading. Here's a mnemonic to help you remember that:

<vocabulary>October</vocabulary> gives you <reading>jew</reading>el <reading>guts</reading> (じゅうがつ). In October, your guts magically produce jewels, which you collect (don't ask how) to make some pretty awesome Halloween costumes.

meaningMnemonic: Since the kanji 月 mean both moon and month (because a moon cycle is about a month, presumably), what is the number one month? In English we have an actual name for it, and that name is <vocabulary>January</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. 月 has two on'yomi readings, but it always uses the がつ reading in the names of specific months, so here's a mnemonic to help you remember that:

<vocabulary>January</vocabulary> always gives you <reading>itchy</reading> <reading>guts</reading> (いちがつ). It's a weird affliction that comes around every January and makes your guts incredibly itchy. Probably due to all that overindulgence over the new year.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji 月 means both month and moon (because moon cycles take about a month, presumably). So, what is the number two month? It's not January... it's not March... it's <vocabulary>February</vocabulary>!
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. 月 has two on'yomi readings, but since this is a specific month name, it's going to use the がつ reading. Here's a mnemonic to help you remember that:

<vocabulary>February</vocabulary> is the month when somebody gives you a <reading>knee</reading> in the <reading>guts</reading> (にがつ). A complete stranger comes around each February just to knee you right in the guts. Kind of a jerk move, but hey — everyone's got their own way of dealing with the wintertime blues, right?

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading. When learning the kanji, you didn't learn that reading, so here's a mnemonic to help you with this word:

You walk up to the tree. You have to get inside, because it takes you someplace secret. So, you take out your <reading>key</reading> (き) and put it in the slot, opening the door in the tree so you can slip inside.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading. When learning the kanji, you didn't learn that reading, so here's a mnemonic to help you with this word: 

Be sure you always carry water when visiting the <reading>me zoo</reading> (みず). That's a zoo with rows and rows of cages of... me. And I'm thirsty!

meaningMnemonic: This vocab is made from a single kanji, which means it shares the same meaning as the parent kanji: <vocabulary>fire</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it uses the kun'yomi reading. When learning the kanji, you didn't learn that reading, so here's a mnemonic to help you with this word:

What comes off of <vocabulary>fire</vocabulary>? Lots and lots of <reading>hea</reading>t (ひ). Feel it coming off the fire, touching and warming your face.

meaningMnemonic: A fire mountain is probably what you think it is: A <vocabulary>volcano</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well. The 山 reading is ざん instead of さん because of rendaku (sequential voicing).

meaningMnemonic: This vocab made up of a single kanji shares its meaning with said single kanji. That meaning is <vocabulary>dog</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: Since this is a word made up of a single kanji and nothing else, you can assume that this word will use the kun'yomi reading. The reading you learned for this kanji happens to be the kun'yomi reading as well. So if you know the kanji you know the reading.

meaningMnemonic: A <kanji>child</kanji> <kanji>dog</kanji> is a <vocabulary>puppy</vocabulary>!
readingMnemonic: Even though this is a jukugo word, it doesn't use the on'yomi readings. Fortunately you learned the kun'yomi readings with the vocab words 子 (こ) and 犬 (いぬ). Just think of 子犬 as those two words mashed together to remember this one.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: The reading for this word is the same as the one you learned when learning the kanji.

meaningMnemonic:
The <kanji>king</kanji>'s <kanji>child</kanji> is the <vocabulary>prince</vocabulary>. Someday he'll be the 王, if he's lucky, but right now he's just the king's child, a.k.a. the prince!

If you prefer some grammatical details, you can try to remember that when a word consists of two kanji, sometimes the first kanji has a possessive relation to the second. In this case, 王 possesses 子, making it the king's child. Historically, male children had priority in royal succession, so I guess that's why this word means "prince."
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well. Also take note that the reading for 子 (し) is changed to じ.

That said, this reading can be easy to confuse with other similar words, so here's a mnemonic to help you out:

The <vocabulary>prince</vocabulary> is always saying "<reading>Ohh</reading>, <reading>Je</reading>sus (おうじ), please let me be king!" In fact, the prince probably says "Ohh, Jesus" to ask for help at least once a day.

meaningMnemonic: The woman king is the queen. You know this because the woman who comes before the king must be the queen. Imagine this woman in front, leading the king around. Only a queen could do that! For a more technical explanation, just remember that when a word consists of two kanji characters, the first kanji often modifies the second. In this case, 女 modifies 王, making it the woman king, a.k.a. the queen!
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well. That said, this reading can be easy to confuse with other similar words, so here's a mnemonic to help you out:

The <vocabulary>queen</vocabulary> is named <reading>Jo</reading>-Ann (じょ), or Jo for short, and she's always joking. Hey, wait a minute. Jo… king. 女… king. Jo… おう!! 女王! Get it? When you think of the queen, just remember how much she loves joking (女王).

meaningMnemonic: The king's woman (or girl) is the princess. You know she's the princess because she comes after the king — she's just his daughter and doesn't have as much power. Imagine the king with a young woman following after him. Must be the princess! If you prefer some grammatical details, you can try to remember that when a word consists of two kanji, sometimes the first kanji has a possessive relation to the second. In this case, 王 possesses 女, making it the king's girl (almost like "daddy's girl"). In other words, the princess!
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well. That said, this reading can be easy to confuse with other similar words, so here's a mnemonic to help you out:

The <vocabulary>princess</vocabulary> is named <reading>Jo</reading>-Ann Jr. (じょ), or Jo for short , and she loves making people say "Ohh, Jo!" (王女) by sneaking up and scaring them. Whenever you think of the princess now, imagine how many times people have screamed "Ohh, Jo!" at her.

meaningMnemonic: This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means "exit," so what is the verb form of that? It's <vocabulary>to exit</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn that reading with this kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you:

You want <vocabulary>to exit</vocabulary> the room you're in. Why? Because it has a <reading>de</reading>ad (で) body in it. Seriously. You should exit before someone tries to pin this dead body on you. Quick, leave now!

meaningMnemonic: The <kanji>mouth</kanji> you <kanji>exit</kanji> out of is just a doorway (I hope), but an easier way to describe this word or concept is to call it an <vocabulary>exit</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. 出口 is an exception to this rule, however, and uses its kun'yomi readings. There's no particular reason why this is, but oftentimes a "body part" word (like mouth) changes a word to use kun'yomi readings instead of on'yomi ones. You already know the kun'yomi reading for 口 (くち) from learning the vocab, but to remember the reading for 出 you can think the following:

If I exit out this mouth, I'll end up in the ocean (you were swallowed by a whale) and I will surely end up <reading>dea</reading>d (で).

Also keep in mind that because of rendaku, くち becomes ぐち.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it uses the kun'yomi reading. You haven't learned this reading yet, so here's a mnemonic to help you:

Look to the <vocabulary>right</vocabulary>. There are <reading>me</reading>at <reading>gee</reading>se (みぎ) over there. What are meat geese, you ask? They're geese made of meat, that sit to the right of you, trying to get you to do bad things. Like the devil/angel motif you see in cartoons. Well there's nothing on your left. And on your right? Just meat geese. Watching you. Waiting. Geese made of meat. Telling you not to do the right thing.

meaningMnemonic: This vocab word has the same meaning as its parent kanji (single alone kanji usually do that!), meaning it means <vocabulary>four</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
When a vocab is alone like this with no okurigana (hiragana attached to the kanji) it usually uses its kun'yomi reading. Numbers are an exception, though, and use the on'yomi reading. However, four and seven actually use both. We're going to go with the kun'yomi reading here, and since you haven't learned this reading yet, here's a mnemonic to help you:

You have to count to <vocabulary>four</vocabulary>. But you're tired. One... two... three... *<reading>YAWN</reading>* ... four. There, you did it. But *yawn* now you can't stop yawning. You're so, so sleepy. Four is too high a number to count without falling asleep.

By the way! Both the reading よん and し are pretty common, but because the on'yomi reading し sounds just like the word for "death," it isn't used as much. Generally when you're just counting (for example, "1, 2, 3, 4..."), you will use the reading し. But when you are counting things or people, you'll use よん. There are exceptions to this, but you'll learn them as they come up.

meaningMnemonic: 月 in Japanese means "moon" but it also means "month." Why? Probably because the moon takes about a month to get through its cycles. So, what's the fourth moon of the year? Here's a hint, it's the same as the fourth month of the year: <vocabulary>April</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. 月 has two on'yomi readings, but since this is a specific month name, it's going to use the がつ reading. Here's a mnemonic to help you remember that:

In <vocabulary>April</vocabulary>, you habitually eat <reading>shee</reading>p <reading>guts</reading> (しがつ). April is the time for picnics under the cherry blossoms in Japan, and there's nothing you like more than rolling out your picnic blanket and going to town on a big ol' bowl of sheep guts.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it uses the kun'yomi reading. You haven't learned this reading yet, so here's a mnemonic to help you:

Imagine hearing someone to your <vocabulary>left</vocabulary> saying, "<reading>Hi Da'ri</reading>!" (ひだり) which is supposed to be "Hi There," or "Hi Dearie." You turn left and there's no one there. You hear it again, "Hi Da'ri!" Keep turning to the left until you find the person saying hi to you.

meaningMnemonic: This vocab word is exactly the same as its parent kanji, including its meaning as well: <vocabulary>book</vocabulary>. It doesn't have those other meanings though, when it's just 本 alone, it's just a book.
readingMnemonic: The reading for this word is the same as the single kanji it comes from.

meaningMnemonic: Remember the secondary meaning of 本 being "origin"? That technically makes this <kanji>sun</kanji> <kanji>origin</kanji>. Does that sound familiar? Japan is known as the "land of the rising sun", which is why this means <vocabulary>Japan</vocabulary>!
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. The 本 is read just how you learned it, but the 日 reading is sort of an exception here, so here's a mnemonic to help you:

You've finally arrived in <vocabulary>Japan</vocabulary> and immediately fall down on your <reading>knee</reading>s (に). After two excruciatingly long flights, you've finally made it. You're here. This is Japan. Now get up off your knees, people are starting to stare.

meaningMnemonic: Ending with an い tells you that this word is an い-adjective. So since you know that the kanji <kanji>正</kanji> means "<kanji>correct</kanji>," what is the adjective version of that word? Well, it's also <vocabulary>correct</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
This word uses a reading you didn't learn from the kanji. You have to remember the ただ portion in order to read this word, so think about it this way:

After you get the answer <vocabulary>correct</vocabulary> a clown jumps out of nowhere yelling "<reading>Ta-da</reading> (ただ)!!!" Imagine it scaring the crap out of you, too.

meaningMnemonic: This word is made up of a kanji with some hiragana attached, ending in an う sound. That means this word is a verb. The kanji itself means "correct," so what is the verb form of that? It would be <vocabulary>to correct</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn that reading with this kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you: 

After you correct something for someone, you yell out <reading>ta-da</reading> (ただ) very excitedly. Go ahead and imagine yourself doing this a few times to help you to remember.

meaningMnemonic:
As a single kanji vocab on its own, you know this vocab word has the same meaning as its parent kanji: <vocabulary>ball</vocabulary>.

玉 can be used for round objects traditionally recognized in Japanese culture, like beads, marbles, pearls, snowballs, and pachinko balls. For balls used in sports, people typically use the katakana word ボール.
readingMnemonic: This word uses the same reading as the one you learned with the kanji: <reading>たま</reading>.

meaningMnemonic: ねぎ means "onion" in Japanese. But, there are different kinds of onions, right? This happens to be a "ball onion," which is just one of those round ones, like a Walla Walla Sweet <vocabulary>Onion</vocabulary>. Do you know?
readingMnemonic: Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can guess it will probably use the kun'yomi reading. Luckily, that's the reading you learned with the only kanji in this word, so you should be able to read it!

meaningMnemonic:
The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
Just keep in mind that compared to 田んぼ, which you learned earlier, this word is more formal and less colloquial.
readingMnemonic: Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading. When you learned the kanji that makes up this word, all you learned was the kun'yomi reading, so you should be good to go.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading. When learning the kanji, you didn't learn that reading, so here's a mnemonic to help you with this word:

<vocabulary>White</vocabulary> surrounds you. The <reading>sheer</reading> (しろ) amount of white is overwhelming.

meaningMnemonic: This word uses <kanji>white</kanji> and <kanji>person</kanji> ... put those together and you have a <vocabulary>white person</vocabulary>, aka a <vocabulary>caucasian</vocabulary> person.
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Since 人 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use:

Imagine a <vocabulary>white person</vocabulary> you know well wearing their favorite pair of <reading>hack</reading>ed up <reading>jean</reading>s (はくじん). This person likes to wear jeans with as many rips and holes in them as possible. Now imagine them wearing only their hacked up jeans. What a sight!

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: The reading is the same as the one you learned for the kanji, meaning you should already know it!

meaningMnemonic: What is an <kanji>eye</kanji> <kanji>ball</kanji>? Oh wait, that's easy. It's an <vocabulary>eyeball</vocabulary>!
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. <vocabulary>目玉</vocabulary>, however, is an exception. Perhaps this is because it includes a body part (the eye) in it, which often makes a word switch to kun'yomi readings. Either way, it doesn't matter too much. Both the readings you learned for the kanji in this word are the kun'yomi, meaning you should know how to read this one. Just make sure you watch out for the rendaku.

meaningMnemonic: This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means "stand" so what is the verb version of that? It's <vocabulary>to stand</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
You have to remember the た portion of this word to learn the reading of it. Think of it this way:

You have <vocabulary>to stand up</vocabulary> in order to wave goodbye to someone. When you stand up, you also say <reading>ta</reading>-ta (た)!

meaningMnemonic: <vocabulary>Second day</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>day two</vocabulary>. No matter how you look at this combination of kanji, the meaning of the word makes a lot of sense!
readingMnemonic:
Words that count days use an exceptional reading. Even though this is a jukugo word, it uses kun'yomi readings for both the kanji. Think about this word this way:

The circus is in town this weekend and you can't wait to get there. You want to show off your new <reading>foots car</reading> (ふつか) to all the circus clowns. But you have to use your feet to get it there and it ends up taking you <vocabulary>two days</vocabulary>! Darn this old fashioned foots car!

meaningMnemonic: <kanji>Person</kanji> <kanji>person</kanji>, why, that's more than one person. It's <vocabulary>people</vocabulary>!
readingMnemonic: The reading is the vocab word 人 twice, with the second one getting rendaku'd, turning it into びと.

meaningMnemonic: This word has two different meanings. <vocabulary>One day</vocabulary> and <vocabulary>first day</vocabulary>. No matter how you look at this word, those meanings make a lot of sense.
readingMnemonic:
The first meaning is <vocabulary>one day</vocabulary>, and it has the same reading as the kanji you learned, so you should already know this reading.

The reading for ”first day," however, is a big old exception. It doesn’t take the on’yomi or kun’yomi readings, and has its own special (weird) readings. Here’s a mnemonic to help  you remember this one:

On the <vocabulary>first day</vocabulary> of a new relationship you just <reading>tweet a chee</reading>sy (ついたち) line at each other. You're so in love you can't speak normally, so you just use bird calls and cheesy lines to communicate.

meaningMnemonic: <vocabulary>Seventh day</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>day seven</vocabulary>. No matter how you look at this word, it makes a lot of sense.
readingMnemonic:
Words that count days use an exceptional reading. Even though this is a jukugo word, it uses kun'yomi readings for both the kanji. Think about this word this way:

You've been stuck inside working for <vocabulary>seven days</vocabulary> and today you finally have a day off. Your friend asks you if you want to go for a drive but you say, "<reading>Nah, no car</reading> today" (なのか), because you've been cooped up long enough. Seven days is too long to be inside.

meaningMnemonic: <vocabulary>Third day</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>day three</vocabulary>. No matter how you look at this word, it makes a lot of sense.
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Counting days, however, are an exception (at least up to ten). You need to use the kun'yomi readings to read this word. You already know the kun'yomi reading for 三 from when you learned the word 三つ. Here is a way to remember the reading for 日:

On the <vocabulary>third day</vocabulary> you have to give <reading>me</reading> (み) a ride in your <reading>car</reading> (か). I don't have a car of my own, so don't forget me, okay?

meaningMnemonic: <vocabulary>Ninth day</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>day nine</vocabulary>. No matter how you look at this word, it makes a lot of sense.
readingMnemonic: Words that count days use an exceptional reading. Even though this is a jukugo word, it uses kun'yomi readings for both of the kanji. You learned this reading for 九 when you learned 九つ, so you should be able to read this one too!

meaningMnemonic: <vocabulary>Eighth day</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>day eight</vocabulary>. No matter how you look at this word, it makes a lot of sense.
readingMnemonic:
Words that count days use an exceptional reading. Even though this is a jukugo word, it uses kun'yomi readings for both the kanji. Think about this word this way:

Every <vocabulary>eighth day</vocabulary> you have to buy <reading>yo</reading>gurt to fuel your <reading>ca</reading>r (ようか). Your car runs on yogurt, but it needs to be refilled every <vocabulary>eight days</vocabulary> to keep running. It's really inconvenient because it's just ONE day more than a week. And a week just feels better. Oh well.

meaningMnemonic: <vocabulary>Tenth day</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>day ten</vocabulary>. No matter how you look at this word, it makes a lot of sense.
readingMnemonic:
Words that count days use an exceptional reading. Even though this is a jukugo word, it actually uses kun’yomi readings. Think about this word this way:

This marks the <vocabulary>tenth day</vocabulary> since this <reading>talker</reading> (とおか) began talking non-stop. Talkers love talking, but this one is getting quite unbearable… Ten days of talking, can you believe it?

Note that とお, generally pronounced "toh" with a long "o" sound, is not a common spelling, and you’ll only see it with a handful of kanji and vocabulary, like in this case. I mean, how often do you encounter a talker who can go on non-stop for ten days? That's pretty rare, right?

meaningMnemonic: If you're in the <kanji>middle</kanji> of a bunch of <kanji>water</kanji>, then you're most likely <vocabulary>underwater</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word that uses the on'yomi readings of the kanji. You should be able to read this on your own.

meaningMnemonic: Put <kanji>left</kanji> and <kanji>right</kanji> together and you end up with <vocabulary>left and right</vocabulary>! Just make sure you say them in the right order!
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word that uses the on'yomi readings of the kanji. You should be able to read this on your own.

meaningMnemonic: <kanji>Enter</kanji> something with all your <kanji>strength</kanji> and you will look down to see that what you're doing is <vocabulary>input</vocabulary>ting data. You're inputting it right now! Into this site! The answers you <vocabulary>enter</vocabulary> into WaniKani with all your strength can be said with this word. Wow. Full circle already, and you're only on level 2. Look at you go!
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Since 力 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use:

Where you work, <vocabulary>input</vocabulary>ting things earns you <reading>new</reading> <reading>lock</reading>s (にゅうりょく). Not only is your job only about <vocabulary>enter</vocabulary>ing things into a computer, you're also paid exclusively in new locks. Maybe consider a career change!

meaningMnemonic:
You know that the kanji 田 means <kanji>rice paddy</kanji>, so it should be easy to work out that this word also means <vocabulary>rice paddy</vocabulary>.

田んぼ is a common way to say "rice paddy," especially in casual conversation. You can tell because if you ever came across a ju<reading>mbo</reading> (んぼ) rice paddy, you'd probably say something like, "Whoa, that's one jumbo 田んぼ!" When a 田んぼ is that jumbo, the surprise makes your speech naturally more relaxed and informal.
readingMnemonic: This word has hiragana attached to the kanji, which is a good sign that the reading is kun'yomi. You learned that reading when you learned the kanji, so you should be good to go.

meaningMnemonic: You cut the <radical>leaf</radical> with your <radical>sword</radical> into <kanji>ten thousand</kanji> pieces!
readingMnemonic: Now that you have <kanji>ten thousand</kanji> pieces of tiny leaf paper, you decide to turn them into a <reading>man</reading>ga (まん)! Each little leaf piece will become a panel.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the radical are the same, so remembering the meaning of this kanji is as simple as making sure you know the radical for <radical>now</radical> first! If you know the radical, you know this kanji means <kanji>now</kanji> as well.
readingMnemonic: Right <kanji>now</kanji> the <reading>con</reading>dors (こん) are in danger! Not next week, not next year, right now! Haven't you heard that deforestation and pollution are killing all of the condors?!

meaningMnemonic: Your <radical>head</radical> has a <radical>person</radical> <kanji>inside</kanji> it!
readingMnemonic: You know this kanji means <kanji>inside</kanji>, so you can keep thinking about the person that's inside you, running around. You finally discover him, and try to take him out, but before you can, he pulls out a <reading>kni</reading>fe (ない) and threatens to cut you if you do anything irrational.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>fins</radical> that fell onto my <radical>sword</radical> were sliced into many <kanji>part</kanji>s. Following this logic, this also means <kanji>minute</kanji>, which is part of an hour, and <kanji>divide</kanji>, which is the action of splitting something into many parts.
readingMnemonic: Now that the fins have been cut into neat <kanji>part</kanji>s, it's time to cut something else: <reading>bun</reading>s (ぶん).

meaningMnemonic: If you have <radical>seven</radical> <radical>swords</radical>, there's only so much you can do with them. The main thing? Probably using them to <kanji>cut</kanji> something.
readingMnemonic: Because having seven swords for yourself is too much, you decide to take the swords and divide them up amongst pairs of people. You <kanji>cut</kanji> them into pairs, and give <reading>sets</reading> (せつ) to everyone who needs them in the village.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>slide</radical> is <radical>dry</radical> at <kanji>noon</kanji>. The sun is brightest at noon, so it dries the morning dew off the slide, making noon the perfect time to slide.
readingMnemonic: <kanji>Noon</kanji> also happens to be the time <reading>Go</reading>dzilla (ご) decided to attack for the same reasons: it's dry and kids are using the slide.

meaningMnemonic: A <radical>narwhal</radical> sitting on a <radical>stool</radical> is your <kanji>friend</kanji>.
readingMnemonic: "Hi <kanji>friend</kanji>," says the narwhal. "I must warn you about all the <reading>you</reading>ths." (ゆう)

meaningMnemonic: The kanji is the same as the radical, so this means <kanji>fat</kanji>.
readingMnemonic: Imagine a <kanji>fat</kanji> person who loves to sleep on <reading>futo</reading>n (ふと).

meaningMnemonic: You have a <radical>small</radical> <radical>slide</radical>. The thing about small slides is that <kanji>few</kanji> people can use them. Not everyone can fit their bum in a small slide — only a few small ones can make it down.
readingMnemonic: Another reason so <kanji>few</kanji> people can use it is that the <reading>Sho</reading>gun (しょう) is trying to go down the slide. We're talking a grown man who's way too big for this little slide, so a few people have to wait behind him — it's a slide traffic jam.

meaningMnemonic: If you have a <radical>bow</radical> and a <radical>stick</radical> you need to <kanji>pull</kanji> the two back together to shoot it. If you can't pull it, you won't be shooting anything.
readingMnemonic: When you <kanji>pull</kanji> the bow back, your body strains and you feel an immense <reading>he</reading>at (ひ) rise inside of you. You keep pulling and the heat grows hotter and hotter.

meaningMnemonic: The radical <radical>heart</radical> and the kanji <kanji>heart</kanji> are the same. Rejoice and let your heart be free!
readingMnemonic: The <kanji>heart</kanji>, where is it stored in your body? Most people thought it was in the chest, but in reality, your <kanji>heart</kanji> is stored in your <reading>shin</reading> (しん).

meaningMnemonic: The radical and the kanji for <kanji>door</kanji> are the same!
readingMnemonic: You open the <kanji>door</kanji> and immediately get it closed back on your <reading>toe</reading> (と), literally causing your toe to explode.

meaningMnemonic:
The radical and the kanji for <kanji>direction</kanji> are the same! Huzzah!

There is also another meaning too, though. This can also mean <kanji>way</kanji> as well. Think of it this way: When you move in one direction in your life, you are developing your own way. "The way of [your name here]."
readingMnemonic: What <kanji>direction</kanji> do you always know? Which direction do you always want to go? <reading>Ho</reading>me (ほう). Something about your home always stays with you, no matter how far away you go. So if you're lost and you don't know which direction to go, just think of home!

meaningMnemonic: The radical and the kanji for <kanji>cow</kanji> are exactly the same! That makes it easy to remember, so long as you know your radicals well enough.
readingMnemonic: Have you ever heard of わ<reading>ぎゅう</reading> (wagyu) beef? That ぎゅう is this ぎゅう.

meaningMnemonic: The radical and the kanji for <kanji>father</kanji> are the same.
readingMnemonic: Imagine your father is <reading>Cheech</reading> (ちち) from Cheech and Chong.

meaningMnemonic: <kanji>Fur</kanji> and <radical>fur</radical> are the same as each other, so the meaning of this kanji should be simple. This can also mean <kanji>hair</kanji>, too, though more like furry hair.
readingMnemonic: With <kanji>fur</kanji>, whisk your imagination to a land where there are stalks of fur all around, just like a big tall grassy field (except instead of grass, it's fur). You're surrounded by the black fur, the wind is blowing. Imagine yourself touching the long fur and smelling it. What does it feel and smell like? Now it's time to get rid of it. There's just too much <kanji>hair</kanji>! To get rid of it, you'll need to <reading>mow</reading> (もう) it down with a lawn mower. Imagine yourself starting up the lawn mower, and taking it around, mowing down the hair. Hear the sound of the mower in your head (they're loud and distinct). The important thing is to imagine yourself mowing everything. Key is to think "fur → in the fur → get rid of the fur → mow the fur," in that order. That'll get you to "mow," which will help you to remember the on’yomi.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>stop</radical> radical is the same as the <kanji>stop</kanji> kanji.
readingMnemonic: You have to <kanji>stop</kanji> because there is a <reading>shee</reading>p (し) in front of you. You try to walk around the sheep, but it moves to stop in front of you again. Every time the sheep stops you stop too.

meaningMnemonic: You’ve got a <radical>winter</radical> with lots of <radical>ice</radical>. But doesn't any winter have lots of ice? Exactly. That's why this is just <kanji>winter</kanji>.
readingMnemonic: <kanji>Winter</kanji> is also the time of year you give out presents: "One fu' you, one fu' you and… one <reading>fu' you</reading> (ふゆ)!"

meaningMnemonic: The kanji is the same as the radical — it means <kanji>north</kanji>.
readingMnemonic: You go north, and notice that everyone has a keytar (きた). You know, those keyboards that people hold and play like a guitar?

meaningMnemonic: A <radical>triceratops</radical> is lying out to <radical>dry</radical>. But it's so big, and it's lying on its side, so only <kanji>half</kanji> of it gets dry.
readingMnemonic: "You're half wet and half dry! I will cut you in <kanji>half</kanji> with my laser blaster so you're proper again," a voice says. It is  <reading>Han</reading> Solo (はん), the anti-establishment guy who hates when things aren't even.

meaningMnemonic:
The kanji and the radical are the same, so remembering the meaning of this kanji is as simple as making sure you know the radical for <radical>old</radical> first! If you know the radical, you know this kanji means <kanji>old</kanji> as well.

Note: This "old" does not refer to people. Think of it as "old things" instead.
readingMnemonic:
<kanji>Old</kanji> things are misunderstood by the <reading>子</reading>s (こ) of the world.

*Note:* Come up with a 子 character you can use for all the こ reading mnemonics going forward. This child will show up many times throughout your mnemonic journey, and it will help if you imagine the same character the whole time.

meaningMnemonic: You have a <radical>private</radical> <radical>mouth</radical> that's a <kanji>machine</kanji>. But you have to keep your machine mouth private because it would scare all the non-machine people around you.
readingMnemonic: To make sure they can't tell what's <kanji>machine</kanji> and what's human, you <reading>dye</reading> (だい) the mouth to make it look like skin. You find dye the same shade as your normal mouth and just slap it on there.

meaningMnemonic: You're known to get the ailment <radical>evening</radical> <radical>toe</radical>, which is when your toe catches on fire after getting really, really hot (imagine this happening, ouch!). The only way to fix it is to cool your toe down, and the best way to do that is to go <kanji>outside</kanji>.
readingMnemonic: You go <kanji>outside</kanji> (you're not supposed to be out there at night!) and you're accosted by a couple of guys. Then, all of a sudden, <reading>Guy</reading> (がい) Fawkes jumps from the roof above you and takes them out, then starts running. "Guy Fawkes!" you yell after him!

meaningMnemonic: You see a manhole <radical>lid</radical> and a <radical>towel</radical>, both with the name of a <kanji>city</kanji> on them. Decorative manhole lids and souvenir towels — two things cities love to slap their names on, especially in Japan.
readingMnemonic: This <kanji>city</kanji> must have a lot of <reading>shee</reading>p (し) because, besides the manhole lid and the towel, all you can see are sheep. There are sheep everywhere. It's a sheep city.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>canopy</radical> you use in <radical>private</radical> is very <kanji>wide</kanji>.
readingMnemonic: You like being <kanji>wide</kanji> because it makes you feel like a different person. But not just any person, like a super <reading>hero</reading> (ひろ)!

meaningMnemonic: Someone breaks part of your <radical>window</radical>. Who did this? It was your <kanji>mother</kanji>!
readingMnemonic: Suddenly your <kanji>mother</kanji> points her bloodied finger at you and laughs, "<reading>Haha</reading> (はは)!" She keeps laughing, haha haha haha, over and over. You feel even more alarmed.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the radical are the same, so remembering the meaning of this kanji is as simple as making sure you know the radical for <radical>task</radical> first! If you know the radical, you know this kanji means <kanji>task</kanji> as well.
readingMnemonic: Today, your <kanji>task</kanji> is to eat <reading>yo</reading>gurt (よう). It's nutritious, it's delicious, and I'm telling you to do it, so you have to. A task is basically just something you have to do, so that's what this is.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>arrow</radical> radical and the <kanji>arrow</kanji> kanji are the same. Sort of a "straight" shot to memorizing this kanji, you know?
readingMnemonic:
To remember the reading や we use the word “Yankees” (like the evil baseball team, go M’s!).

What is this arrow doing? It’s following around all the <reading>Ya</reading>nkee (や) baseball players, stalking them, waiting to make its move (I’ll let you decide what its “move” is).

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>roof</radical> <radical>child</radical> stands upon the roof, holding up a single <kanji>letter</kanji>, a single <kanji>character</kanji>, a single <kanji>symbol</kanji> for the world to see.
readingMnemonic: The <kanji>character</kanji> that is being shown is "J," which stands for <reading>Je</reading>sus (じ). The kid wants everyone to see this <kanji>letter</kanji> and <kanji>symbol</kanji>.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>bright</radical> radical and the <kanji>bright</kanji> kanji are the same!
readingMnemonic: What do you say when you see something <kanji>bright</kanji>? "<reading>Ah</reading> (あ)!" Like when you open the curtain and the sunshine hits your eyes, or someone shines a flashlight right into your face. Ahhhh, so bright!

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>life</radical> radical and the <kanji>life</kanji> kanji are the same, so hopefully you now just know them both.
readingMnemonic: You can save <kanji>life</kanji> or take it away with your new <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい). This is the saber you got from doing the correct thing (remember?), and now you need to figure out how to use it with this life. So what do you do?

meaningMnemonic: There's something draped over that stick. It's a <radical>towel</radical>! It's the kind of towel you clean with, so it's sort of old and crusty, which is why it holds its square shape even when it's hanging on the stick like that.

See the criss-cross in the almost-square shape? That's the frames of a big <radical>window</radical> you can look out of. It's not perfectly shaped like a rice field, it's more artistic. That's why this is a window and 田 is not.

Remember the radical for drop (of liquid)? Looks like it's shattered into two pieces here, which means it must be <radical>ice</radical> now. You know, since a drop could only break in two after turning into ice.

Remember the sprout from the dirt radical? This looks like that, but wait a minute… there's some extra growth now! This thing is so full of <radical>life</radical>! It's even got a big, slide-shaped leaf growing on the top left. That's why it's the life radical.

This is the radical for <radical>now</radical>. It's a bit tough to see, but it kind of looks like a fancy clock. The 𠆢 is the top part hanging over the clock. The ラ shows a triangular clock face with a little cuckoo on top, peeking out to tell you this means now — right now!
This looks just like a folding <radical>stool</radical>, am I right?

You only have some ice and a stick for your spoon, because you're way up north. At the top of the world, there's only ice to eat, and — if you're lucky — a bit of stick for flavor. That's the <radical>north</radical> for you.
The sun AND the moon are out? With both their light-powers combined, it's incredibly <radical>bright</radical> outside.

This looks like the guy from the big radical, but he's got something extra on his belly. That's his exposed belly button, because he's a sumo wrestler now: big and <radical>fat</radical>. In sumo, more fat means more power, so imagine he's been training to get as strong and fat as possible.

A leaf growing out of a… mouth?? Look again and see the mouth is actually made of <radical>stone</radical>. The leaf here was just growing out of a crack on the side. Makes this stone mouth sculpture look kinda elegant, if you ask me. And obviously leaves don't grow on mouths, so whenever you see a leaf on a mouth, remember it's gotta be stone.

This radical looks like a <radical>spoon</radical>! Well, a spoon that's been bent a lot by an angry person or a psychic. Go grab a spoon and try to bend it into this shape. Then hold on to it so you'll never forget that this is the spoon radical!

This is the radical for <radical>winter</radical>. It looks like a skier going downhill (to the left), with their skis out in front and their poles out behind them. And when do people ski? In winter!

You could also remember this as looking like the evening radical but with an extra long stroke, since the evenings are long in winter. It gets dark early in winter, you know?

This looks like a clothesline pole you would <radical>dry</radical> clothes on. It's got two very straight horizontal poles, for maximum drying efficiency. If you were gonna dry your clothes, you'd dry them on this thing.
This is a <radical>canopy</radical>. See the cloth draping down and the little point at the middle holding it up? You could sit under here and enjoy a bright summer day without getting any sun!

This looks like a long index finger bent at the bottom, with several other stubby fingers growing out of it on either side. That makes it the <radical>fingers</radical> radical. A bit creepy, but imagine how easily you could grip something or scratch your back with fingery fingers like these. Pretty nice, right?

This is also a lot like the hand radical (手) but smaller. And what are the smaller parts of a hand? The fingers! You can remember it that way too, if you want.

Take a good look at this one. It kind of looks like a <radical>heart</radical>. Not the heart emoji kind, but a real-deal beating heart. Basically, there's a lump in the middle (the heart), and three lines that make up the arteries. Okay, so it's not the most anatomically correct heart, but just look at those floppy arteries going in and out, doing their thing and keeping you alive. What's not to love?
This radical looks like a dude running off to the right. He's heading in a very distinct <radical>direction</radical>. Which direction was it again?
Do you remember the hair radical? This is like that, except now the hair is attached to a tail. Animals don't usually have hair though. Instead, they have <radical>fur</radical>.

This is the <radical>father</radical> radical, since you can see a very fatherly face in it — two droopy eyes at the top, a big nose in the middle, and a mustache flaring out at the bottom. Sort of a generic older gentleman father-type face, isn't it?

Put a cross over the mouth of a container full of all the <radical>old</radical> things you no longer need. They've all served their purpose but have gotten old, so it's now time to let them rest in peace under the cross. Goodbye, old things!

This looks like a little whiteboard to keep track of your <radical>task</radical>s for the day. It's got three little legs on the bottom. And see those blank squares? They're sticky notes you can write your tasks on. Work tasks, school tasks, WaniKani reviews… So what are your tasks for today? Imagine putting them in those little squares.

This is a gun on top of a big person. The gun is big too, obviously. Why is this big guy's gun so big? Because it shoots <radical>arrow</radical>s! Bullets are pretty small, so the guns that shoot them can be any size. But you need a big gun if you want to shoot a big arrow, right? That's why this is the radical for arrow.

This one looks like a flag, but don't be fooled — it's got a line over the top of it, kind of like a door frame. That makes this a little <radical>door</radical>. Specifically a swinging door, with the vertical line on the left being its door hinge. Kind of a weird door, it's true, but you can see it, right?
This is a <radical>roof</radical> ー a flat roof with a little chimney sticking out. How cute!

For better or worse, this looks enough like the poop emoji (💩) to make this the <radical>poop</radical> radical. Try to remember it for its distinct outline. If it helps you can also note the private radical on the bottom of this one, since you should probably be in private when you poop…
Do you know those rice paddy hats? The ones shaped just like this radical? That's why this radical is a <radical>hat</radical>.
The three horns represent the <radical>triceratops</radical>, one of the most awesome dinosaurs ever.

<reading>Colle</reading>ct (これ) <vocabulary>this one</vocabulary> right here! Go ahead, collect this one.

You can use これ to talk about anything that's close to you. Things that are close to you are easy to collect, after all!

It's only <reading>through</reading> (する) doing things that we get better at them, so you need <vocabulary>to do</vocabulary> them as much as you can.

する basically means <vocabulary>to do</vocabulary>, but it’s incredibly versatile and goes beyond its English equivalent. Besides being used alone, it can be put together with a whole range of nouns to turn them into verbs. We call these する verbs and you'll learn a lot of them as we go along!

<reading>Ringo</reading> (リンゴ) just ate your <vocabulary>apple</vocabulary>! You were about to take a bite, and he grabbed it. What is Ringo doing snatching apples? Surely he can afford his own.

Did you know that Ringo Starr once appeared in a Japanese TV commercial for <vocabulary>apple</vocabulary> juice? That might help you remember this word, too!

リンゴ is also commonly written in hiragana, and even in kanji, but you’ll probably see it in katakana the most.

コーヒー is the katakana version of the English word <vocabulary>coffee</vocabulary>. 

Just watch out for the second syllable, which changes to ヒー in Japanese.

<reading>Connie Chihua</reading>hua (こんにちは) says <vocabulary>hello</vocabulary>. Connie Chihuahua is an amazing talking chihuahua. She can only say "hello", but that alone is pretty impressive.

こんにちは is a formal way to say "hello". It’s generally used during the daytime, from late morning to early evening. Watch out for the は on the end, which is always pronounced わ.

He <reading>eats</reading> (いつ) and eats and eats. <vocabulary>When</vocabulary> does this guy not eat?

いつ is a question word that is used to ask <vocabulary>when</vocabulary>.

The アメリカ part is pretty easy so long as you know katakana. It's the word for "America." You know that <kanji>人</kanji> means "person," so you can figure out that America + Person = <vocabulary>American person</vocabulary> — specifically a <vocabulary>person from the USA</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
Often times, when two kanji come together to form a kanji compound (or jukugo), you use the on'yomi readings. This is similar to that, though instead of another kanji it's a foreign word. This pattern works with almost any country as well. Just add 人 to a country name and you've described the people who live there. You'll see more of these before you're finished here.

And since 人 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use: 

<vocabulary>Americans</vocabulary> absolutely love their <reading>jean</reading>s (じん), it's a well-known fact!

meaningMnemonic: フランス is "France." So what is a "France person?" That would be a <vocabulary>French person</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
Often times, when two kanji come together to form a kanji compound (or jukugo), you use the on'yomi readings. This is similar to that, though instead of another kanji it's a foreign word. This pattern works with almost any country as well. Just add 人 to a country name and you've described the people who live there. You'll see more of these before you're finished here.

And since 人 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use: 

As with all words for nationalities, the 人 here is pronounced じん. Like people of all other nationalities, a <vocabulary>French person</vocabulary> loves their <reading>jean</reading>s (じん) too!

meaningMnemonic: When you see a katakana word, it's a safe bet that it is a foreign loan word from somewhere else. This one's interesting, because it combines katakana and kanji! The first <vocabulary>marble</vocabulary> came to Japan from Portugal, so the ビー part of the word is short for "vidro," which means "glass" in Portuguese. Anyway, don't get thrown off by that ビー. Maybe just imagine that your favorite marble is black and yellow like a bee.
readingMnemonic: If you know katakana as well as the reading that you learned when you learned the kanji <kanji>玉</kanji>, you should be able to read this. Just watch out for the rendaku.

meaningMnemonic: Since this is a kanji with hiragana attached that ends with an う sound, you know that this word is a verb. What's the ultimate verb version of the kanji 生? That would be <vocabulary>to be born</vocabulary>, the very beginning of <kanji>life</kanji> as we know it!
readingMnemonic:
You have to remember the う part of this word to read it. To remember that, let's use a mnemonic: 

Something is about to be born, and when you lean in for a closer look… it’s an <reading>u</reading>ni (う). Yes, a sea urchin. You weren’t expecting that. Close your eyes and imagine what the process of an uni being born might look like, to help you remember this reading.

meaningMnemonic:
Normally a single kanji alone like this would mean the same thing as its parent kanji, but in the case of <kanji>生</kanji> it's a little different (but still related). When you think of something that has life, it's <vocabulary>fresh</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>raw</vocabulary>. Think sashimi or sushi, for example. You want that to be as close as possible to life as you can, so you eat it when it's fresh and raw. This word can also be used to describe a <vocabulary>live</vocabulary> event, like a concert or sporting event. 

You'll see this word at the beginning of other words. For example, 生たまご is "raw egg."
readingMnemonic:
This uses one of the kun'yomi readings. Here's a mnemonic to help you remember it:

After eating <vocabulary>raw</vocabulary> fish, my yoga teacher and I say <reading>nama</reading>ste (なま) to the sushi chef, bowing low to show our appreciation.

meaningMnemonic: When you have <kanji>one</kanji> <kanji>ten thousand</kanji> you just have <vocabulary>ten thousand</vocabulary>. In Japanese, though, you specify how many ten thousands you have, though (which is weird in English, but should make sense if you think about it).
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: When you have <kanji>two</kanji> <kanji>ten thousand</kanji>s, you have <vocabulary>twenty thousand</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: <kanji>Ten</kanji> <kanji>ten thousand</kanji>s is equal to <vocabulary>one hundred thousand</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading. When learning the kanji, you didn't learn that reading, so here's a mnemonic to help you with this word: 

What'll you drink now? I'll have a Zima, <reading>ima</reading>.

Say "ZIMA IMA" ten times fast.

meaningMnemonic: The <kanji>now</kanji> <kanji>day</kanji> is what day? Oh, it's <vocabulary>today</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
The reading for this word is a complete exception, though the word itself is incredibly common and useful. When you think of "today" think about what you have to do <vocabulary>today</vocabulary> for work.

You have to work in one of those mall <reading>kio</reading>sks (きょう). Just think about how sad you are that you have to do this today. What a crummy day. It's sunny outside and you're stuck in a mall manning this hermit crab kiosk.

Note: This reading mnemonic is pretty far away from the actual reading, as far as reading mnemonics go. Use this to get yourself close, and then use your own brain grapes to get the rest of the way there. This one won't be as easy as most of the others, but do some good old fashioned memorization and you should be okay. I won't ask you to do this much, fortunately. :)

meaningMnemonic: The <kanji>now</kanji> <kanji>month</kanji> is actually <vocabulary>this month</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. 月 has two on'yomi readings, but since this is NOT a specific month name, it's going to use the げつ reading. Here's a mnemonic to help you remember that:

Only <vocabulary>this month</vocabulary>, your pet <reading>con</reading>dor <reading>gets</reading> (こんげつ) to sleep in your bed. You usually keep it in a cage, but once a year, you make an exception. And that's this month. How cute!

meaningMnemonic: You have <kanji>half</kanji> a <kanji>part</kanji>. Half a part is still <vocabulary>half</vocabulary>, right?
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic:
This word has the same meaning that you learned with the kanji: <vocabulary>part</vocabulary>, or <vocabulary>portion</vocabulary>. By extension, it can also mean <vocabulary>content</vocabulary>, as in "alcohol content" because that's the portion of alcohol in a drink.

This word is used for fractions, too, as in one third or one quarter. Again, you can see how this links to "parts" and "portions", right?
readingMnemonic:
This word is usually combined with a word or phrase indicating whose part or portion you're referring to. That's probably why this single kanji vocab word uses the on'yomi instead of the kun'yomi reading. Check out the common word combinations to see what we're talking about!

And when it means <vocabulary>part</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>portion</vocabulary>, it's read ぶん. Probably because your buns come in two parts. Ohoho.

meaningMnemonic: Think back to the old old olden days. Back when decisions were made with swords. A <kanji>big</kanji> <kanji>cut</kanji> is going to be <vocabulary>important</vocabulary>. Just imagine a couple samurai squaring off, then charging, then making one big cut. It's the most important thing they'll do in their lives (if they live!).
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Since 大 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use:

It's extremely <vocabulary>important</vocabulary> to keep your <reading>tie</reading>s in <reading>sets</reading> (たいせつ). If you don't arrange them neatly in sets, the tie police might just come knocking at your door and take you with them!

meaningMnemonic: This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>cut</kanji>, and the verb form of that is <vocabulary>to cut</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: When you think <vocabulary>to cut</vocabulary> something, what do you think about cutting? One thing is cutting down a tree, which is also known as <reading>木</reading> (き). You 切る a 木.

meaningMnemonic:
A person who is a friend is just that, a <vocabulary>friend</vocabulary>!

Note that 友人 is a more formal word that’s often used in professional settings.
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Since 人 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use:

All your <vocabulary>friend</vocabulary>s are <reading>you</reading>ths in <reading>jean</reading>s (ゆうじん)! Every single one. If any of your friends is not a youth in jeans, they're simply not your friend, I'm afraid.

meaningMnemonic: This is a single kanji with an い on the end, meaning you know it's probably an adjective. What's the adjective form of the <kanji>fat</kanji>? It's the same: <vocabulary>fat</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
This is the kun'yomi reading of the kanji (because it's a single kanji, all alone with hiragana attached). You learned that reading with the kanji, so you should be good to go. But here's a mnemonic just in case:

The <vocabulary>fat</vocabulary> cat loves to curl up on the <reading>futo</reading>n (ふと) all day.

meaningMnemonic: This word is a single kanji with hiragana attached, though there is no specific clue as to what type of word it is. That means you can usually guess it's a noun, adverb or na-adjective (in this case it's a noun / adverb). The meaning of this word is pretty much the same as the kanji, though, making it fairly easy. The kanji meaning for <kanji>少</kanji> is <kanji>few</kanji>, and the meaning of the vocab form is <vocabulary>a little</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>a few</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: Did you know that this word created an English word you probably already know? Have you ever heard someone say "just a skosh"? That's <vocabulary>少し</vocabulary>! Hopefully you know this English word and therefore know this Japanese word as well.

meaningMnemonic: This word has a single kanji with hiragana attached, ending in a い. This means you know it's an adjective. What is the adjective form of <kanji>few</kanji>? It's also <vocabulary>few</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
The reading for this one is fairly difficult, as it's the kun'yomi reading (you can tell it's kun'yomi because of all the hiragana attached). You have to remember the すく part to remember the reading of this word. Think of it this way: 

You have a <vocabulary>few</vocabulary> of something. This something is <reading>suck</reading>ers (すく). Imagine putting all (three-ish) of them in your mouth at once. How does it taste? Can you taste the flavors from each of the few?

meaningMnemonic:
This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>pull</kanji> and the verb version vocab means <vocabulary>to pull</vocabulary>.

This word can be transitive or intransitive. You use 引く in a transitive way to say you pull something, for example. But it can also mean <vocabulary>to withdraw</vocabulary> when you talk about things that pull back, like water that recedes, pain that subsides, soldiers that withdraw, or even your body recoiling when something puts you off.
readingMnemonic: Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. The reading (ひ) is the same as the one you learned for the kanji, meaning you should already know it!

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading. When learning the kanji, you didn't learn that reading, so here's a mnemonic to help you with this word: 

Imagine a giant <vocabulary>heart</vocabulary> sitting in a rowing boat. It's also drinking cocoa. Focus on all three of these parts and associate them into one story / memory. Once you've done that, sing the "<reading>cocoa row</reading>" (こころ) song! I don't know what that song is, but I want you to make one up and actually sing it, out loud. If you do, I bet you won't forget this word's reading too easily.

meaningMnemonic:
A <kanji>door</kanji>'s <kanji>mouth</kanji> is the <vocabulary>doorway</vocabulary>! 

戸口 is mostly used for doorways that lead outside. It has an old-fashioned feel to it and is becoming less common these days, especially in spoken Japanese.

You might notice that this word resembles 出口 (exit) and 入り口 (entrance). Like in those words, the character 口 just represents an opening here.
readingMnemonic: The reading for this one is mixed. You get the kun'yomi readings for both kanji, even though it's a combo (jukugo) kanji. Since 戸's only reading is the kun'yomi reading you should know it. Also, you should know the kun'yomi reading for 口 as well from level 1. That means as long as you remember that this is a kun'yomi fest over here, you should be able to read this word. Also watch out for the rendaku (also known as sequential voicing). That just means the く becomes the voiced ぐ.

meaningMnemonic:
The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.

You learned 方 means <vocabulary>direction</vocabulary>, but it can also mean <vocabulary>way</vocabulary>, as in この方がいい (this <vocabulary>way</vocabulary> is better) or やり方 (the <vocabulary>way</vocabulary> of doing something). 

Interestingly, 方 can also be used to refer to people politely, like こちらの方 (this person). This is because, in traditional Japanese, people—especially noble ones—were often referred to indirectly, as if indicating their presence from a direction.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading, but it can actually use kun'yomi or on'yomi depending on the context. Here are a couple mnemonics to help you remember both readings and how they're most often used:

You can always remember which <vocabulary>direction</vocabulary> your <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう) is in. It's in こっちの方 (this direction)!

And the best <vocabulary>way</vocabulary> to get there is with a <reading>cata</reading>pult (かた). Just climb in the catapult and launch yourself there — it's the best やり方.

meaningMnemonic: This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>stop</kanji>, so the verb vocab version of that is <vocabulary>to stop something</vocabulary>. Alternatively, it can also mean <vocabulary>to stop doing something</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. This word has two different readings for its slightly different meanings, so here's a couple mnemonics to help remember which is which:

When you need <vocabulary>to stop something</vocabulary> from moving, you use your <reading>toe</reading> (と). For most people, this would just lead to a stubbed toe, but you have toes of iron — sturdy enough to stop even speeding locomotives in their tracks.

On the other hand, when you need <vocabulary>to stop doing something</vocabulary> — you know, a bad habit or whatnot — you call over the <reading>Ya</reading>nkees (や). Imagine the whole New York Yankees squad cheering you on (or threatening you with their bats). You'd be able to quit immediately.

meaningMnemonic: You're in the <kanji>middle</kanji> of something when someone yells "<kanji>stop</kanji>!" They hit you with a <vocabulary>cancellation</vocabulary> order and demand the immediate <vocabulary>discontinuation</vocabulary> of all your activities. Welp, it looks like you've been canceled — better go find something else to do.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading. When learning the kanji, you didn't learn that reading, so here's a mnemonic to help you with this word: 

Look down! You're holding something covered in <vocabulary>fur</vocabulary>. It's a furry <reading>ke</reading>ttle (け)! It's the weirdest kettle you've ever seen, but presumably it keeps the water hot for much longer.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well. This is the polite and humble form of <vocabulary>father</vocabulary>. You use this when you're talking about your own <vocabulary>dad</vocabulary> to other people in formal settings, especially if you aren't very close to them.
readingMnemonic: The reading for this word is the same as the one you learned when learning the kanji.

meaningMnemonic:
This word begins with an honorific (お), making whatever comes after it more elevated. Then there's 父, which you know means <kanji>father</kanji>. Lastly, we have the honorific name-ender <ja>さん</ja>. So this is a nice way to say <vocabulary>father</vocabulary>. 

You'll want to use this when you're addressing or calling out to your own <vocabulary>dad</vocabulary>, or when you're talking about him or someone else's dad in a casual setting.
readingMnemonic:
The part you have to learn to read is the 父 portion. It uses the kun'yomi, but not the one you initially learned with the kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you remember this new reading:

The thing your <vocabulary>father</vocabulary> loves the most is <reading>とう</reading>きょう. He will not stop talking about とうきょう to you, and you just have to listen. とうきょう this, とうきょう that. To really get this picture in your head, make sure to imagine your dad incessantly droning on about とうきょう.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are the same. This word is a bit broader than <vocabulary>cow</vocabulary> though, and can also be used to be <vocabulary>bull</vocabulary> and <vocabulary>ox</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it uses the kun'yomi reading. Here's a mnemonic to help you to remember it: 

You're leading the cows around the field. To get them to move, you yell out "<reading>USH</reading>, USH, USH!" (うし) because you have a speech impediment and want to say "MUSH MUSH MUSH." Go ahead and say this out loud, yelling it if you can.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: The reading for this word is the same as the kun'yomi reading you learned for the kanji, meaning you should know both!

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it uses the kun'yomi reading. You already learned that reading with the kanji, so you should be good to go here!

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it's supposed to use the kun'yomi reading. But, there is no useful kun'yomi reading for this kanji, so even the single kanji vocab word version uses the on'yomi reading, which you've already learned!

meaningMnemonic:
This is a single kanji with an い on the end, meaning you know it's probably an adjective. What's the adjective form of <kanji>old</kanji>? It's also <vocabulary>old</vocabulary>, <vocabulary>ancient</vocabulary>, or <vocabulary>out-of-date</vocabulary>.

This word is used for things like clothes, food and friendships, but not for people! So a 古い友人 (old friend) is someone you've been friends with for a long time, not an elderly friend.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji with some hiragana attached, it's going to use the kun'yomi reading. You learned the on'yomi when learning the kanji portion, so let's use a mnemonic to help you with the reading of this word: 

If you're <vocabulary>old</vocabulary>, you're also <reading>full</reading> (ふる) of memories. And you're probably full of old secrets. And you're full from old people food.

meaningMnemonic: The combination of kanji doesn't make perfect sense, but it's alright. Take something that's <kanji>middle</kanji> <kanji>old</kanji> and you have something that's <vocabulary>used</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>secondhand</vocabulary>.
