It's not 100% old yet... it's still in the middle, meaning you can give it to someone else to use, like your younger siblings.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: As long as you know the two kanji that make up this word, you should be able to figure out the meaning of this word as well.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: When you combine these two kanji, you should be able to see how it forms the word <vocabulary>two machines</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: As long as you know both of the individual kanji meanings, you should be able to put together this one to get <vocabulary>five machines</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: If you know the two kanji separately, you should be able to put them together to form the meaning of this kanji.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic:
The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.

Note that 外 doesn't always simply indicate the physical position of something. It can also be used figuratively to refer to what's outside of one's inner circle or home.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading. When learning the kanji, you didn't learn that reading, so here's a mnemonic to help you with this word: 

You look <vocabulary>outside</vocabulary>, it's totally frozen. You throw <reading>salt</reading> (そと) outside to melt the ice.

meaningMnemonic: An "outside person" is a <vocabulary>foreigner</vocabulary>. This word is fairly common, though it's not super polite, either. It's okay most of the time, but it can be used derogatorily depending on the situation. The more polite version is 外国人 (outside country person) but you won't learn that until you learn the kanji for 国.
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Since 人 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use:

Who's the <vocabulary>foreigner</vocabulary>? Well obviously the <reading>guy</reading> wearing <reading>jean</reading>s (がいじん)!

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: The reading for this word is the same as the one you learned when learning the kanji.

meaningMnemonic: When the <kanji>city</kanji> <kanji>stand</kanji>s something up, that thing becomes <vocabulary>municipal</vocabulary>. You know, like a <vocabulary>city-run</vocabulary> library or a city-run gym or something. The city is what keeps that place standing.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: The name of the city (シアトル／しあとる) with a <kanji>市</kanji> attached to it means it is "the city of ____." You can do this with any city (try it out on your hometown!). So this word means <vocabulary>Seattle</vocabulary>, or more specifically <vocabulary>City of Seattle</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This word uses on'yomi reading, which you learned with the kanji.

meaningMnemonic: This is a single kanji with an い on the end, meaning you know it's probably an adjective. What's the adjective form of <kanji>wide</kanji>? It's also <vocabulary>wide</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: The reading for this word is the same as the reading you learned in the kanji section: ひろ, making this <reading>ひろ</reading>い.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they both mean mother. 母 is a formal and humble way to say "my mother" when talking to others. By using the plain form and omitting respectful language toward your own mom, you actually sound more humble, which makes a polite impression on the person you're speaking to. You wouldn't, however, use it when talking to your mom or when talking about someone else's mom — it would come off as too blunt for that!
readingMnemonic: The reading is the kun'yomi reading, but this is the reading you learned with the kanji. Just remember, your <vocabulary>mother</vocabulary> is a terrible person and won't stop laughing at you: "<reading>HA HA</reading>! HA HA!”

meaningMnemonic:
This word begins with an honorific (お), which generally adds a nuance of respect or politeness to what comes after it. Then there's 母, which you know means <kanji>mother</kanji>. Lastly, we have the honorific name-ender さん. So this is a nice way to say <vocabulary>mother</vocabulary>. You use it when talking about the <vocabulary>mom</vocabulary>s of friends or casual acquaintances, when talking about your own mom in a casual social setting, or when talking to your own mom.

In more formal settings, you'll want to use 母 (pronounced はは) to talk about your own mom and the super respectful お母さま to talk about someone else's mom.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it uses the kun'yomi reading. You have to remember the かあ portion. Here's a mnemonic to help you to remember it: 

What does your super nice <vocabulary>mother</vocabulary> do for you (this is why you're so polite to her!)? She drives you all around in her <reading>car</reading> (かあ) taking you wherever you need to go... soccer, kendo, whatever. What a nice mother you have!

meaningMnemonic: This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>task</kanji> or <kanji>use</kanji>. You can think of this verb as a more formal way to say <vocabulary>to use</vocabulary>, and that is <vocabulary>to utilize</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn that reading with this kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you to learn the もち portion of もちいる: 

What will you <vocabulary>utilize</vocabulary> to get the job done? You have <reading>mochi</reading> (もち) in your hand, so you'll use that. Now go think of some kind of imaginary task you'll complete with mochi, making it as ridiculous as possible.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: The reading for this word is the same as the one you learned when learning the kanji.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: You only learned one reading for the kanji that makes up this word, and it's the same as the reading for this word (of course). That means you should already know the reading.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji for <kanji>person</kanji> and <kanji>life</kanji> combined makes you think about human life. What is your <vocabulary>human life</vocabulary> like?
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Since 人 and 生 both have two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you:

Your <vocabulary>life</vocabulary> boils down to just two things: <reading>jean</reading>s and a <reading>sa</reading>ber (じんせい). Your whole life, that's all you've ever needed, jeans and a saber. What are you, some kind of American pirate?

meaningMnemonic: イギリス means "Great Britain" or "United Kingdom." So what is a "Great Britain person?" You guessed it — a <vocabulary>British person</vocabulary>, or <vocabulary>Brit</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
Often times, when two kanji come together to form a kanji compound (or jukugo), you use the on'yomi readings. This is similar to that, though instead of another kanji it's a foreign word. This pattern works with almost any country as well. Just add 人 to a country name and you've described the people who live there. You'll see more of these before you're finished here.

And since 人 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use: 

As with all words for nationalities, the 人 here is pronounced じん. All nationalities, including <vocabulary>Brit</vocabulary>s, love <reading>jean</reading>s (じん)!

meaningMnemonic:
The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.

When this kanji is a word on its own, it's actually very often written in hiragana. Still, the kanji version of the word is worth knowing, too!
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading. When learning the kanji, you didn't learn that reading, so here's a mnemonic to help you with this word:

There's something inside and you wanna get to what's within. Look inside. <reading>Ooh, chee</reading>se (うち)! Your favorite. Ooh cheese, how I love you.

meaningMnemonic: This counter is used with some kind of <kanji>machine</kanji> or device, usually a fairly large one (so nothing itty bitty). Cars are a good example of this. But it can also be used for things like computers, which were once the size of rooms, but are now nice and small. Thanks technology!
readingMnemonic: The reading is the same as the one you learned with the kanji. Be sure to omit the 〜 in your answer as it is only a placeholder for a number.

meaningMnemonic: When you see this on a number, for example ７人 or ３人, you're using it to count people. Just remember, both 一人 and 二人 have alternative readings. From 3 and up it's just the number plus にん, like this. ５人 is ごにん, １０人 is じゅうにん, etc. This is the <vocabulary>counter for people</vocabulary>, which makes this word the <vocabulary>number of people</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
This is a counter, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Since 人 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use:

Just think of the sheer <vocabulary>number of people</vocabulary> at <reading>Nin</reading>tendo (にん). It must be huge. This really is just a <vocabulary>counter for people</vocabulary> that work at Nintendo.

meaningMnemonic:
A <kanji>little</kanji> <kanji>woman</kanji> is a <vocabulary>girl</vocabulary>. 

少女 is a relatively formal word that's often used in writing to describe a <vocabulary>young girl</vocabulary>, usually between childhood and adolescence (or between the ages of about five and eighteen).
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: When a vocab word is a single kanji without okurigana (hiragana attached to the kanji) it usually has the same meaning as the kanji it's spawned from. If you know the kanji meaning you'll also know the vocab meaning. It's <vocabulary>ten thousand</vocabulary>!
readingMnemonic: When a vocab word is a single kanji with no okurigana, it usually uses the kun'yomi reading. Numbers are an exception to this. Numbers use the on'yomi reading, which you've already learned by learning the kanji's reading! How nice!

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: This one uses the on'yomi reading, which you've learned from the kanji already!

meaningMnemonic: Remember how you learned that 大人 means "adult?" Well this is the adjective version of that. The I-adjective (see how it ends in い?). What adjective do adults want to be able to use for their own kids? They want to say they are <vocabulary>obedient</vocabulary>, <vocabulary>quiet</vocabulary> kids!
readingMnemonic: You already learned this reading when you learned 大人! You should be able to read this too!

meaningMnemonic:
A <kanji>door</kanji> is a <vocabulary>door</vocabulary> — the kanji and the word are exactly the same.

戸 can refer to any kind of door, but it's more commonly used for <vocabulary>sliding door</vocabulary>s, since those are the traditional style of doors in Japan. For Western-style or high-tech doors, people tend to just say ドア.
readingMnemonic: This has the same reading as the kanji you learned, meaning you know the reading!

meaningMnemonic:
A <kanji>woman</kanji> <kanji>person</kanji> is a <vocabulary>woman</vocabulary>.

The の particle here means the first noun is modifying the second one, so you can probably see why 女の人 (a woman person) really just means woman! It's more polite than saying 女 by itself, so that's all you have to remember.
readingMnemonic: This word is actually two separate words combined with a particle. So, since you treat them like separate words, you read each of them with the kun'yomi readings (they are both single kanji words all alone). That being said, in level 1 you learned about the vocab readings for these separate words, so use that knowledge to remember the reading for this word as well.

meaningMnemonic:
This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. 

The kanji itself means <kanji>wide</kanji>, and the verb version is what happens when you try to make something wider. How do you make something wider? That's right, you have <vocabulary>to spread something</vocabulary>, <vocabulary>to unfold something</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>to open something up</vocabulary>. All of those actions will make a thing wider!

This is a transitive verb. That means it takes a direct object. You spread something. Like a newspaper, or a cloth on a table.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word has okurigana (hiragana attached to the kanji) you know that it's probably going to be the kun'yomi reading. You learned this reading with the kanji, but here's a reminder mnemonic to help you:

You are going <vocabulary>to spread something</vocabulary>. What are you going to spread? How about your arms, so that you can fly into the sky like the super <reading>hero</reading> (ひろ) that you are! Most heroes can fly when they spread their arms wide!

meaningMnemonic:
You know that 山 means mountain. Add the hiragana びこ to the end and this gives you <vocabulary>echo</vocabulary>. That's because bee children (bee 子) love echos, especially <vocabulary>mountain echo</vocabulary>s! Imagine baby bees buzzing as loudly as they can and hearing their voices echoing back at them through the mountains.

As the kanji suggests, 山びこ is generally used to describe the echo you get if you shout in the mountains. To describe other types of echo, you can use the katakana word エコー.

By the way, there is actually a kanji for the second part of this word, but it's so rare that it's usually written in kana!
readingMnemonic: You learned this kanji reading when you learned the vocabulary 山.

meaningMnemonic: First we have a katakana word, so it's safe to assume that it's a loan word. Like many loan words, it comes from English. Say it out loud and what do you have? That's right, "table". Then comes the particle の, which joins two nouns together. Finally we have 上, which you learned means "up" or "above". Put all of these pieces together and we have a phrase meaning <vocabulary>on the table</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>tabletop</vocabulary>!
readingMnemonic: In this phrase, 上 is a standalone word, so it uses the same reading that you learned when you learned the vocabulary. Easy to stay on top of that one, right?

meaningMnemonic: This starts with another katakana word, so you can assume that it's a loan word. This one also comes from English. Can you figure out what it means? That's right, "bed". Then comes the particle の, which joins two nouns together. Finally, we have 下, which you learned means "down" or "below". Put these elements together and you get <vocabulary>below the bed</vocabulary>, <vocabulary>under the bed</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>beneath the bed</vocabulary>. What's under your bed?
readingMnemonic: 下 is a standalone word here, so it uses the same reading that you learned when you learned the vocabulary.

meaningMnemonic: What do we call the <kanji>sun</kanji>'s <kanji>exit</kanji> from the horizon? That'll be the <vocabulary>sunrise</vocabulary>. Imagine the sun exiting the horizon and climbing up into the sky.
readingMnemonic: This uses the kanji's kun'yomi readings, which you've already learned. As long as you remember those, you should be good to go!

meaningMnemonic:
You just learned the vocab 生, meaning fresh. Combined with the katakana word for beer makes this draft beer or unpasteurized beer.

Note that while in a lot of countries, "draft beer" refers only to beer that's drawn from a keg, 生ビール can also refer to unpasteurized canned or bottled beer, giving it a shorter shelf-life, but also keeping it fresh.
readingMnemonic: Be careful to use kun'yomi for 生 here. Other than that, as long as you know your katakana, you should be good to go!

meaningMnemonic: Remember how 入り口 means "entrance" and 出口 means "exit"? This vocab is just the 口 part with a direction added to it. This also makes it both an entrance or exit. So when you have the kanji for <kanji>north</kanji>, it makes this both a <vocabulary>north exit</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>north entrance</vocabulary>. This is a great word to know if you need to know which side of a train station you need to leave on to go somewhere or meet someone!
readingMnemonic: Just like 入り口 and 出口, this also uses kun'yomi readings. And just like before, watch out for the rendaku that changes くち to ぐち!

meaningMnemonic:
What makes it <kanji>bright</kanji> around here? It's <vocabulary>light</vocabulary>!

Note: 明かり is mostly used for light coming from a specific source, like a lamp or light bulb. You'll learn a more general word for light in a couple levels.
readingMnemonic: Since this word has okurigana (hiragana attached to the kanji) you know that it's probably going to be the kun'yomi reading. You already know the reading from the kanji!

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>hole</radical> radical and the <kanji>hole</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Spectacular!
readingMnemonic: <reading>Ana</reading> (あな) fell into a <kanji>hole</kanji>. Laugh at Ana and her unfortunate misfortune of having fallen into a hole like a ninny. Haha, Ana – you’re stupid and clumsy!

meaningMnemonic: Under his <radical>roof</radical>, the <radical>king</radical> stares into a <radical>drop</radical> of water containing his greatest <kanji>treasure</kanji>. Just kidding, the king has way more treasure than that. The drop of water is just reflecting what's around him: mounds and mounds of treasure, stacked all the way up to the roof.
readingMnemonic: All of this <kanji>treasure</kanji> is kept in the king's <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう). He can't let other people see how much treasure he has, or they'll get mad. Or worse, ask him to share some with his people. Yuck. The treasure stays at home.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>stone</radical> radical and <kanji>stone</kanji> kanji are the same thing!
readingMnemonic: You throw a <kanji>stone</kanji> through a window... and, now you can see something very <reading>sexy</reading> (せき).

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>origin</radical> radical and the <kanji>origin</kanji> kanji are the same!
readingMnemonic:
The <kanji>origin</kanji> of everything can be found in <reading>Gen</reading>ji (げん). Genji is the name of the main character in the world's first ever novel, "The Tale of Genji." 

Picture him as a rich, pretty boy who cares a ton about his looks and gets along really well with all the ladies. He's also really great at poetry, which helps the lady situation.

meaningMnemonic:
The <radical>world</radical> radical and the <kanji>world</kanji> kanji are the same, but there is another really important meaning you need to know: <kanji>generation</kanji>. 

Think of it this way: the world is just made up of many different generations of people, plants, and animals all getting along together.
readingMnemonic: It's your job to protect the <kanji>world</kanji> and every <kanji>generation</kanji> with your special <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい). It was given to you to uphold correctness, and you'll pass it on to the next generation when it's time. Then they'll watch out for the world when you're gone.

meaningMnemonic:
The radical <radical>master</radical> and the kanji <kanji>master</kanji> have the same meanings!

This kanji also means <kanji>main</kanji> because the master is the main person of a dojo or household or business.
readingMnemonic: Do you remember back to the radical mnemonic, where the <kanji>master</kanji> is floating a water drop above his head? Imagine back to there. Once you do, the most amazing thing happens. The master sends the drop of water into a <reading>chu</reading>te (しゅ) and it goes careening through an intricate series of them.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> of <radical>alligators</radical>... she's no ordinary leader, that's for sure. She's not a politician, she's not a queen... She's one of the <kanji>Other</kanji> Ones, a group of strange, eccentric people who become leaders of animals.
readingMnemonic:
To remember the reading た, we use the word "taco":

Then there's the <kanji>other</kanji>s of the others. These are the <reading>ta</reading>co (た) worshipers. They're really strange.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>forehead</radical> of this <radical>beggar</radical> is on the <radical>ground</radical>. Except it's not exactly the ground, it's a giant <kanji>copy</kanji> machine, and he's making copies of his forehead!
readingMnemonic: This ground <kanji>copy</kanji> machine can only be the work of one person: a <reading>sha</reading>man (しゃ)!

meaningMnemonic: If someone tries to dig up <radical>dirt</radical> on your <radical>private</radical> life, tell them no thanks, that stuff is in the <kanji>past</kanji>. Some things should stay in the past. Not everyone needs to know everything about your life and your past.
readingMnemonic: To remember the reading きょ, we'll use "Little Kyoto." This will differentiate きょ from きょう, which is regular-sized Kyoto. Get it? You decide to bury all this private dirt from your <kanji>past</kanji> in Little <reading>Kyo</reading>to (きょ). That stuff is from when you were just a little <reading>子</reading> (こ) and you don't like to think about it.

meaningMnemonic: There's a <radical>mouth</radical> in the <radical>ground</radical> (in our case, it's a hatch), and inside is some kind of <radical>prison</radical> you can't quite get to. If you've ever seen Lost, this will sound familiar... on the hatch, there's actually a series of <kanji>number</kanji>s that you don't understand.
readingMnemonic:
"You want to know the <kanji>number</kanji> to the hatch?" yells an old voice from inside. You say "yes," and the voice gives you the numbers and you're in! You go down into the prison, where there is one prisoner: Grandpa <reading>ごう</reading>いち.

Note: For the ごう reading mnemonics, we use Grandpa ごういち because こういち is used for the こう reading. So just remember that ごう comes from Koichi's grandpa ごういち.

meaningMnemonic: The radical for <radical>center</radical> and the kanji for <kanji>center</kanji> are exactly the same.
readingMnemonic: Remember that guy from the 央 radical fastening his belt across the <kanji>center</kanji> of his body? This guy always secures his belt at the center — not slightly to the left, not slightly to the right, but dead center. His precision is so mind-blowing that everyone watching can't help but say, "<reading>Ohh</reading>!"(おう).

meaningMnemonic: If you want to <radical>dry</radical> your <radical>horns</radical> you need to lay them <kanji>flat</kanji>. If they aren't flat they'll always be a little damp somewhere and you'll never have a moment's <kanji>peace</kanji>.
readingMnemonic: Being <kanji>flat</kanji> isn't enough, so you cover the whole thing in <reading>hay</reading> (へい). Hay absorbs water, so you add a flat layer of it.

meaningMnemonic: Take your <radical>fingers</radical> to the <radical>street</radical> to <kanji>hit</kanji> some people! Street fingers are for curling into a fist. And that street fist wants to do some hitting!
readingMnemonic: As you <kanji>hit</kanji> one chump after another, suddenly your <reading>da</reading>d (だ) comes marching out of your house. Uh oh. Dad caught you hitting, which, honestly, isn't a very nice thing to do, so he has every right to stop you.

meaningMnemonic: Add a <radical>drop</radical> of something magical to <radical>water</radical> and suddenly it turns into big block of <kanji>ice</kanji>, just like that.
readingMnemonic: When you think of <kanji>ice</kanji>, I want you to think of someone frozen with ice. Do you know anyone named <reading>Corey</reading> (こおり)? Anyone famous named Corey, perhaps Corey Feldman? Imagine this Corey person frozen solid with ice.

meaningMnemonic: There's a <radical>cross</radical> inside your <radical>mouth</radical>, so the things you say are very pious. Everything you say, you <kanji>say humbly</kanji>.
readingMnemonic: As you're saying all these humble things, you're also <reading>mow</reading>ing (もう) your neighbor's lawn. Your neighbors love you so much, because you're so humble, and you mow their lawn for them.

meaningMnemonic: The <kanji>plate</kanji> kanji and the <radical>plate</radical> radical are exactly the same, like two matching pieces of china.
readingMnemonic: The person washing all the <kanji>dish</kanji>es was named <reading>Sarah</reading> (さら). She was named that because of her large head, which was shaped like Sarah Jessica Parker’s head (very big).

meaningMnemonic: What do you get if you lend a <radical>spirit</radical> your <radical>umbrella</radical>? <kanji>Thanks</kanji>, of course! Especially if that spirit is Totoro, the spirit of the forest.
readingMnemonic: So what did you receive as a <kanji>thanks</kanji>? You open it up, and there's a <reading>ray</reading> (れい) gun inside!

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> is next to the <radical>tree</radical>. What is he doing? He's leaning against it, <kanji>rest</kanji>ing.
readingMnemonic: As he <kanji>rest</kanji>s, he suddenly gets hungry and pulls a <reading>cu</reading>cumber (きゅう) off the tree and bites into it. A good rest isn't complete without some crunchy tree cucumbers.

meaningMnemonic: If you go down a <radical>slide</radical> and get <radical>dirt</radical> on your <radical>legs</radical>, it must have been the fault of the <kanji>previous</kanji> person, aka the person who went down the slide <kanji>ahead</kanji> of you. They should've cleaned off their dirty legs <kanji>before</kanji> they went down the slide, but they didn't, and now you've got dirt all over your legs too.
readingMnemonic: Then you realize: the <kanji>previous</kanji> person on the slide must've been a <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん). Centaurs track dirt everywhere 'cause they're always galloping around in the wilderness.

meaningMnemonic: What is the <radical>evening</radical> <radical>mouth</radical>? Imagine a mouth that's up in the night sky. It's just there, talking, and only comes out at night. It only appears in one area of the world. (What area would you choose? Imagine yourself going to that place to see the "evening mouth.") And it appears only one month out of the year (more on this in a second). The "evening mouth" is the most famous thing in the _____ area (this is the area you choose). He's so <kanji>famous</kanji> that everybody knows his <kanji>name</kanji> ("Evening Mouth").
readingMnemonic: So, when does “Evening Mouth” appear? Turns out, “Evening Mouth” only comes out to talk in <reading>May</reading> (めい). You know this because it’s just starting to be spring, and when you look around (you’re watching “Evening Mouth” again) you see some spring flowers come up.

meaningMnemonic: You have a <radical>gun</radical> and a <radical>cow</radical>, which must mean it's that time of the <kanji>year</kanji>! Every year on New Year's Eve, you get asked to shoot a cow, as per tradition. You really don't want to though, so you deliberately miss every time.
readingMnemonic: It seems everyone has caught on to the fact that you deliberately miss every <kanji>year</kanji>, so this year you make a <reading>nen</reading>doroid (ねん) decoy in the shape of a cow.

meaningMnemonic: When the <radical>sun</radical> is above the <radical>cross</radical>, it's time for some people to go church — which is always way too <kanji>early</kanji> on a Sunday, am I right? And if you want to get to church early, you need to be <kanji>fast</kanji>, otherwise you'll be too late.
readingMnemonic:
To remember the そう reading we use the word "soul."

When you're forced to get up <kanji>fast</kanji> and <kanji>early</kanji> you can feel it in your <reading>sou</reading>l (そう). You're so tired and it's so early, your soul cries out, "Please don't make me go this fast this early. I'm gonna leave your body if you keep this up!"

meaningMnemonic: These days <radical>energy</radical> is the greatest <radical>treasure</radical>. Not gold, money, or anything like that. Imagine finding some in a literal treasure chest. As soon as you open it, out bursts the <kanji>energy</kanji> in a kind of <kanji>spirit</kanji> ball.
readingMnemonic: As you reach inside the <kanji>energy</kanji> <kanji>spirit</kanji> ball, you feel something hard inside. It's a large <reading>key</reading> (き) that gives you access to another treasure.

meaningMnemonic: There is a <radical>leaf</radical> growing out of the <radical>sun</radical>! You fly out into space to check it out and when you get closer you see that it isn't just one leaf, it's a <kanji>hundred</kanji>! That makes sense, you wouldn't be able to see just one leaf on the sun from where you were on earth.
readingMnemonic: You fly closer, counting all one <kanji>hundred</kanji> leaves when you hear a strange sound. You take off your space helmet and hear, "GUH <reading>HYUCK</reading> (ひゃく)!" You know that laugh... it's Goofy! On top of the hundredth leaf is Goofy laughing and waving around. He must have built this leaf tower to get your attention.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>bamboo</radical> radical and the <kanji>bamboo</kanji> kanji are exactly the same!
readingMnemonic: Today is free <kanji>bamboo</kanji> day, so just <reading>take</reading> (たけ) all the bamboo you want.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>thread</radical> radical and <kanji>thread</kanji> are the same in looks as well as in meaning.
readingMnemonic: You have some <kanji>thread</kanji>... what's the first thing you do with it? You take it and put it in your mouth, and start sucking it down like spaghetti. You have decided to <reading>eat</reading> (いと) the thread.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>ear</radical> radical and <kanji>ear</kanji> kanji are the same in looks as well as meaning.
readingMnemonic: Put your hand to your <kanji>ear</kanji> and sing... "<reading>Mimi</reading>mimimimiiiii!" (みみ) and listen to your tone with your ear.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>insect</radical> radical and <kanji>insect</kanji> are the same in looks as well as and meaning.
readingMnemonic: It's an <kanji>insect</kanji>! Get it! You squish it with your foot - what sound does it make? A <reading>mush</reading> (むし) sound.

meaningMnemonic: Someone's taking <radical>tree</radical> <radical>measurements</radical>… Why are they taking measurements of trees? Because they're planning to cut them down to make their <kanji>village</kanji>.
readingMnemonic: They take all the wood back to where they're building their <kanji>village</kanji>, and once they construct the village center they paint a <reading>mura</reading>l (むら) on the side of it. The mural is of the forest where they got the trees from, to honor the village's origins.

meaningMnemonic: The radical for <radical>man</radical> and the kanji for <kanji>man</kanji> are exactly the same.
readingMnemonic: When you think of a <kanji>man</kanji>, think of <reading>dawn</reading> (だん), because that man has to get up at dawn to power his rice paddy every day.

meaningMnemonic: When you see both a <radical>rice paddy</radical> and a <radical>street</radical> in the same place, you have the makings of a whole <kanji>town</kanji>!
readingMnemonic: You go to visit this nice little <kanji>town</kanji>. But then, there she is... Every time you walk down the street, the street-woman Mrs. <reading>Chou</reading> (ちょう) arrives to ruin your day.

meaningMnemonic: All these <radical>flowers</radical> were planted by your <radical>leader</radical> with nothing but a <radical>spoon</radical>. Now the whole place is absolutely full of beautiful <kanji>flower</kanji>s. Flowers everywhere.
readingMnemonic: Secretly, the leader is actually planting all these <kanji>flower</kanji>s for <reading>Hannah</reading> (はな), a girl he has a huge crush on. Everyone thinks the leader's a nice, selfless guy, but really he's just trying to impress Hannah.

meaningMnemonic: The radical <radical>see</radical> and the kanji <kanji>see</kanji> are exactly the same!
readingMnemonic: You see something. It's walking towards you, but you need to see better. When the person comes close, you realize "That person is <reading>me</reading> (み)!"

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>shellfish</radical> radical and the <kanji>shellfish</kanji> kanji are the same! It can be just the <kanji>shell</kanji> part too.
readingMnemonic: To get some <kanji>shellfish</kanji>, all you have to do is hop in your <reading>kay</reading>ak (かい) and get some. Shellfish live in the water.

meaningMnemonic: The radical for <radical>red</radical> and the kanji for <kanji>red</kanji> are the same thing!
readingMnemonic: This kanji means <kanji>red</kanji>, <reading>AKA</reading>, also known as, あか.

meaningMnemonic:
The radical for <radical>foot</radical> and the kanji for <kanji>foot</kanji> are exactly the same.

That being said, there's also another meaning you should keep in mind, which is <kanji>sufficient</kanji>. If you think about it, one foot should be sufficient enough to walk around with if you hop.
readingMnemonic: What do you wear on your <kanji>foot</kanji>? You wear a <reading>sock</reading> (そく).

meaningMnemonic: The radical <radical>car</radical> and the kanji <kanji>car</kanji> are exactly the same.
readingMnemonic:
When we need to remember the reading しゃ, we use "shaman".

There's a <kanji>car</kanji>. It's the nicest car you've ever seen. Exquisite, beautiful. Wouldn't you be surprised to learn it belongs to a <reading>sha</reading>man (しゃ)? Well it does. This shaman charges a premium for his shaman services so he can afford fancy cars like this.

meaningMnemonic: The radical <radical>not</radical> and the kanji <kanji>not</kanji> are the same thing! That makes it easy... Not!
readingMnemonic:
To remember ふ we'll use the word "fool."

Think of those jokes, where someone says something, then ends with a loud "<kanji>NOTTTT</kanji>!" Someone is doing that to you, over and over. What is that person? That person is a <reading>foo</reading>l (ふ).

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> <radical>samurai</radical> didn't become leader by sitting around not taking action. He got to where he is by <kanji>doing</kanji>.
readingMnemonic: He was <kanji>doing</kanji> things with <reading>shee</reading>p (し). He is the leader of the sheep clan, and he was able to do more with sheep than anyone else. That's why he's the leader.

meaningMnemonic: You'll have to complete the line on the right side in your mind, but see the rectangle <radical>bed</radical> and the pillows? This is totally a bed. That missing line is where you pulled back the covers when you got up this morning.
Obviously, this looks just like the good ol' 3.14 something-something all people are supposed to learn about. It's the math symbol that has to do with circles... <radical>pi</radical>!
See the <radical>cliff</radical>? It's facing to the left. What do you think this cliff is jutting out over? Maybe an ocean, or a valley? Cliffs love to jut out over things like that.

The king is hovering a drop of water over his head. He's acquired the ability to manipulate water at will, making him the <radical>master</radical> of all water! If you think about how all life depends on water, that really makes him the master of everything…

You have a roof on top of what looks like a volcano. What's the roof covering? It's definitely covering the hole at the top. I guess someone thought if they built a roof on the hole, none of the lava could come out. But isn't there a hole in the chimney? Guess their theory had some holes in it… Oh no, now lava is coming through the hole. RUN!!! RUN!!!!!!!!
This radical looks like an <radical>umbrella</radical>, specifically just the handle part. A good old curvy umbrella handle, so you can hang that umbrella on your arm when it stops raining.

Remember the guy from the big radical? Here he's bending his arms at the elbow to fasten some kind of belt, right across the <radical>center</radical> of his body. It's one of those belts that just goes across your waist, which is actually perfect for such a big guy, since he really needs to secure his center (of gravity).

A rice paddy needs power, and that power comes from a <radical>man</radical>. Picture a rice paddy full of thick, wet mud. It's no easy field to work in, but this powerful man will bend over to rake and hoe tirelessly to make sure it thrives. What a man!

You have two lines with legs below them. And what is that bit above the legs? Well, it's where you came from — your <radical>origin</radical>. Yep, between two legs is where you originated, no matter how you look at it.

Have you ever used one of those triangular rulers? This looks just like one of those, because this is the <radical>measurement</radical> radical. Just think of all the angles you could measure with this thing!

This radical technically means person (like 人), but we're going to shift the meaning a little bit. It's kind of like the person radical, but it's taller and skinnier, standing above the others and raising its fist to lead them. That's why this is a <radical>leader</radical>, not a regular person.

Sure, this <radical>forehead</radical> is a bit square, but that's because it's the top part of the head (冂) radical. This is just the forehead part of that! You can even imagine this as the forehead of Frankenstein's monster, since his forehead was pretty square too.

Also, don't confuse this for a roof. You know this is a forehead and not a roof because this doesn't have a cute little chimney sticking out of the top. Foreheads definitely don't have chimneys.
See the mouth and long, fang-like teeth of this <radical>alligator</radical>, which is facing to the right? You wouldn't want to get your hand stuck in there, that's for sure.

This radical looks like a person with a long dress running behind them. That's because they're all dressed up to go to a <radical>ceremony</radical>. You usually have to wear a long dress (or something formal) if you're going to a big ceremony, right? So when you see this radical, think of all the different ceremonies you might have to go to in your life and what you'll need to wear.

This radical looks like some sort of <radical>spirit</radical>. It faces to the left with a big hood over its head, and creepy arms slanting downward at the sides. Can you picture it?

If not, just think of this as the katakana character for "ne." Ne for necromancer, bringing back spirits from the grave! OooooOOoooOoo!

There's a cross branching off of this stool. Since it's branching out, we'll just call this the <radical>branch</radical> radical. I guess the tree this stool was made of is coming back to life in the form of a branch.

This is the radical for <radical>energy</radical>. If you imagine a train under this radical, then the lines would be steam bursting out of it, flowing back from the front of the train (on the left) as it goes chugging along. Steam was the best way to generate energy back in the day, you know? That's why this is the radical for energy.

Use your imagination to draw a circle outside this shape. The grids from the radical start to look like longitude and latitude lines on a globe, don't they? That's why this is the <radical>world</radical>.

This is a few plates drying on a rack. They're stacked together in a line. Imagine reaching out and grabbing one of those plates for yourself. And you only need one, so let's call this radical <radical>plate</radical>.
See how tall the stalks are with little bushy things at the top? It kind of looks like <radical>bamboo</radical>, if you use your imagination.

In some poop you find something small. You lean in, carefully pull it out, and realize it's a <radical>thread</radical>. What's that doing there? Well, it came from a cat, and cats love to eat thread when you aren't looking. And floss and hoodie strings and mouse tails… But this time, just thread.

Doesn't this look like someone on their knees, holding their clasped hands out and begging for something (forgiveness, their life, a promotion, etc.)? That's why this radical is known as the <radical>beggar</radical>.
Can you see the <radical>horns</radical>? You could put these horns on anything. Donkeys, people, monkeys, and so on. That's exactly what we'll be doing when we learn kanji that have horns too.
On the ground there are a bunch of <radical>spikes</radical> coming out. You wouldn't want to step on those now, would you?

This is the <radical>ear</radical> radical, since it looks like an ear. As much as a radical can, anyway. Those two middle lines are the ridges that guide the sound into your ear, and the lines that stick out to the right are where the ear attaches to your head.

If you think about it, ears are just eyes that see sound. Whoa, dude… That explains the eye (目) and ear (耳) radical resemblance. Just remember this one is "ear" because ears stick out, just like the lines sticking out of the side of this radical.

This looks like the middle radical on top of a sideways leaf. What can you often find sitting on the middle of a leaf? An <radical>insect</radical>. Insects looove sitting right in the middle of leaves. That's why this is the insect radical.

See the little things sticking out of the ground? Those little things are pretty little <radical>flowers</radical>.

You could also see this as a little flower crown, especially since this radical almost always appears at the top of a kanji.

This eye has legs, since you have to let your eyes run over something in order to <radical>see</radical> it. That's why those eyes need legs — to see. They're wandering eyes, they want to see the world!!

A pile of dirt fell on you and now your legs and (scuba) fins are sticking out from under it, turning <radical>red</radical>. You know, because you've been stuck in the sun so long. Everything's all sunburned and red. Can scuba fins get a sunburn? Yeah, when it's red hot outside. That's why this means "red."

Doesn't this look like a top-down view of the frame of a <radical>car</radical>? There are the axles at the top and bottom, and the body in the middle between them. That's why this is the car radical.

This radical looks like a <radical>shellfish</radical>. What looks like the eye radical is its long spiral shell. The bottom part that looks like fins is its little legs poking out, helping the shellfish move around.

This is the <radical>foot</radical> radical. See the person doing a flying dragon kick to the right? What do they hit the target with? Obviously their foot. Imagine that foot coming straight at your face. At that point, your only hope is that it's clean. Nobody wants a foot to the face, especially not a filthy foot.

This looks like a regular arrow, but it's not. Let's call it the "not arrow." It can not move forward, because it's been stopped by some kind of line above it. Every arrow wants to fly, but this one is not going anywhere. Look at it, being all negated like that. Well, that's just what this radical does. It nots things.

The <radical>samurai</radical> radical looks a lot like the dirt radical. The difference is this one has a longer top horizontal line, which is for those broad warrior shoulders. The bottom line is also shorter, since it's the little cushion the samurai is sitting on. Samurai didn't just fight, you know. They sat on cushions and wrote poems and stuff too. This is a very sensitive samurai.

This is the radical for <radical>rice</radical>. Specifically raw rice, like the grain that grows out of the ground when rice is raw and fresh. When rice is particularly ripe, it'll kind of look like this at the top of the stalks. See the long leaves and the little rice tufts at the top? That's why this radical is "rice."

The leader (ｲ) radical has a little something extra on top of him — he's got a casual cap on because it's his day off. And what does he like to do on his day off? He likes to <radical>loiter</radical>. Look at him just loitering around. What a loiterer.

<reading>Do re</reading> (どれ) mi fa sol la ti... <vocabulary>which one</vocabulary> of these notes are you going to start singing from?

The question word どれ is used to ask <vocabulary>which one</vocabulary> out of three or more choices.

テレビ comes from the English word <vocabulary>television</vocabulary>. It’s been shortened, which is really common with loanwords in Japanese. The "vi" sound becomes ビ, too.

テレビ is sometimes written as "TV", but even then it’s usually pronounced テレビ.

Wanna play <reading>Un</reading>o (うん)? <vocabulary>Yep</vocabulary>! 'Course I do. I always wanna play Uno.

うん is a casual way to say <vocabulary>yes</vocabulary>, or <vocabulary>yeah</vocabulary>, so you’d generally use it with friends and family, and in other informal situations.

Want me to write a <reading>hai</reading>ku (はい)? <vocabulary>Yes</vocabulary>, of course!

はい is a formal word for "yes". You can use it to let someone know they’re right, or simply that you’re listening, as well as to respond if someone says your name.

ノート is a shortened form of <vocabulary>notebook</vocabulary>, with an extra "o" on the end since you can’t end a word with "t" in Japanese.

You can also use this to mean <vocabulary>notes</vocabulary>, in the context of taking down notes in a class.

I <reading>saw your Nara</reading> (さようなら), but I then had to say <vocabulary>goodbye</vocabulary>. It was just a short trip, but at least I saw your Nara.

さようなら is a formal way to say goodbye, so a good word to use with colleagues and teachers.

A quick tip before I say goodbye: さようなら often drops its middle う, becoming さよなら.

<reading>Con ban, wah!</reading> (こんばんは) cries your friend, just as you say <vocabulary>good evening</vocabulary>. There's a report on the evening news about a con ban (yes, that's a ban on conning people). Wah! Exciting stuff.

You can use こんばんは as a greeting from around 5pm or 6pm, in both formal and informal situations.　Watch out for that は on the end, which is pronounced わ here too.

Your <kanji>right</kanji> <kanji>hand</kanji> is your <vocabulary>right hand</vocabulary>! This word can also be used to mean <vocabulary>right arm</vocabulary>, and <vocabulary>right-hand side</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. This word, however, uses the kun'yomi readings. This is possibly because of 手, because body parts often use kun'yomi. You learned both readings with the individual words, so you should be able to read this.

meaningMnemonic: <vocabulary>Fourth day</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>day four</vocabulary>. No matter how you look at this combination of kanji, the meaning of the word makes a lot of sense!
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Counting days, however, is an exception (at least up to ten). You need to use the kun'yomi readings to read this word. You already know the kun'yomi reading for 四, so that's fine (the ん gets turned into a small っ, though). Think of it this way:

On the <vocabulary>fourth day</vocabulary> of being lost in the wilderness, you start yelling "<reading>Yo</reading>, <reading>ca</reading>r (よっか)!" out of desperation, never getting an answer. You keep marching on, occasionally yelling "Yo, car!", but no car ever comes to help you.

meaningMnemonic: Any number with a つ attached is a word that means "NUMBER Things." In this case, it's <vocabulary>four things</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can assume it uses the kun'yomi reading. The ん part of よん gets converted to a small っ though, so watch out for that.

meaningMnemonic: You have four and you have thousand. Multiply them together and you have <vocabulary>four thousand</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. That's true for the 千 part, but the 四 reading is kun'yomi. You should know how to read this word as long as you've been studying your kanji!

meaningMnemonic: What do you get when you have four tens? You get <vocabulary>forty</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. The only exception to this is 四, which uses the kun'yomi reading, since the on'yomi reading sounds too much like the word for "death." Luckily you know these reading already, so there's nothing new to learn!

meaningMnemonic: Your <kanji>left</kanji> <kanji>hand</kanji> is your <vocabulary>left hand</vocabulary>! This word can also be used to mean <vocabulary>left arm</vocabulary>, and <vocabulary>left-hand side</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. This word, however, uses the kun'yomi readings. This is possibly because of 手, because body parts often use kun'yomi. You learned both readings with the individual words, so you should be able to read this.

meaningMnemonic: This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means "life," making this word mean "<vocabulary>to live</vocabulary>."
readingMnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use a kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn the い reading with this kanji though, so here's a mnemonic to help you:

What does it mean <vocabulary>to live</vocabulary>? Life is pretty <reading>icky</reading> (いき ... the き comes from the hiragana part), but you have to take that ickiness and roll with it if you want to have a good life... if you want to live!

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic:
You pick up a <vocabulary>stone.</vocabulary> You throw it through a window. Huh. That was surprisingly <reading>easy</reading>.

Do it again. Throw the stone again. It's so easy, just do it.

meaningMnemonic: This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>fat</kanji> so the verb form of that is <vocabulary>to get fat</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You learned that reading with the kanji, so you should be good to go. But here's a mnemonic just in case:

It's easy <vocabulary>to get fat</vocabulary> if you're always in your <reading>futo</reading>n (ふと).

meaningMnemonic: The kanji <kanji>rest</kanji> has a little hiragana attached, but it's nothing that would suggest it's anything but a noun (it is!). It means the same thing as the kanji, <vocabulary>rest</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji with some hiragana, it should use the kun'yomi reading. When learning the kanji, you didn't learn that reading, so here's a mnemonic to help you with this word: 

So, do you want a <vocabulary>rest</vocabulary> from work or school? You should say "<reading>YAASS</reading> (やす)!"

meaningMnemonic: This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji 休 means <kanji>rest</kanji>, so what's the verb version of that? It's <vocabulary>to rest</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>to take time off</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn that reading with this kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you: 

You need <vocabulary>to rest</vocabulary>, right? Don't just answer with a "yes," this deserves a "<reading>YAASS</reading>" (やす)! YAAASSS let's RESSST!!!

meaningMnemonic:
A <kanji>stop</kanji> that's just a <kanji>rest</kanji> (aka not permanent) isn't a straight up stop. You're planning on continuing again. That's why this is a <vocabulary>pause</vocabulary>, <vocabulary>suspension</vocabulary>, or <vocabulary>hiatus</vocabulary>.

This is a fairly formal word, and it's often used when an activity or a service is temporarily suspended. For example, you could use it to talk about a class being suspended, a machine suspending its operation, or a pop band going on a hiatus. Check out our common word combinations below for more example usages.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: This is a <kanji>rest</kanji> <kanji>day</kanji>, aka a <vocabulary>day off</vocabulary>. Depending on where you're from, you might also call this a <vocabulary>holiday</vocabulary>. It's basically any day you have off from work or school.
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. You know the on'yomi for 休, but since 日 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use:

On every <vocabulary>day off</vocabulary>, you eat not one, but two 休-cumbers. <reading>Gee, two</reading> (じつ)? Take it easy!

meaningMnemonic:
The kanji means <kanji>previous</kanji>. This is an adverb version of that word. It's a little thing you can begin sentences with. You can't start your sentences with "previous" but you can start it with "<vocabulary>First of all</vocabulary>" or "<vocabulary>to start with</vocabulary>."

This is usually written without kanji though, so keep that in mind.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. In fact, the reading for this word is very rare, and kind of an exception. We'll use a mnemonic that uses both the kanji part and the hiragana attachment part. 

<vocabulary>First of all</vocabulary> and <vocabulary>to start with</vocabulary>, you have to make it through this <reading>maze</reading> (まず).

meaningMnemonic:
Everything that happened to you <kanji>previous</kanji>ly is just the <vocabulary>tip</vocabulary> of the iceberg, and the important stuff is <vocabulary>ahead</vocabulary> of you, in your <vocabulary>future</vocabulary>. So make sure you put that stuff <vocabulary>first</vocabulary>!

The original meaning of this word is the tip of something, like a pen or a finger. From that meaning, it's expanded into all sorts of meanings, like ahead, first, and future, depending on the context and where it is in the sentence. It can also mean "previous" in formal contexts (and we'll accept that meaning too, but we're not teaching it here because it's more advanced).
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it uses the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn that reading with the kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you:

Just <vocabulary>ahead</vocabulary> of you are the <vocabulary>tip</vocabulary>s of a <reading>saw</reading> and a <reading>key</reading>(さき). You have no idea what they're doing there, but both saws and keys have very sharp tips, so watch out you don't hurt yourself. Imagine yourself edging carefully past those saw and key tips.

You can differentiate this from 先ず (まず) because it doesn't have any hiragana on the end.

meaningMnemonic: The <kanji>previous</kanji> <kanji>previous</kanji> <kanji>month</kanji> (remember that 々 just repeats the kanji before it) is the <vocabulary>month before last</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well. Just remember — this is NOT a specific month name, so it's going to use the げつ reading of 月.

meaningMnemonic: The <kanji>previous</kanji> <kanji>month</kanji> is <vocabulary>last month</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. 月 has two on'yomi readings, but since this is NOT a specific month name, it's going to use the げつ reading. Here's a mnemonic to help you remember that:

Since <vocabulary>last month</vocabulary>, your <reading>cen</reading>taur <reading>gets</reading> (せんげつ) up every night and comes to sleep in your bed. He's been having really bad nightmares since last month, but… why? Did something traumatic happen to your centaur last month? Try to remember.

meaningMnemonic: The <kanji>previous</kanji> <kanji>day</kanji> isn't necessarily yesterday. It can be any recent day, which is why this word means <vocabulary>the other day</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Since 日 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use:

<vocabulary>The other day</vocabulary>, you saw two <reading>cen</reading>taurs! <reading>Gee, two</reading> (せんじつ) of them?! You're lucky you're still alive!

meaningMnemonic:
<kanji>Previous</kanji> <kanji>life</kanji>... So we're talking about someone who has a previous life, or maybe previous life experience? When you have previous life experience, you can teach others about it, making you qualified to be some kind of <vocabulary>teacher</vocabulary>.

先生 is often used for school teachers, but it's also used to show respect to doctors, lawyers, authors, and other respected experts. It's used as a standalone word or a name ender like トム先生.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: The <kanji>name</kanji> of this <kanji>person</kanji> is well known, but why? It's because they're an <vocabulary>expert</vocabulary> in their field, or a <vocabulary>master</vocabulary> of something. If you're an expert or a master of something, people are going to know your name — even if you're just an expert at being an idiot.
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Since 人 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use:

Did you know that every <vocabulary>expert</vocabulary> has their own pair of <reading>May</reading> <reading>jean</reading>s (めいじん)? A true <vocabulary>master</vocabulary> of anything has a specific pair of jeans they wear only in the month of May. That's what makes them so good at what they do.

meaningMnemonic: A <kanji>name</kanji> written in <kanji>letter</kanji>s is the <vocabulary>surname</vocabulary>. Back in the day your surname was the important name (it's your family name after all, and family was king back in the day). This is the name you'd write out in kanji... aka in letters. So, that's why this combination of kanji means "surname."
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. 名 uses an alternate on'yomi reading here that you haven't learned yet, so here's a mnemonic to help you out:

Imagine you and your significant other are about to get hitched, and you're trying to decide your future <vocabulary>surname</vocabulary>. "Let's just combine 'me' and 'you' and go with <reading>Myou</reading> (みょう)," you say. Yes, from now on, you two will share the surname Myou.

meaningMnemonic: A <kanji>writing</kanji> <kanji>letter</kanji> is simpler than it sounds. It's really just a <vocabulary>letter</vocabulary>, right? You're reading 文字 right now, in fact!
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. The reading of 文 is a bit of an exception here. It uses a shortened version of the on'yomi もん, which is も. Here's a mnemonic to remember this:

You know all the <vocabulary>letters of the alphabet</vocabulary> only because of <reading>Mo</reading>e (も). If it wasn't for your buddy Moe teaching you all the letters, you probably wouldn't even be able to read today. Thanks Moe!

meaningMnemonic:
You learned 文字 means "letter (of the alphabet)" or "character." And what does the kanji character for <kanji>one</kanji> look like in Japanese? A <vocabulary>straight line</vocabulary>!

Think of this word as meaning "the 一 character." In Japanese, it's most often used to describe the straight line a person's lips make when they're pressed together in concentration, frustration, or silence.
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Since 文 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use:

Anyone who goes around with his mouth in a <vocabulary>straight line</vocabulary> is probably a <reading>mon</reading>ster (もん). How could anyone walk around with such an uninviting expression on their face? Basically, anyone who isn't smiling constantly is a monster, as far you're concerned. And that includes those monsters with 一文字-shaped mouths.

meanings: The Year Two Thousand And Eleven
readings: にせんじゅういちねん
meaningMnemonic: It's the 2011 <kanji>year</kanji>. I think the meaning is pretty obvious. This just means <vocabulary>year 2011</vocabulary> or simply <vocabulary>2011</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: The reading is a little trickier. You start with two thousand (二千／にせん), then you need to add eleven to it (十一／じゅういち). Then, you simply add the reading for 年 which you already know. You can assume it's the on'yomi reading because it's attached to something else, like a jukugo. From there, you get the entire reading.

meanings: By The End Of The Year
By The End Of This Year
Before The End Of The Year
Before The End Of This Year
meaningMnemonic:
<kanji>Inside</kanji> the <kanji>year</kanji> means <vocabulary>by the end of the year</vocabulary>, or <vocabulary>within the year</vocabulary>.

年内 is used to talk about things happening before the current year is over. It can also be used to talk about one's plans for <vocabulary>the rest of the year</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: The <kanji>year</kanji> <kanji>middle</kanji> sounds like it should be something like "the middle of the year," but it's not. Think of something that happens in the middle of the year and then all other parts of the year. It's just referring to everything within this year, which is how we get <vocabulary>year round</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings for the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well. 中 does have a strange reading, though, which is じゅう. This is basically just ちゅう changing to ぢゅう, but since ぢ is very rare it just switches to じ and you end up with じゅう. That's called rendaku.

meaningMnemonic: You'd maybe think this just means <kanji>one</kanji> <kanji>year</kanji> of <kanji>life</kanji>, but it actually means <vocabulary>first year student</vocabulary>. A second year student is a 二年生, and so on. Just think about how crazy the Japanese are about school. You can see why school is life, and not life itself, making this vocab word "first year student."
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic:
Someone of <kanji>few</kanji> <kanji>year</kanji>s is someone who's young. Since we already have 少女 for girls, 少年 means <vocabulary>boy</vocabulary>.

少年 is a bit of a formal term that often refers to a <vocabulary>young boy</vocabulary>, usually between childhood and adolescence, or sometimes to a <vocabulary>youth</vocabulary> in general.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: The <kanji>past</kanji> <kanji>year</kanji> is just <vocabulary>last year</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: This is a single kanji with an い on the end, meaning you know it's probably an adjective. What's the adjective form of <kanji>early</kanji>? It's also <vocabulary>early</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
The reading for this word is the kun'yomi reading, which you did not learn when you learned the kanji. You have to remember the はや portion to be able to read this word. In order to do that, here's a mnemonic: 

You're <kanji>fast</kanji>! You're <kanji>early</kanji>! Your chop comes down onto your opponent's neck so quickly! As you chop him, you yell "<reading>HAYA</reading>!" (はや)

meaningMnemonic: Essentially you have the kanji 早 twice, because 々 is the repeater kanji. When you do something doubly <kanji>early</kanji>, there must be some kind of urgency to it, right? So you need to take action <vocabulary>as soon as</vocabulary> you finish whatever it is you're doing.
readingMnemonic:
This word has two possible readings, each with a different nuance, so here are some mnemonics to remember them by:

そうそう is for when you do something quickly or immediately after something else, like 早々にホテルを出る (to leave a hotel quickly) or オープン早々 (as soon as a shop opens). You can remember this because <vocabulary>as soon as</vocabulary> you get to work, you do everything really <vocabulary>quickly</vocabulary>, so your work performance is just <reading>so-so</reading> (そうそう).

はやばや is for when you do something earlier than usual, like going to bed or closing up shop before the usual time. It often appears as 早々と. You can remember this because when you want to close up shop <vocabulary>early</vocabulary>, you say "<reading>Hiya… bye-ya</reading>!" (はやばや) to all the customers who come in. Saying both hi and bye when they walk in is the best way to hint that you're trying close early. Hiya, bye-ya! Yeah, they get the hint.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: The reading is the same as the kanji that makes up this word. Lucky you!

meaningMnemonic: You <kanji>enter</kanji> into the <kanji>energy</kanji>. When entering into (positive) energy, you are going <vocabulary>to take a liking</vocabulary> to something and <vocabulary>to be pleased</vocabulary> and <vocabulary>to be happy</vocabulary> with it. Just imagine yourself entering into some happy energy when you take a liking to something.
readingMnemonic: The 気 portion is pretty straight forward. The 入る reading is the same as the one you learned with the vocabulary 入り口. Just imagine you're really, really, reeeally pleased with your lovely new pet <reading>ea</reading>gle (い)! You're so satisfied and happy with it. Feel the good energy your eagle is bringing you.

meaningMnemonic:
If you do something in <kanji>one</kanji> burst of <kanji>energy</kanji>, you are doing it <vocabulary>in one go</vocabulary>. Whether you're reading an entire book in a single sitting or chugging a whole beer in a single gulp, this thing you're doing is happening all <vocabulary>at once</vocabulary> — in one fell swoop.

一気に refers to one continuous push with no breaks. The image is momentum: doing something in a single burst, rather than something happening instantaneously.
readingMnemonic: The reading is the same as the ones you learned with the individual kanji, though いち from 一 is shortened to いっ.

meaningMnemonic:
The <kanji>energy</kanji> that's a <kanji>part</kanji> of you at any given time is reflected in your <vocabulary>mood</vocabulary>, aka your <vocabulary>feeling</vocabulary>. What kind of energy is part of you right now? Good mood energy, or bad feeling energy? 

気分 refers to someone's mood or emotional state, and sometimes to how they feel physically. You'll hear it when people describe how they're doing in the moment, like 気分がいい (I feel good), 気分がわるい (I feel unwell), or 気分がのらない (I'm not in the mood).
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: If a <kanji>person</kanji> has a lot of <kanji>energy</kanji> then they're very charismatic and fun! What's another feature of charismatic / fun people? They are most likely <vocabulary>popular</vocabulary> as well.
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Since 人 and 気 both have two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you:

You're so <vocabulary>popular</vocabulary> that you've been given the <reading>Nin</reading>tendo <reading>key</reading> (にんき). That's right, Nintendo gave you the master key to their entire office. Just like that.

This is a lot of responsibility even for your levels of popularity, so be careful not to lose this key!

meaningMnemonic: What is the <kanji>origin</kanji> of your <kanji>energy</kanji>? It is your <vocabulary>health</vocabulary>. This word also means <vocabulary>energy</vocabulary>, and when it's used as an adjective it means <vocabulary>healthy</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>energetic</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: What is the <kanji>heaven</kanji> <kanji>energy</kanji>? If you think about it, that's all the stuff that falls on us from the sky. Lightning, rain, snow, hail... What is all of that? It's the <vocabulary>weather</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: What is <kanji>book</kanji> <kanji>energy</kanji>? It is energy you put into reading - it is your studies! In order to have book energy, though, you have to be very <vocabulary>serious</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: You have a <kanji>flat</kanji> <kanji>energy</kanji>. What kind of energy would be a flat one? Probably a <vocabulary>cool</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>calm</vocabulary> one. You'd also tell people about this flat energy by saying you are <vocabulary>all right</vocabulary> or just <vocabulary>okay</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: You have only <kanji>one</kanji> <kanji>book</kanji> <kanji>energy</kanji>. No other books cross your mind. Only this one book. Why? Because you have such a <vocabulary>one-track mind</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: The reading is based off the on'yomi readings for everything, though every reading is slightly altered. いち getting shortened to いっ, ほん changing to ぽん, and き gets changed to ぎ. They're based off the readings you know already, though, so making this shift shouldn't be too hard... that is, unless you have an 一本気, ammiright?

meaningMnemonic: <kanji>one hundred</kanji> <kanji>ten thousand</kanji>s is how much? Multiply the two together and you get a <vocabulary>million</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: When you have <kanji>four</kanji> <kanji>hundred</kanji>s, you have <vocabulary>four hundred</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This word has number four in it, and uses the kun'yomi reading for that kanji (よん). The reading for 百 though, is the on'yomi reading (ひゃく). This is pretty normal when it comes to words containing four in Japanese. You know both of these readings already. Hooray!

meaningMnemonic: When you have <kanji>five</kanji> <kanji>hundred</kanji>s you also have <vocabulary>five hundred</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This word uses the on'yomi readings for both kanji, so you should be able to read it already!

meaningMnemonic: When you have <kanji>two</kanji> <kanji>hundred</kanji>s, you have <vocabulary>two hundred</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic:
What is <kanji>bamboo</kanji>’s <kanji>child</kanji>? That would be a baby bamboo. Because "baby bamboo" doesn't really make sense, we'll instead call this word <vocabulary>bamboo shoot</vocabulary>s. 

When this is written as 竹の子, the bamboo shoots are probably still growing in the ground. If they’re picked and cooked and ready to eat, the pronunciation is the same, but it’s usually written in other forms, such as タケノコ.
readingMnemonic: If you know the readings of the kanji for 竹 and the vocabulary for 子 you should be able to read this word as well.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well!
readingMnemonic: Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading which is also the reading you associated with the kanji, earlier.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: Since this word is made up of a single kanji it should use the kun'yomi reading (which is the reading you learned with the kanji).

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading which is the reading you learned with the kanji. So you should be able to read this word already.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well. This word is actually more general than the English words <vocabulary>insect</vocabulary> and <vocabulary>bug</vocabulary>, though. It covers things like worms and spiders, too, so in that sense it's more like <vocabulary>creepy-crawly</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading, which is also the reading you learned when you learned this kanji!

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading. This is the reading you learned already with the kanji. Huzzah!

meaningMnemonic: You have a <kanji>village</kanji> <kanji>person</kanji>. That isn't someone dancing while wearing construction worker clothes, that's a <vocabulary>villager</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This word uses double kun'yomi for its reading, probably because it's like two separate words (village and person) put together into one big word. You should know these two kun'yomi readings (it's the main reading for 村 that you learned and you should know 人 by now), though you will need to look out for the rendaku'd びと reading.

meaningMnemonic:
The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.

Just be aware that this word used alone can sound a bit rude. You'll learn a more neutral option very soon.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it uses the kun'yomi reading. Here's a mnemonic to help you to remember it: 

There is a <vocabulary>man</vocabulary>. Think of the manliest man you can. He works in an auto shop. As a child, he was an <reading>auto 子</reading> (おとこ), working at the auto shop from an early age. This turned him into a man very quickly. 

Like other mnemonics that aren't lined up quite perfectly, your goal here is to get close enough to remember the true reading. Step, step, step, jump!

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading. When learning the kanji, you didn't learn that reading, so here's a mnemonic to help you with this word: 

You walk into this town. You hate it (because it's vocab, and who doesn't hate vocab?). So what do you do? You pull out a <reading>match</reading> (まち) and burn this town to the ground. Whoa, you some kind of psycho?

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading. This is the reading you learned when you learned the kanji, so you're good to go. Don't forget to stop and smell the roses on the way, though.

meaningMnemonic: The <kanji>flower</kanji> <kanji>fire</kanji>... what looks like flowers on fire? That would be <vocabulary>fireworks</vocabulary>. Totally makes sense, right?
readingMnemonic: The readings for the kanji that make this word are the kun'yomi readings, where fire's ひ changes to び. You already learned the kun'yomi reading for 花 when you learned the kanji, so it's only a matter of remembering to use the kun'yomi for 火. If you do, you should be able to read this word.

meaningMnemonic:
When you have <kanji>flower</kanji>s that you can <kanji>see</kanji>, you are viewing flowers. But not just any flowers. What flowers do Japanese people love viewing? That's right, this word refers to <vocabulary>viewing cherry blossoms</vocabulary>.

The focus of 花見 is on the act of appreciating blossoming trees, often accompanied by outdoor gatherings, picnics, or parties. Although cherry blossoms are the most commonly associated flowers, the term can encompass other flower-viewing experiences as well.
readingMnemonic: The reading for this vocab is all kun'yomi, but you learned the kun'yomi readings of both these when you learned their kanji. So no worries there. You can spend the extra time viewing the cherry blossoms.

meanings: To Take A Look At Something
meaningMnemonic:
This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>see</kanji> so the vocab here means <vocabulary>to see</vocabulary>, <vocabulary>to look</vocabulary>, or <vocabulary>to watch</vocabulary>.
