Seeing, looking, watching — 見る doesn't care. No need to squint over word choice here.
readingMnemonic: The reading for this word is the same as the reading you learned with the kanji (kun'yomi) so you should be able to read this.

meaningMnemonic: This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. It's related to the meaning of the kanji 見 "see," but this one is about having someone else see something. In other words, it means <vocabulary>to show</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: The reading for this word is the same as the one that you learned with the kanji, so you should be able to read it.

meaningMnemonic: Your <kanji>see</kanji>ing <kanji>way</kanji> is your <vocabulary>way of seeing</vocabulary> something. This is like when someone has a dumb opinion and you're like, "oh, okay, so that's one way of seeing it..."
readingMnemonic:
This uses the kun'yomi readings from the vocab words 見る and 方. The 方 reading here (かた) wasn't the main one you learned though, so here's a mnemonic to help it stick:

You've been told your <vocabulary>way of seeing</vocabulary> things is a little narrow, so in order to gain a broader <vocabulary>perspective</vocabulary>, you decide to <reading>cata</reading>pult (かた) yourself in the air. That'll certainly give you an interesting <vocabulary>viewpoint</vocabulary>.

meaningMnemonic:
When you <kanji>moon</kanji> <kanji>see</kanji> something, you're doing a <vocabulary>moon viewing</vocabulary>.

月見 is a traditional autumn event in East Asia where people admire the full moon and enjoy seasonal treats, which in Japan include moon-shaped dumplings, sweet potatoes, and chestnuts. 月見 also often pops up in the names of foods that resemble the moon in some way, like a 月見バーガー — a hamburger whose fried egg topping is playfully compared to a full Harvest Moon.
readingMnemonic: This word uses kun'yomi readings for both kanji. You learned the つき reading with the 月 vocab word, and the み reading with the kanji 見 (and with some of its vocab, like 見る). You probably won't have trouble remembering the み part, so here's a mnemonic just for the つき bit: Every year when it's moon viewing time, you get out two keys (つき). The first key lets you out onto the veranda, where you do the actual moon viewing, and the second key unlocks your pantry full of moon-viewing snacks. These two keys are key to enjoying moon viewing at your house.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: The reading for this word is the same as the one you learned with the kanji, meaning you should be able to read this already.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: This has the same reading as the kanji you learned, meaning you know the reading!

meaningMnemonic: ちゃん is the honorific you put on a child's name. So, who is this mysterious child you're calling <kanji>red</kanji>? When a baby is born, what color are they? You got it, they're all red. And, because you want to be nice and cute you add ちゃん to that. That's why あかちゃん is a <vocabulary>baby</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: If you know the reading of the kanji <kanji>赤</kanji> you should know the reading of this too.

meaningMnemonic: This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>leg</kanji> or <kanji>sufficient</kanji>, and we're going with the "sufficient" meaning for this word! This word means <vocabulary>to be enough</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn that reading with this kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you to remember the た portion. Whenever we need to remember the た we use the word "taco." 

What do you have enough of? What is sufficient? Turns out it's <reading>ta</reading>cos (た). Your bunker is filled with them and it's going <vocabulary>to be enough</vocabulary> to last the winter.

meaningMnemonic:
The kanji and the word are almost exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well. A <kanji>foot</kanji> is a <vocabulary>foot</vocabulary>, you know?

Although this word is more commonly used for feet, sometimes it can also refer to <vocabulary>leg</vocabulary>s. Now you've got a leg up in understanding 足!
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading. When learning the kanji, you didn't learn that reading, so here's a mnemonic to help you with this word: 

If you remember in the radical/kanji mnemonic, you kick someone with your foot. But what happens after you kick them? Turns out they were made of fire, and you just burned your foot off, turning it to <reading>ash</reading>es (あし). Imagine this happening.

meaningMnemonic:
You have something that's <kanji>not</kanji> <kanji>sufficient</kanji>. When something isn't sufficient, you have an <vocabulary>insufficiency</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>shortage</vocabulary>.

This word is more commonly used in formal contexts. In more casual, everyday conversations, 足りない is often preferred to express something being insufficient.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading. When learning the kanji, you didn't learn that reading, so here's a mnemonic to help you with this word: 

You visit your mom who lives inside of a <kanji>car</kanji>. It's sad, and you feel bad. The first thing that pops out of your mouth is: "<vocabulary>Cool Room, Ma</vocabulary> (くるま) because you don't know what else to say about your ma's living situation. 

Be sure to sort of cut off the last letters of the first two words... something like "coo' roo' ma!" Sad thing is, there is only one room in this car your mom calls home.

meaningMnemonic: The <kanji>car</kanji> <kanji>inside</kanji> is simply that, <vocabulary>inside the car</vocabulary>. This can be used for other means of transport too, so it can also mean <vocabulary>inside the train</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>inside the bus</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: The <kanji>two</kanji> <kanji>generation</kanji> is the <vocabulary>second generation</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: The <kanji>three</kanji> <kanji>generation</kanji> is the <vocabulary>third generation</vocabulary>. Depending on the context, you can sometimes use this alone to say you're third generation Japanese. Usually, though, you'll want to specify whether you're third generation Japanese or another nationality.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: 主 means <kanji>main</kanji> so when you add に it becomes the adverb <vocabulary>mainly</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn that reading with this kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you: 

You <vocabulary>mainly</vocabulary> spend your time talking to your master Moe. But you mainly address him like, "<reading>Oh Moe</reading> The Great!" (おも). That's because he's above you. Imagine yourself saying "OH MOE THE GREAT!" as you walk into his room, bowing low.

meaningMnemonic:
The <kanji>master</kanji> <kanji>person</kanji> isn't just a master. You have to think back to when they were making these kanji. Who was the master? It was the <vocabulary>head of household</vocabulary>. This head of household probably told servants what to do and was all bossy and stuff. He was the master person. 

Also, this often means <vocabulary>husband</vocabulary>. Because, I guess the head of household back in the day was the husband. Those sexist ancient people!
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Since 人 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use:

In Japan, tradition says the head of a household must slide down a <reading>chu</reading>te in only <reading>jean</reading>s (しゅじん). Whenever you need to speak to the head of the household, they'll come sliding down a chute wearing nothing but jeans. What a sight!

meaningMnemonic: The <kanji>doing</kanji> <kanji>way</kanji> is basically a "way of doing" something. More simply put, this is the <vocabulary>method</vocabulary>. "What's your way of doing this?" No no no. "What's your METHOD." There we go.
readingMnemonic:
This word uses the on'yomi reading for 仕 (no surprises there), but the kun'yomi reading for 方, so watch out for that! You're probably more familiar with the on'yomi for 方, so here's a mnemonic to help with the kun'yomi:

Your <vocabulary>method</vocabulary> of <reading>cata</reading>pulting (かた) anything you don't like into the air is definitely the best <vocabulary>way of doing</vocabulary> things.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading. When learning the kanji, you didn't learn that reading, so here's a mnemonic to help you with this word: 

You need to go for a drive to the public gardens, but you aren't going to get your normal car, you're going to get your <kanji>other</kanji> car. Your <reading>hoe car</reading> (ほか)! It's a car made entirely out of garden hoes. You can drive it and hoe with it at the same time! Your other car can't do that!

meaningMnemonic:
When you have an <kanji>other</kanji> <kanji>person</kanji>, you really have <vocabulary>another person</vocabulary>.

他人 has a sense of unfamiliarity or distance, and suggests that the person isn’t well-known, or is different from you or a specific group of people. It doesn't usually mean "another person" in the sense of an additional person, or another person distinct from the one you’re talking about.
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Since 人 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use:

<vocabulary>Another person</vocabulary> just stole your <reading>ta</reading>co and your <reading>Nin</reading>tendo (たにん). This complete <vocabulary>stranger</vocabulary> just walked up and took your taco and your Nintendo console straight out of your hands. Yoink.

meanings: To Take A Picture of Something
meaningMnemonic: This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>copy</kanji> so you know this means <vocabulary>to copy</vocabulary>. You know this word means "to copy" because it ends with す. You're copying the documents you need to <reading>sue</reading> (す) someone who screwed you over.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn that reading with this kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you: 

You have to remember the うつ part. To remember that, think of yourself as a beatboxer. You're saying <reading>うつ</reading>うつうつ over and over again. Thing is, the other beatboxer is going <vocabulary>to copy</vocabulary> everything you do. That's why you're suing him.

meaningMnemonic:
The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.

This word is generally added to the end of a number, as in 三号 ("number 3"). It can also go together with other words to give things like train car numbers, as in ２号車 ("car 2"), and room numbers. When used on its own, it indicates the edition number of magazines and the like.
readingMnemonic: This word is made up of a single kanji all alone, but it uses the on'yomi reading. This is probably because you'll usually see this word attached to other things, like numbers. This shows the edition or number of an item. So, even though it's alone, it's usually used like jukugo, which is why the reading for this one is the same as the reading you learned when you learned the kanji.

meaningMnemonic: The <kanji>middle</kanji> of the <kanji>central</kanji>... that is really, really in the <vocabulary>center</vocabulary> if you ask me (and is very <vocabulary>central</vocabulary> as well). This is one of those "overdid it on the meaning" words.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same with a little hiragana sticking out. Because the hiragana doesn't really indicate what it is, it's okay to assume that they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a kanji plus some hiragana attached, it should use the kun'yomi reading. When learning the kanji, you didn't learn that reading, so here's a mnemonic to help you with this word: 

Something is very, very <vocabulary>flat</vocabulary>. Perhaps someone who doesn't eat much, like a model? Which model? Let's imagine it's <reading>Tyra</reading> (たいら) Banks.

meaningMnemonic: The <kanji>flat</kanji> <kanji>day</kanji>s are boring. They're the same. One day after another. That's definitely not your exciting weekends... that's your boring, flat <vocabulary>weekdays</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Since 日 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use:

<vocabulary>Weekdays</vocabulary> are flat, but you do at least earn two entire bales of <reading>hay</reading>. <reading>Gee, two</reading> (へいじつ) of them! What a hard worker you are.

meaningMnemonic:
This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>hit</kanji> so the vocab is <vocabulary>to hit something</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>to strike something</vocabulary>. It can also mean <vocabulary>to type</vocabulary>.

You can use this word to describe bumping a part of your body on something, hitting a ball or a nail, or typing on a keyboard. It can also describe hitting a person, but that use is less common and sounds a bit literary.
readingMnemonic:
This word uses the kun'yomi reading, which you haven't learned yet, but never fear! A mnemonic is here:

If you’re planning <vocabulary>to hit</vocabulary> an <reading>u</reading>ni (う), you’re going to need a special bat or hammer. I don’t know why you’d want to hit a sea urchin, but make sure you have the right equipment if you do, because those things are spiky!

meaningMnemonic:
<kanji>One</kanji> <kanji>hit</kanji> is simply… <vocabulary>one hit</vocabulary>!

一打 refers to a single impactful strike. It's often a hit in ball sports, but can also be a punch in boxing or a button tap in video games.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word that uses the on'yomi readings of the kanji. You should be able to read this on your own.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading which is what you learned with the kanji.

meaningMnemonic: This is かき <kanji>ice</kanji>, which is <vocabulary>shaved ice</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: The reading for 氷 is the same as the one you learned (こおり) except it gets converted to ごおり because it comes after かき, so it's rendaku'd.

meaningMnemonic:
This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>say humbly</kanji>, and this is just the verb form, <vocabulary>to say humbly</vocabulary>. You can also call this <vocabulary>to say</vocabulary>, just remember that it's humble!

This is a very humble way to say "to say" so it's used in especially formal situations, and only to talk about yourself or your associates. Because of its formality, it's almost always in the ます form, which is 申します.
readingMnemonic: The reading for this kanji is the kun'yomi reading, but that's the reading you learned with the kanji. High Fives.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading which is also the reading you learned when you learned the kanji.

meaningMnemonic: You have a <kanji>small</kanji> <kanji>plate</kanji>. Wait, is that it? Yep, that's it.
readingMnemonic: さら uses the same reading as the one you learned with the kanji, except it's ざら this time because of the rendaku. 小 uses an alternate kun'yomi reading. You can remember it by thinking "What are small? Kids (子) are small. Bwahaha."

meaningMnemonic: The meaning of this word is the same as the kanji, except it has an お before it, which just makes the thing you're saying elevate the other person more. Basically, it's more polite and awesome.
readingMnemonic: The reading is the same as the one you learned with the kanji.

meaningMnemonic:
When what you do is morally or legally <kanji>not</kanji> <kanji>correct</kanji>, you are engaging in <vocabulary>misconduct</vocabulary>.

不正 is a formal word used for dishonest actions. It can mean outright <vocabulary>fraudulent</vocabulary> acts — misusing public funds or accessing someone's bank account without their permission — or it can simply refer to intentional <vocabulary>rule-breaking</vocabulary>, like cheating on an exam or in a sports game.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: This is a single kanji plus the hiragana るい. Words that end in い are usually adjectives, and this is no exception. What's the adjective form of <kanji>bright</kanji>? It's also <vocabulary>bright</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
This word has a similar reading to 明かり, but here the kanji is read as あか instead of just あ. Here's a mnemonic to help you remember this:

What's a <vocabulary>bright</vocabulary> color? <reading>赤</reading> (あか), aka "red," obviously. Think of a really bright あか color when you see 明るい.

meaningMnemonic: You have a child cow. What do you think a child cow is? Probably a <vocabulary>calf</vocabulary> or a <vocabulary>baby cow</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: Since you should know the readings of the words 子 and 牛 from the previous level, you should be able to read this word too even though it's using the kun'yomi readings for the kanji.

meaningMnemonic: This is a pretty old word, and in the olden days, everything was <kanji>cut</kanji> by <kanji>hand</kanji>, including <vocabulary>postage stamp</vocabulary>s. Having to cut thousands of <vocabulary>stamp</vocabulary>s each day by hand sounds incredibly laborious, so maybe that's why this word came to be associated specifically with stamps.
readingMnemonic: You'd think that the reading for this would consist of the on'yomi readings, but human body parts (hand) do funny things to words. In this case, it's the kun'yomi readings of both the kanji. You should know the kun'yomi reading for <kanji>切</kanji> and <kanji>手</kanji> from the previous levels, meaning you should be able to remember the reading for this word fairly easily. Watch out for the small っ, though.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well. You can also use this word to talk about someone's <vocabulary>age</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading. When learning the kanji, you didn't learn that reading, so here's a mnemonic to help you with this word: 

This <vocabulary>year</vocabulary> you plan on <reading>tossi</reading>ng (とし) out all your old stuff as a New Year's resolution. You pronounce it "toshing" though, because it has more vigor that way.

meaningMnemonic: This vocabulary word is the same as the 'name' meaning of the kanji. If you can remember that, you'll have no trouble remembering this word as well.
readingMnemonic: Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it probably uses the kun'yomi reading. You did not learn this reading, so here's a mnemonic to help you to remember: Just think of the first part of the English word <vocabulary>name</vocabulary>. What is it? It's <reading>na</reading> (な), just like this word!

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: The reading you learned with the kanji is the same as the reading for this word. No <vocabulary>hole</vocabulary>s in your knowledge!

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: Your <vocabulary>treasure</vocabulary> has a <reading>tackler</reading> (たから) guarding it at all times. He tackles anyone who gets close. If someone wants your treasure, they have to go through the tackler!

meaningMnemonic: A <kanji>treasure</kanji> <kanji>stone</kanji> is a very valuable stone. What kind of stones are considered treasures? How about a <vocabulary>jewel</vocabulary>, or a <vocabulary>gem</vocabulary>?
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word that uses the on'yomi readings of the kanji. You should be able to read this on your own.

meaningMnemonic: This is a single kanji with an い on the end, meaning you know it's probably an adjective. What's the adjective form of <kanji>red</kanji>? Why, it's <vocabulary>red</vocabulary>!
readingMnemonic: This has the same reading as the kanji you learned, meaning you know the reading!

meaningMnemonic: This is a single kanji with an い on the end, meaning you know it's probably an adjective. What's the adjective form of <kanji>white</kanji>? <vocabulary>White</vocabulary>, of course!
readingMnemonic: This has the same reading as the vocab 白 you learned, so you already know the reading. Go you!

meaningMnemonic: When a vocab word is a single kanji without okurigana (hiragana attached to the kanji) it usually has the same meaning as the kanji it's spawned from. If you know the kanji meaning you'll also know the vocab meaning. It's <vocabulary>one hundred</vocabulary>!
readingMnemonic: When a vocab word is a single kanji with no okurigana, it usually uses the kun'yomi reading. Numbers are an exception to this. Numbers use the on'yomi reading, which you've already learned by learning the kanji's reading! How nice!

meaningMnemonic: <kanji>Two</kanji> times <kanji>ten</kanji> <kanji>days</kanji> is <vocabulary>twenty days</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
Words that count days use an exceptional reading. The readings for 二十 are exceptions, and 日 takes the kun'yomi reading. Here's a mnemonic to help with that:

If you make one hat for <vocabulary>twenty days</vocabulary> you'll have enough <reading>hats</reading> (はつ) to fill up your <reading>car</reading> (か) completely! That's a lot of days and a lot of hats.

meaningMnemonic:
If you go to (ごと) a <kanji>circle</kanji>, you need to go to it <vocabulary>in its entirety</vocabulary>. That means you have to circle around the <vocabulary>whole</vocabulary> thing. Otherwise, it isn't even a circle. It's like a line or a squiggle or something.

You often hear this word when talking about food – like when biting into a whole apple or accidentally swallowing a grape without chewing. But it's not just for food: you can also use it to talk about memorizing a whole book from beginning to end, for example.
readingMnemonic: Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You learned this reading, so you should be able to read this too!

meaningMnemonic:
You <kanji>exit</kanji> but you do it with かける added to it. Just think of that かける as you are exiting while being "cocky" about it. You do this when you want <vocabulary>to go out</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>to leave</vocabulary>. You go out in a cocky way!

This word can only be used for a person going out. You can't say a train 出かける'd.
readingMnemonic: Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You learned this reading when you learned 出る, so if you can remember that, you should be able to remember this one too!

meaningMnemonic: If there is a <kanji>big</kanji> <kanji>energy</kanji> it's going to fill up the <vocabulary>atmosphere</vocabulary>! Think about big energies you feel in the atmosphere: thunderstorms, tornadoes, the aurora borealis. Just remember, this is the atmosphere in the sky, not the atmosphere in a fancy restaurant.
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Since 大 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use:

Imagine an <vocabulary>atmosphere</vocabulary> consisting entirely of <reading>tie</reading>s and <reading>key</reading>s (たいき), floating around. The ties are getting tangled together, and the keys keep bashing you on the head. Bring back the normal atmosphere, this one's horrible.

meaningMnemonic:
You've already learned that 口 means "mouth" and here it's followed by the particle に, which pinpoints a location. The verb する means "to do" so you're doing something to your mouth! Logically enough, this expression is used to describe lots of actions that you do with your mouth. It's often used to mean <vocabulary>to mention</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>to say</vocabulary>, but with an added descriptive nuance that you've said something accidentally or unexpectedly, maybe something you shouldn't have said! 🙊

It can also mean other things done with the mouth, like <vocabulary>to eat</vocabulary> and <vocabulary>to drink</vocabulary>!
readingMnemonic: In this expression, 口 is a standalone vocabulary word, so it uses the same reading that you learned when you learned the vocabulary.

meaningMnemonic:
You already encountered アメリカ in the previous level, so you should know that it means "America." Put <kanji>north</kanji> in front of this and you get <vocabulary>North America</vocabulary>, just like in English!

Note that 北アメリカ refers to the continent of North America, as opposed to the northern part of the USA. アメリカ on its own usually means the USA, but when it has "north" or "south" in front of it like this, it's always referring to the continents.
readingMnemonic: You might be tempted to read this using the on'yomi readings, but this actually uses kun'yomi. You learned that reading with the kanji, so you should have no problems reading this!

meaningMnemonic: When the <radical>moon</radical> is bright, it's easy to see that a <radical>pig</radical> is indeed a <kanji>pig</kanji>.
readingMnemonic: Legend has it that if you catch a <kanji>pig</kanji> in moonlight, it will tell you its secrets. Sure enough, this pig tells you it's from <reading>Bhuta</reading>n (ぶた).

meaningMnemonic: Who's someone who's just a <radical>mouth</radical> with <radical>legs</radical>? That's your know-it-all <kanji>older brother</kanji>, who won't shut up about anything.
readingMnemonic: You wish you could box your loud-mouth <kanji>older brother</kanji> up and send him far away to <reading>きょう</reading>と. きょうと is super far away from where you currently live, so just ship your older bro off so he can never bother you again.

meaningMnemonic:
The <radical>leader</radical> for the <radical>ceremony</radical> can't come so you need a <kanji>substitute</kanji> to <kanji>replace</kanji> them ASAP!

This kanji has another meaning too. The leader can't come to the ceremony because they forgot what <kanji>period</kanji> of time it was. If they think they're in the wrong <kanji>age</kanji>, they won't come because they're trying to lead some monkeys in the ceremony instead.
readingMnemonic: The <kanji>substitute</kanji> leader is wearing the wrong color clothes, so you have to <reading>dye</reading> (だい) them, like they did in the olden <kanji>age</kanji>s.

meaningMnemonic: You go down a <radical>slide</radical> made of <radical>branch</radical>es and your <kanji>skin</kanji> scrapes right off — ouch! Lucky for you, it was just your old outer layer of skin, and now you've got a fresh shiny layer. Yay for exfoliation! You're not sure what to do with this big, floppy layer of old skin, though…
readingMnemonic: Without your rough old <kanji>skin</kanji> to protect you, you feel the full <reading>he</reading>at (ひ) of the sun now. It burns… it burns! Yeah, you better protect that fresh new skin from the heat.

meaningMnemonic: You put a <radical>lid</radical> on your <radical>father</radical>. But, he won't taste good alone, so you <kanji>mix</kanji> in some other ingredients. They <kanji>mingle</kanji> inside the pot with him.
readingMnemonic: With a <kanji>mix</kanji> of your father and other ingredients <kanji>mingl</kanji>ing in your cooking pot, you lift up the lid... your father pops up. Your father is <reading>こう</reading>いち. He stares back at you with a big smile.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>meet</radical> radical and the <kanji>meet</kanji> kanji are the same!
readingMnemonic: What better way to <kanji>meet</kanji> new people than to start <reading>kay</reading>aking (かい)! Get into your kayak and just float out into the water. You'll eventually bump into someone else in a kayak and boom, you're meeting someone new!

meaningMnemonic: You see a <radical>triceratops</radical> in the distance. But, it's odd. It's perfectly <radical>pi</radical> shaped (that is, circular, like a pie). Some kind of piceratops, if you will. The reason they are this shape is because they want to absorb as much <kanji>light</kanji> as possible. They evolved to be round so they don't have any corners casting a shadow.
readingMnemonic: As you watch this majestic beast absorbing <kanji>light</kanji>, suddenly several dozen feral <reading>こう</reading>いちs burst out from the wilderness, spears in hand. Oh no! Those こういちs are hunting this rare piceratops!

meaningMnemonic: There's a <radical>mustache</radical> on the <radical>ground</radical>. Upon closer inspection, you see that the mustache on the ground is exactly the <kanji>same</kanji> as yours!
readingMnemonic:
When we need to remember the reading どう, we use the word "doughnut".

While this is already strange enough, you notice something stranger. Over a couple feet away is a <reading>dou</reading>ghnut (どう) who also has the <kanji>same</kanji> mustache as you (and the one that fell on the ground). The doughnut looks at you for a moment, then scampers off. This doughnut must be a mutant because normally doughnuts don't move.

meaningMnemonic: There's a <radical>mouth</radical> inside a <radical>mouth</radical>. But if you look even closer (not actually, but we're pretending), you can see another mouth inside that mouth... and another one inside of that one! It keeps going and going. Can you count the number of <kanji>times</kanji> there's a mouth inside a mouth? This kanji also means <kanji>revolve</kanji> too. Think of each "time" a thing happens, it loops back around and comes back to the beginning, revolving around again another time.
readingMnemonic: If you're having a hard time wrapping your head around the infinite number of <kanji>times</kanji> these mouths <kanji>revolve</kanji>, just picture yourself in a little <reading>kay</reading>ak (かい) going around and down through the mouths.

meaningMnemonic: How many <radical>evenings</radical> are there? There are <kanji>many</kanji>.
readingMnemonic:
To remember the reading た we use the word "taco."

Let's start with the kanji meaning: <kanji>many</kanji>. You go outside in the evening (because that's what this kanji is made up of) and are surprised when you're hit by <kanji>many</kanji> <reading>ta</reading>cos (た) flying from the sky. For some reason it's raining tacos, and of course you have no idea why.

meaningMnemonic: What superhero is most like a <radical>triceratops</radical>? …If you said <radical>Wolverine</radical>, you're <kanji>correct</kanji>! Why is that the <kanji>right</kanji> answer? Because the triceratops has three horns, and Wolverine has three blades coming out of his hands. It's obvious, isn't it? But you knew that of course.
readingMnemonic: Because you got the answer <kanji>correct</kanji>, you win a free trip to <reading>とう</reading>きょう. Congrats!

meaningMnemonic: You have a <radical>gun</radical> and you see some <radical>windows</radical>. What do you think you're going to do with this gun? Of course, you're going to shoot the windows... <kanji>every</kanji> single one of them. <kanji>Each</kanji> and every one.
readingMnemonic: Now that <kanji>every</kanji> window is broken, you feel pretty stupid because <reading>mi</reading>ce (まい) are pouring in through <kanji>each</kanji> one. You've made a way for mice to get into your house. That wasn't smart.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>rice</radical> radical and <kanji>rice</kanji> kanji are the same in looks and meaning. This kanji can also mean <kanji>America</kanji>, maybe because America is covered in rice or fields or something?
readingMnemonic: A ton of <kanji>rice</kanji> is grown in <kanji>America</kanji>. And yet you never see rice in American cuisine. So where's all that unused rice going? Actually, it all gets dumped into a big <reading>bay</reading> (べい). They have too much rice and no place to store it, so they've started using bays to store it.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>feathers</radical> radical and <kanji>feather</kanji> kanji are the same in looks as well as in meaning.
readingMnemonic: You've gathered <kanji>feather</kanji> after feather, like some kind of wannabe birdman or birdwoman, and now you're ready to fly. But as soon as you're about to take off, someone dumps <reading>honey</reading> (はね) on you, making your feathers all goopy.

meaningMnemonic: In a <radical>coffin</radical> there's a <radical>beggar</radical>, doing his begging. But it doesn't work, nobody helps him. So, he just begins to <kanji>think</kanji>: Why am I in this situation? How could I have avoided it? How will I get out? Think, dammit, think!!
readingMnemonic: What does the beggar <kanji>think</kanji> of? A thought bubble appears near his head. In it is a picture of <reading>こう</reading>いち! He's thinking that if こういち were here, he'd save him. Oh, if only こういち could save us all!

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>meat</radical> radical and <kanji>meat</kanji> are the same in looks as well as in meaning.
readingMnemonic: You know who loves <kanji>meat</kanji>? <reading>Nick</reading> (にく). You probably know a Nick — imagine him eating some succulent meat right now.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>self</radical> radical and <kanji>self</kanji> are the same in looks as well as in meaning.
readingMnemonic:
When we need to remember じ we use the word "Jesus."

Who said you should always look at your <kanji>self</kanji> before tossing stones (or whatever) at others? <reading>Je</reading>sus (じ) did. So, make sure you look at your own self before being a jerk.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>color</radical> radical and <kanji>color</kanji> are the same in looks as well as in meaning.
readingMnemonic: You already have <kanji>color</kanji> on the mind because you know the meaning of this kanji, so think of something colorful about yourself. Imagine your <reading>ear</reading> (いろ) being a weird color... green, purple, red, whatever. Just make it really bright and standing out.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>go</radical> radical and the <kanji>go</kanji> kanji are exactly the same!
readingMnemonic: "Oh, did he <vocabulary>go</vocabulary> again?" When <reading>こう</reading>いち is not around, that's what people often say because he goes to the <reading>gyo</reading>za (ぎょう) restaurant almost every day. こういち just loves gyoza so much, you always know where he is when he is not home.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>west</radical> radical and the <kanji>west</kanji> kanji are the same. Isn't that nice?
readingMnemonic: You go off to the <vocabulary>west</vocabulary>. It's the Wild West, even. There aren't many people out here, making it easy to find a <reading>niche</reading> (にし) for your snake oil business.

meaningMnemonic: Our <radical>leader</radical> has a <radical>lip ring</radical>. Wait, our leader has a lip ring? <kanji>WHAT</kanji>??
readingMnemonic: This leader with the lip ring wants one thing. <kanji>What</kanji> is it? A <reading>nanny</reading> (なに) who can make <reading>naan</reading> (なん). The leader loves naan because it's soft enough to eat with their lip ring on. And a nanny needs to make it because the leader can't do anything on their own.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> has a <radical>book</radical> all about the human <kanji>body</kanji>. Knowing about bodies is a privilege. One that only leaders get—it helps them control all their subjects, like you.
readingMnemonic: The leader's <kanji>body</kanji> book also taught her about something very body specific: how to tie a <reading>tie</reading> (たい). You can't have a tie on your body, no no. Those are for the leader and her smart body.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical>'s <radical>key</radical> is the key that <kanji>make</kanji>s all the other keys. It's sort of like the one ring to rule them all, the one ring to bind them... except with keys.
readingMnemonic: The leader <kanji>make</kanji>s all the keys and puts them in a <reading>sack</reading> (さく) to distribute them to the underlings, who then cut them into shape with a <reading>saw</reading> (さ).

meaningMnemonic: You have a <radical>mouth</radical> full of two massive <radical>drop</radical>s and… <radical>treasure</radical>?! Upon closer inspection, they appear to form some sort of <kanji>diagram</kanji>!
readingMnemonic: After decades of analyzing the <kanji>diagram</kanji>, scientists found out it leads to… a <reading>zoo</reading> (ず)?!

meaningMnemonic: You have a <radical>samurai</radical> with a <radical>flag</radical> on a <radical>stick</radical>. On his flag is the symbol 声, the kanji for <kanji>voice</kanji>. Why does he have that there? He feels like his Shogun isn't giving him and his fellow Samurai a voice, so he's brought a flag with the word "voice" written on it to protest.
readingMnemonic: You decide to join the samurai’s protest for more <vocabulary>voice</vocabulary> but immediately notice… they’re all <reading>子, eh</reading> (こえ)?!

meaningMnemonic: There is someone wearing <radical>horns</radical>, holding a <radical>bow</radical>, and going down a <radical>slide</radical> held up by a <radical>stick</radical>. What type of person do you imagine? I'd say someone who's an immature boy. That would be your <kanji>younger brother</kanji>.
readingMnemonic: Your <kanji>younger brother</kanji> loves playing dress-up so much that he's now started <reading>dye</reading>ing (だい) his hair all kinds of colors.

meaningMnemonic: You're walking at night when you discover a <radical>lantern</radical> covered entirely in <radical>hair</radical>, making it a very strange <kanji>shape</kanji>. In fact, this is a <kanji>form</kanji> you'd never expect a lantern to take.
readingMnemonic: You stare at the <kanji>shape</kanji> until you finally recognize it: <reading>ca</reading>ke (けい)! Well, it's hairy, glowing cake, but it's still shaped like a big slice of cake.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>ground</radical> is scattered with <radical>rice</radical>, which will cause the Predator to <kanji>come</kanji> because he loves rice. Then once he's gone, the <kanji>next</kanji> Predator will come to eat.
readingMnemonic: When they come, though, they're all disappointed. It turns out it's <reading>rye</reading> (らい) grains, and rye grains taste terrible. Yuck.

meaningMnemonic: Plant a <radical>spirit</radical> in the <radical>dirt</radical> before you start your <kanji>company</kanji> and it will be successful. That's what all the great, old Japanese companies did back in the olden days. That's why companies like Mitsubishi have been around for hundreds of years. They planted a spirit in the dirt to help their company grow, and grow it did!
readingMnemonic: Where did you learn about this method of <kanji>company</kanji> growing? From a <reading>sha</reading>man (しゃ), of course. Shamans aren't just spouting nonsense, they know what they're talking about. And spirits are right up their shaman alley.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>angle</radical> radical and the <kanji>angle</kanji> kanji are the same!
readingMnemonic: To see those kanji angles from every <kanji>angle</kanji>, you <reading>cock</reading> (かく) your head to the side.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>say</radical> radical and the <kanji>say</kanji> kanji are the same!
readingMnemonic: If you aren't sure what to <kanji>say</kanji>, just think about <reading>Gen</reading>ji (げん) and ask yourself, "What would Genji say?"

meaningMnemonic: <radical>Fins</radical> decorate a <radical>hat</radical>, with (presumably fish) <radical>mouths</radical> stitched to it. Who wears strange hats like this? Turns out, the <kanji>valley</kanji> people are the ones who wear these. I'm talking about the "Valley Girls" of Los Angeles, California. They're into the crazy fashion, and they'll wear anything if it's deemed "cool" or something like that.
readingMnemonic: Hanging around with all the <kanji>Valley</kanji> Girls in the sunny area of LA is also a robot. It's WALL-E's distant (and lazy, lounging) relative, <reading>TAN-E</reading> (たに). He sits around getting a tan in LA with the Valley Girls while his cousin picks up trash.

meaningMnemonic: The radical <radical>run</radical> and the kanji <kanji>run</kanji> are exactly the same!
readingMnemonic: You <kanji>run</kanji> and run and run and suddenly you feel your <reading>sou</reading>l (そう) leave your body. Still running, you turn around and see your soul running a few steps behind you.

meaningMnemonic: Someone on a <radical>scooter</radical> has an <radical>axe</radical>, and they're getting <kanji>near</kanji> to you really fast! Oh no, they're so <kanji>close</kanji> now, you need to get out of here!
readingMnemonic: As they draw <kanji>near</kanji>, you recognize who is riding the scooter: the <reading>Kin</reading>g (きん) of England! Behind him, a bunch of his guards also on scooters and wielding axes appear and move <kanji>close</kanji> to you.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>village</radical> you always think of first is your <kanji>hometown</kanji>.
readingMnemonic: Your <kanji>hometown</kanji> is where you <reading>sat</reading> (さと), nostalgia tugging at your heartstrings amid the endless rice fields and dirt.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>life</radical> radical is without the slide (as it sometimes is), but when you combine that with the <radical>winter</radical> radical, you have life during winter. What gives you life during the winter? It's the <kanji>wheat</kanji> you store away.
readingMnemonic: You found your <kanji>wheat</kanji>. Thank God. You'll survive. You have to take the wheat back to your house, though. On your way, you get <reading>mug</reading>ged (むぎ).

meaningMnemonic: You have a wild <radical>viking</radical> <radical>child</radical> you have to take care of. He won't do anything but <kanji>study</kanji>, surprisingly. You can't get him to eat, use the bathroom, or anything else. He just wants to study.
readingMnemonic: This crazy viking child who prefers <kanji>study</kanji> over pillaging (and other viking activities)... the other vikings just stop and <reading>gawk</reading> (がく) at him when they walk by.

meaningMnemonic: You have a <radical>tree</radical> and another <radical>tree</radical>. You basically have yourself a <kanji>forest</kanji>!
readingMnemonic: So, now you have this <kanji>forest</kanji> and you want to cut it down (so much money!). But, you don't have any tools, only your hands. You think... I've done karate for a while, so I can probably chop it down that way. You get in a chopping stance, then do your battle cry and hit: "<reading>HAYA ... .SHI******</reading>!" (はやし)

meaningMnemonic: What was the biggest <radical>hole</radical> in the <radical>construction</radical> of the world? The <kanji>sky</kanji>. If you think about it, the sky is like a big hole someone forgot to fill in. That's why this kanji also means <kanji>empty</kanji>! Makes sense — nothing is more empty than the sky.
readingMnemonic: The <kanji>sky</kanji> is usually empty until clouds stage a <reading>coup</reading> (くう). Basically you can think of the sky as the site for one coup after another as the weather changes.

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>gold</radical> radical and the <kanji>gold</kanji> kanji are the same!
readingMnemonic: The most <kanji>gold</kanji> in the land belongs to the <reading>Kin</reading>g (きん) of England. In fact, you've never actually met someone with gold, it all goes straight to the King whenever someone finds some. You just have a few pennies to work with.

meaningMnemonic: The radical for <radical>rain</radical> and the kanji for <kanji>rain</kanji> are the same thing.
readingMnemonic: What if the <kanji>rain</kanji> actually tried to <reading>aim</reading> (あめ) for you? As in, drops would move out of their normal flight path just to hit you on your head? You'd be a lot wetter, that's for sure.

meaningMnemonic:
The <radical>blue</radical> radical and the <kanji>blue</kanji> kanji are exactly the same.

Additionally, this can also mean <kanji>young</kanji>. You can remember this because young people have potential as limitless as the blue sky!
readingMnemonic: You've been feeling really <kanji>blue</kanji>. Mostly because your skin, hair, and eyes have all turned blue. It was an out-of-the-blue transformation! But now someone places his hand on your shoulder and points at your <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい). It's blue too! You aren't alone!

meaningMnemonic: You plant your <radical>flowers</radical> in the light of the <radical>sun</radical> in the shape of a <radical>cross</radical>, but it's too hot—the sun is too strong—and you end up with <kanji>grass</kanji>. Flowers can't grow in these conditions.
readingMnemonic: The <kanji>grass</kanji> on top is brown and burned from the sun, so you need to take a <reading>cool saw</reading> (くさ) and hack the tops off. The saw is actually physically cool to the touch, to help heal the grass' sunburned bits. It just happens to look cool too.

meaningMnemonic: For some reason, you are <radical>stand</radical>ing on the <radical>sun</radical>. It's obviously very hot, but the thing you notice most is the <kanji>sound</kanji> of you burning up.
readingMnemonic: This <kanji>sound</kanji> isn't just loud, it's familiar. Finally you place it: <reading>on</reading>ions (おん)!

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>night</radical> radical and the <kanji>night</kanji> kanji are exactly the same.
readingMnemonic: You call your friend at <kanji>night</kanji> and say "<reading>Yo, Lou</reading> (よる)!"

meaningMnemonic: The <radical>axe</radical> radical and the <kanji>axe</kanji> kanji are exactly the same, with one minor difference. This kanji is also used as the <kanji>bread loaf counter</kanji>, probably because bread was so big and hard in the past that they had to use axes to slice them.
readingMnemonic: You imagined being back in time too good and now you're stuck using an <kanji>axe</kanji> to be a <kanji>bread loaf counter</kanji> for the <reading>Kin</reading>g (きん) of England. You aren't sure which king he is or when in time you are, but he's mean and keeps eating all the bread you cut up with your axe.

meaningMnemonic: A single drop (丶) is just water. But as soon as there are three, rising higher and higher, you know what's coming. A <radical>tsunami</radical>! Think of those drops as wave crests. Three whole tsunami waves, stacking up? That's a big tsunami.
See the slide going into the dirt? What appears to be a slide here is actually a <radical>coffin</radical>, sliding into the dirt to be buried.
You know <radical>Wolverine</radical> from the X-Men? He has three claws that come out of his hand whenever he needs to fight, not at all unlike the radical you see here.
You have 十 plus 十 (never mind that the left one is kinda curvy)... What do you get when you add these two kanji together? You get <radical>twenty</radical>.
This is the radical for <radical>meat</radical>. What's meat-like about this? Think of a nice square slab of meat with some beautiful 人-shaped marbling lines in it. Seeing it now? Mmm, meat.

Remember the eye radical? Imagine that's your eye now, and there's a drop of paint falling into it, making your eye color unique. Now it's what makes you you. It's what makes you your <radical>self</radical>.

In prison, dreaming in bed is the only way to see <radical>color</radical>. Picture a dark, dank cell with four gray walls. Now imagine the colorful dreamscapes your bed would take you to every night. What a contrast! That's why a prison bed = color.

You have the radicals for loiter, two, and barb. Someone who uses two barbs on you while you're loitering really wants you to <radical>GO</radical>!! GO on, stop loitering outside of my convenience store, ya dang kids!

You have the legs of a bird with wings outspread, landing in the mouth of her nest to rest. And when does she go back to rest? When the sun sets in the <radical>west</radical>! Imagine all the birds flying home as the sun sets. That's why this radical means west.

You could also see this as pi coming out of a mouth. Why would a mouth be talking about pi? Probably to figure out the curvature of the sun's path as it moves across the sky — to the west, always to the west!

This looks like something hooked around the corner of a mouth — it's a <radical>lip ring</radical>! Use your imagination, because it's either a giant lip ring or a very small mouth. It's angular because kanji prefer straight lines, but try to picture it as the kind of studded, angular lip ring your trendy friend wore in high school.
This radical is a <radical>scooter</radical>. See the handlebars and the little light? And there's a platform at the bottom you can stand or sit on (if it's one of those nice motorized scooters).

This is the radical for <radical>axe</radical>, since it looks like one. The two horizontal strokes are the head, the long left line is the whole edge of its blade, and the vertical line in the middle is its short handle. Kind of a strange axe, I admit, but an axe is an axe.
There are a bunch of lines coming out of a mouth. Think of them as speech lines in a little speech bubble. Those lines represent the things you <radical>say</radical>.

Underneath that hat are two bars of <radical>gold</radical>, but under those there are some spikes. This is some kind of trap for people who try to take your gold. And why else would you make a trap? Everyone wants to protect their gold.
The towel is getting soaked with drops of liquid. That's because it's <radical>rain</radical>ing. Get your towels and other clothes inside before they get soaked through from this rain!

You have life on top of the moon (ignore that life is missing its little slide on the left side). What would life on the moon look like? Well, first of all, they have no air, so they probably look pretty <radical>blue</radical> from asphyxiation. When you meet them, it turns out you're right. They're a civilization of blue people. Imagine them all living up there, being blue and Smurfy.

See the <radical>mustache</radical> going around the mouth? Man, I wish I could grow a sweet mustache like this. (Sure, the mustache looks a lot like the head radical here, but just imagine it as a mustache this time!)
This is a very oddly shaped <radical>key</radical>, but it's a key nonetheless.

See the person running (to the right)? That's why this is the radical for <radical>run</radical>. Just don't confuse this with foot (足). The head in this one has streaks around it because the person is running so fast.

A rice paddy and some dirt — dirt roads, in fact, because this is a <radical>village</radical>. Small villages like this are mostly rice paddies and dirt (roads), you know? You could even think of this as a bird's-eye view of a village.

Under the hat there are two people <radical>meet</radical>ing in private. Look at them huddled up in there. They must have a reason to want to meet in such a private place, but that's not for you to judge. Maybe next time you have to meet with someone you'll hide under a hat with them too.

Think of an old-style <radical>lantern</radical>. It's in a box-shaped holder lined with rice paper and sits on four legs (you can only see two from this angle though). You can imagine people using these before electricity, right? That's why this radical is a lantern.
This radical already looks like a pair of rectangular <radical>feathers</radical>, right? I guess those short lines in each feather are like the filaments of the feathers.

Here we have the ground radical, with something underneath it. What likes lying in the ground, covered in mud? A <radical>pig</radical>! Use your imagination and see if you can find the head near the top, the piggy tail at the bottom right, and the little piglets on the left, nestling up to this mama pig.

When you're in prison, your only task is to count every <radical>angle</radical> in your cell. You count the angles of the walls and ceiling over and over again to pass the time. How many angles could there be in a room with four walls? Picture it and try to count those angles now.

See that lid on top? It's actually a flying saucer. "Take me to your leader," say the aliens inside, arriving from a strange-looking moon. When do you think you'd see something like this? At <radical>night</radical>. When people see UFOs or the moon, it's usually night. That's why this is the night radical.

On the top part of the head (冂) radical, there is a horn radical with one extra spike in the middle. This is a horned helmet with one extra spike. Who were famous for having horned helmets like that? The Vikings! That's why this radical is called <radical>Viking</radical>.

This looks like a stick-figure foot on the left kicking at something, which makes it the radical for <radical>kick</radical>. See the shin and foot swinging up for the kick? You can think of that part on the right as the edge of a ball that's about to be kicked.

<reading>Sorry</reading> (それ), can you grab <vocabulary>that one</vocabulary> for me? Yeah, <vocabulary>that</vocabulary>, thanks!

You can use それ for things that are close to the person you're talking to, or fairly close to both of you.

コンビニ comes from the English word <vocabulary>convenience store</vocabulary>, but who has time for all those syllables? That's why they cut it down to "conveni". So much more convenient that way.

Watch out for the “v” sound, which usually becomes “b” in Japanese, and this word is no exception.
デパート comes from  <vocabulary>department store</vocabulary>. Yet another shortened loanword! There's an extra "o" sound on the end of this one, since a word can't end in "t" in Japanese.

<reading>Sue me, Ma Sen!</reading> (すみません), you yell at Ma Sen, before quickly adding "<vocabulary>Excuse me</vocabulary>…<vocabulary>I'm sorry</vocabulary>, so <vocabulary> sorry</vocabulary>!" You shouldn't have shouted at Ma Sen like that. You hope she won’t actually sue you.

You can use すみません both to apologize and to get someone’s attention. This expression is really common in Japan, and dropping it in wherever you can will help you blend right in!

<reading>Eeh! Eh?</reading> (いいえ) <vocabulary>No</vocabulary>! You’re so surprised by whatever the question was, you can only manage "Eeh! Eh? No!" in response.

いいえ is a formal word for "no", so you can use it to answer politely in the negative.

"<reading>A gnat, ah!</reading> (あなた). I’m going to get <vocabulary>you</vocabulary>! Yes YOU," you shout as you swat at that pesky gnat buzzing around your head.

あなた is a lot less common than “you” in English, but you’ll come across it in adverts, online forums, and textbooks, where it’s used to address a general, anonymous audience. Some people also use it with their significant other, in which case it sounds intimate, like "darling". In casual conversations, though, it can come off as standoffish, so it's better to use someone's name or social title (like "teacher") instead.

This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji 入 means "enter," but this word does not mean "to enter" (which you already learned as 入る). Still, the meaning of 入れる is still related to the kanji's meaning: it means <vocabulary>to insert</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>to put in</vocabulary>. It's something you do to another object, like inserting a key into a lock, or your hand into a glove.
readingMnemonic:
When a vocab is a single kanji plus some okurigana (hiragana stuck on the end of kanji) it's almost always going to be the kun'yomi reading. This is one of two kun'yomi readings you'll be learning for the kanji 入. As long as you know that this vocab means <vocabulary>to insert</vocabulary>, you can use that to remember the reading as well. 

Imagine inserting your hand into a hole and an <reading>ea</reading>gle (い) flies out, screaming: "<reading>い</reading>!" This is something that happens every time you insert your hand into something: an eagle comes out. You don't know why.

meaningMnemonic:
This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji means <kanji>above</kanji>, and this verb means <vocabulary>to go up</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>to rise</vocabulary>.

It's an intransitive verb, so it doesn't take a direct object. For example: Prices go up. Temperatures rise. See? No direct object. You can remember this because stock prices always seems to go up when you see this one <reading>girl</reading> (がる). You don't know how this girl makes stock prices go up, she just does. Think of her as your lucky girl.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word has okurigana (hiragana attached to the kanji) you can guess that it uses the kun'yomi reading. You've learned that reading, but here's a mnemonic in case you need a refresher:

You know this word means <vocabulary>to go up</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>to rise</vocabulary>. Imagine just rising into the air, sort of floating up all of a sudden. What would you say? You'd probably scream "<ja><reading>Ah</reading></ja> (あ)!" the moment you started to go up. You know, out of surprise. Going up? Ah!

meaningMnemonic:
This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji means <kanji>below</kanji>, and 下がる means <vocabulary>to get lower</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>to go down</vocabulary>.

It's an intransitive verb, so it doesn't take a direct object. For example: Prices fall. Temperatures come down. See? No direct object. You can remember this because your fever only goes down when a <reading>girl</reading> (がる) takes care of you. It has to be a girl if you want your fever to come down!
readingMnemonic:
Since this word has okurigana (hiragana attached to the kanji) you know that it's probably going to be the kun'yomi reading. Let's use a mnemonic to remember the reading of the kanji:

You know it means <vocabulary>to fall</vocabulary> so instead of using a <reading>sa</reading>w (さ) to cut down the tree, you decide to let it come to you. It has to come down all by itself. Picture yourself placing a saw at the foot of a tree then sitting back and waiting for it to drop itself at your knees. (This could actually work, don't you judge my methods!)

meaningMnemonic: This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. You already learned that 出る means "to exit," and this is the transitive version of that. In other words, you're doing the action of causing something else to exit, so this word means to take out. When you take something out, you're making it exit where it previously was, right? 出す is a super versatile word that can also mean to turn in (an assignment, for example), to put out something (like the garbage or recycling), or to let out something (a sigh, a sound, or even a bodily fluid). It can also be a casual term for paying for something, since you're taking out your cash and handing it over.
readingMnemonic:
The だ reading is the part you want to remember here, so here's a mnemonic to help you out:

Your favorite thing <vocabulary>to take out</vocabulary> is your <reading>da</reading>d's (だ) credit card. You take it out whenever it's time for a shopping spree — which is all the time, basically. Your dad's okay with this, although you sometimes cause him <vocabulary>to let out</vocabulary> a wail when he sees his monthly statement. Thanks, Daddy — love you!

meaningMnemonic:
This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means "life" so what is the verb form of that? Would it be "to do life"? No, probably not. In this case it is <vocabulary>to give birth</vocabulary>, or <vocabulary>to produce</vocabulary>.

You'll have to differentiate this one from the word 生まれる as well. Just remember that when you give birth, you're the one who has to actually <reading>mo</reading>ve (む) your body by finding the right position and pushing with every ounce of strength you can muster. In contrast, when you're being born, you can just kind of lie there and enjoy the ride.
readingMnemonic:
You have to remember the う part of this word to be able to read it. You already learned this reading with 生まれる, but here's a mnemonic for you just in case you forgot: 

Imagine yourself giving birth, but not to a baby. Instead, it's an <reading>u</reading>ni (う) you're delivering. Luckily, uni aren't fully formed with their spikes at birth so it shouldn't be a problem. Ooof! What a relief!

meaningMnemonic:
This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means "stand" and this means <vocabulary>to stand something up</vocabulary>. It also can mean <vocabulary>to make something</vocabulary>, both in a physical sense, like making a sound, and a mental sense, like making a plan. 

Don't confuse this with 立つ. 立てる means that you're standing something up. As in, "I stood up the action figure" or "I will erect the flag." You're doing the action to something else.
readingMnemonic: Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You already learned the kun'yomi reading for this kanji with 立つ, so you should be good to go!

meaningMnemonic:
This word means <vocabulary>to understand</vocabulary>, which might surprise you if you remember that the kanji 分 means <kanji>part</kanji>. Think of it this way: to understand something you have to break it down into its parts to see how it works.

Watch out, though, because whereas in English you understand something, in Japanese the thing you understand is the grammatical subject (it's doing the action). So, a more literal meaning for this is <vocabulary>to be understandable</vocabulary>. In other words, there's no object, and 分かる is intransitive, meaning it happens on its own. The かる ending is a hint, because verbs with かる as okurigana (kana added to the kanji) are usually intransitive.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn that reading with this kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you:

Whenever you try <vocabulary>to understand</vocabulary> something, you just start screaming "<reading>WAAAAAAAAAHHHH</reading>" (わ). It helps you focus and concentrate on what you’re trying to understand, to the dismay of everyone around you. WAAAAAAAAAHHHH.

meaningMnemonic:
This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji means <kanji>stop</kanji>, so the verb 止まる means <vocabulary>to stop</vocabulary>. 

But this is the intransitive version, so it doesn't take a direct object. A train stops, or you stop at a red light. See? No direct object. You can remember this because you often have to stop when you see a 丸 (まる), a.k.a. a circle. Think about it — red lights, the cancel symbol, and no trespassing signs are all 丸. Even stop signs are… sort of 丸?
readingMnemonic: Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You learned this reading when you learned the 止める, so you should be able to read it already!

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well. This implies that it is your older brother, by the way. Not somebody else's.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading. You haven't learned this reading yet, so here's a mnemonic to help you:

Your <vocabulary>older brother</vocabulary> is different from everyone else's. He has odd powers... some would say powers of the force. Do you know what his name is? It's <reading>Ani</reading> (あに), short for Anikin... wait a sec, does that sound familiar?

meaningMnemonic: What do you think a <kanji>fat</kanji> <kanji>letter</kanji> is? If I were to guess, I'd say a <vocabulary>bold letter</vocabulary>. You know, like <vocabulary>bold text</vocabulary> on a screen.
readingMnemonic: The reading for this one is a bit odd. It combines the kun'yomi ふと from 太 with the on'yomi じ from 字. You learned both these readings with the kanji, so you should be OK here. You'll just have to remember it's a weird mix of kun'yomi and on'yomi. It kind of makes this word stand out… as if it were written in bold letters.

meaningMnemonic:
This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>see</kanji>, and 見える is <vocabulary>to be visible</vocabulary>, or <vocabulary>to be in sight</vocabulary>, though it's often more naturally translated into English as <vocabulary>can see</vocabulary>. It's used when you don't actively intend to see something, it just happens.

This verb can also mean <vocabulary>to seem</vocabulary>, when talking about how you perceive something you can see.
readingMnemonic: The reading for this word is the same as the one you learned with the kanji (the kun'yomi reading). So as long as you know that you should be able to read this word as well.

meaningMnemonic:
The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.

皮 refers to <vocabulary>skin</vocabulary> as something separate from the body. You can use 皮 for when your skin starts to peel off with sunburn, or for animal skin when it is being stripped off or turned into a <vocabulary>hide</vocabulary>. You can also use 皮 to describe the outer layer of fruits and vegetables, like apple skins or banana <vocabulary>peel</vocabulary>s.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading. You haven't learned this reading yet, so here's a mnemonic to help you:

As a human (we think), you have a lot of <vocabulary>skin</vocabulary>. But sometimes a normal shower isn't enough to wash all that skin. What do you do when you can't fit all your skin in the shower or the tub? You get your skin cleaned at the <reading>ca</reading>r <reading>wa</reading>sh, of course!

meaningMnemonic:
This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>mix</kanji> so the verb version is <vocabulary>to mix something</vocabulary>. 

It’s transitive, so you’re doing the mixing. With this word, the things you’ve mixed are still distinguishable from each other. Two types of fish in your aquarium, books of different genres on your shelf — that kind of thing.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn that reading with this kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you: 

Who always said not to mix your white and red socks in the laundry? That would be your <reading>Ma</reading> (ま).

meaningMnemonic: This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>meet</kanji> so the verb form of that is <vocabulary>to meet</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>to encounter</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn that reading with this kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you: 

You have to remember the あ part but let's include the う as well for this one. Who do you want <vocabulary>to meet</vocabulary> more than anyone? Think about it. Then, imagine meeting them. The first thing they do is grab your arm and pinch it. You yell out "<reading>OW</reading>!" (あう) and they laugh and run away.

meaningMnemonic:
Just like its kanji, this word also means <vocabulary>light</vocabulary>. In some cases, it can also refer to a <vocabulary>flash</vocabulary> of light.

This is the more general word for light compared to 明かり, which is why it's often used for sunlight as well.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading. When learning the kanji, you didn't learn that reading, so here's a mnemonic to help you with this word:

If you could shine <vocabulary>light</vocabulary> on just one thing in the world, it'd be <reading>hickory</reading> (ひかり) smoked bacon, of course! Why hickory? Because no other wood adds that perfect balance of sweet, smoky depth. Not applewood. Not maple. Only the hickory smoked kind deserves the spotlight!

meaningMnemonic: The <kanji>moon</kanji> <kanji>light</kanji> is exactly what it sounds like: it's <vocabulary>moonlight</vocabulary>! This word can also refer to a <vocabulary>moonbeam</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Since 月 has two on'yomi readings, and the reading here changes from げつ to げっ, here's a mnemonic to help you remember how to read this one:

Legend has it that the <vocabulary>moonlight</vocabulary> brings out a creature known as the <reading>Geck光</reading> (げっこう), an elusive species of gecko that shines in the moonlight. You've spent your whole life searching for this gecko. Now imagine spending yet another lonely night crawling around in the moonlit wilderness. Everyone thinks you're crazy, but you know you'll find that Geck光 someday!

meaningMnemonic: You have <kanji>sun</kanji> <kanji>light</kanji>. What kind of light would this be? Obviously <vocabulary>sunlight</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Since the にち reading of 日 becomes にっ here, we've made a mnemonic for you to remember this reading:

The best place for <vocabulary>sunlight</vocabulary> in Japan is <reading>Nikkō</reading> (にっこう). It's one of Japan's most beautiful national parks, and the perfect spot to catch some rays!

meaningMnemonic:
This word is the kanji <kanji>same</kanji> with hiragana attached, and it also means <vocabulary>same</vocabulary>.

It's a bit of an oddball among Japanese adjectives, because you don't need な or い to attach it to a noun. So it's usually considered to be a "special" な-adjective. Have a look at the context sentences to see it in action!
readingMnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn that reading with this kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you: 

When you think of the word "same" think <vocabulary>same</vocabulary> <reading>owner</reading> (おな). You're looking at a restaurant that has a sign that says, "New owners!" However, when you walk in you see it's the same terrible owner you remember from before. What a liar.

meaningMnemonic:
You have the <kanji>same</kanji> <kanji>day</kanji>, which comes out to <vocabulary>same day</vocabulary>.

Note that this word is quite formal. To say <vocabulary>the same day</vocabulary> more casually, just use 同じ日 (おなじひ)!
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Since 日 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use:

On the <vocabulary>same day</vocabulary>, you once ate two entire <reading>dou</reading>ghnuts. <reading>Gee, two</reading> (どうじつ) on the same day? How did that feel?

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: The reading is the on'yomi reading which you learned with the kanji. This is probably because this word gets attached to numbers usually, which makes it behave more like jukugo. So, you can have １回, ２回, ３回, or even １００回 if you're into that sort of thing.

meaningMnemonic:
This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>times</kanji>. There's no good verb for the word "times" but you can probably imagine something doing something over and over and over again. What word would represent this? It would be <vocabulary>to revolve</vocabulary>, <vocabulary>to go around</vocabulary>, or <vocabulary>to rotate</vocabulary>.

Bear in mind that this verb is intransitive, so it happens on its own. Something revolves or rotates, all by itself.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn that reading with this kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you: 

You have a bag of walnuts that you're bringing to a friend's house but you trip and drop them all on the ground. You shout, "<reading>Mah Wa</reading>lnuts! (まわ)" as they start <vocabulary>to revolve</vocabulary> around you on the ground. Picture your walnuts going around you, getting all dirty.

meaningMnemonic: <kanji>One</kanji> <kanji>times</kanji> is <vocabulary>one time</vocabulary>. How many times? Just one.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well. Take note that 一 is shortened (as it often is) to いっ.

meaningMnemonic: The <kanji>now</kanji> <kanji>times</kanji> is <vocabulary>this time</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: <kanji>Every</kanji> <kanji>times</kanji> is really just <vocabulary>every time</vocabulary>. Do you get your kanji readings correct 毎回?
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: This is a single kanji with an い on the end, meaning you know it's probably an adjective. What's the adjective form of <kanji>many</kanji>? It is also <vocabulary>many</vocabulary> (or you could say <vocabulary>much</vocabulary>). Speaking of which, there are so many kanji to learn, ammiright?
readingMnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn that reading with this kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you: 

Think of something that is <vocabulary>many</vocabulary>. Doesn't matter what it is as long as you imagine a ridiculous number of them. Many of them. So many that they start avalanching into you and crushing you. The last thing you yell is "<reading>おお</reading>!!!" as you get smushed.

meaningMnemonic: You have <kanji>many</kanji> <kanji>part</kanji>s. Look at them all. You have to choose one of the parts. You can't decide which one you want. This one? <vocabulary>Maybe</vocabulary>. How about this one? Hmm, <vocabulary>maybe</vocabulary> that one too.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic:
You know this is a verb because it ends with an う sound. The kanji for this word is <kanji>right</kanji> or <kanji>success</kanji>, so what's the verb form of that? Turns out it's a ton of things. But it's mostly for when things go right. As in <vocabulary>to be right</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>to be on target</vocabulary>. 

当たる is intransitive, so it doesn't take a direct object. Your answer is right, or your shot is on target. See? No direct object.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn that reading with this kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you to remember the あ portion.

When your guess turns out <vocabulary>to be right</vocabulary>, you can't help but shout, "<reading>Ah</reading> (あ)!" That "Ah" is your victory cry.

meaningMnemonic: This is the noun version of the kanji <kanji>right</kanji>. What happens when you get "a right?" You get <vocabulary>a success</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn that reading with this kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you: 

You have to remember the あ portion of this word to read it. What is the correct answer that will give you <vocabulary>a success</vocabulary>? It is the first answer, <reading>A</reading> (あ).

meaningMnemonic: <kanji>Every</kanji> <kanji>moon</kanji> is <vocabulary>every month</vocabulary> (as you know from all the month-related vocab). This can also mean <vocabulary>monthly</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
This word has the on'yomi reading for 毎 plus the kun'yomi reading for 月. That makes it まいつき. You know the まい reading pretty well, so here's a mnemonic to remember the つき part, just in case:

<vocabulary>Every month</vocabulary>, you make <reading>two</reading> <reading>key</reading>s (つき). Why? Imagine you're a key maker, and two keys is your <vocabulary>monthly</vocabulary> quota. It's a very light key quota, two keys, but that's why you're able to hit it every month.

meaningMnemonic: <kanji>Every</kanji> <kanji>day</kanji> is <vocabulary>daily</vocabulary> (or just <vocabulary>every day</vocabulary>).
readingMnemonic:
