This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Since 日 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use:

<vocabulary>Every day</vocabulary> you watch your favorite program, <reading>My</reading> <reading>Nietzsche</reading> (まいにち). It's a <vocabulary>daily</vocabulary> cartoon about Nietzsche and his life, which you follow religiously. Oh how much you love Nietzsche.

meaningMnemonic: You have <kanji>every</kanji> and <kanji>year</kanji> to combine into <vocabulary>every year</vocabulary> (<vocabulary>yearly</vocabulary> works as well).
readingMnemonic: The reading for 毎 is what you'd expect. The reading for 年 is the kun'yomi reading, though. So, use your knowledge of the vocab 年 to help yourself to remember the reading for this word. You'll need to remember that the reading for this word is strange to get it into that brain of yours.

meaningMnemonic:
The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well. 

Note that this word refers to the grains of rice more than something that's a meal. If you're eating rice at a meal you'd call it ごはん. This is like when you're talking about the rice in the fields or rice grains or something like that.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading. When learning the kanji, you didn't learn that reading, so here's a mnemonic to help you with this word: 

"Everyone, we have rice! <reading>Come</reading> (こめ) and get it!"

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: Since this word is made up of a single kanji with nothing attached it uses the kun'yomi reading. You learned this reading with the kanji, so you should be able to read this.

meaningMnemonic:
This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>think</kanji>, so the verb version is <vocabulary>to think about</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>to consider</vocabulary>. 

Note that this isn't the "to think" that is like "I think that he is a power ranger." No, this is more like the actual thinking action. That's why this word is "to think about" or "to consider" rather than plain old "to think" which is 思う.
readingMnemonic: If you want <vocabulary>to think</vocabulary>, the best way to do so is to do the <reading>conga</reading> (かんが). Imagine yourself doing the conga and thinking away. You're so inspired! The thoughts are flowing as freely as your sweet, slow dance moves.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: Luckily for you, this word uses the on'yomi reading that you learned even though it's a single kanji all on its own. So, you should know it already!

meaningMnemonic: What do you call <kanji>cow</kanji> <kanji>meat</kanji>? You call it <vocabulary>beef</vocabulary> (what's for dinner?).
readingMnemonic: Both readings for both kanji are the readings you learned with the kanji so you should be able to read this.

meaningMnemonic: The <kanji>skin</kanji> on the <kanji>meat</kanji> is kind of like the outer shell. It's not very nice, and people who eat only meat skin tend to use <vocabulary>sarcasm</vocabulary> and <vocabulary>irony</vocabulary>. That being said, Japanese people don't really understand sarcasm. So, maybe that's why "meat skin" means "sarcasm." Is this ironic? Now I'm not even sure.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word that uses the on'yomi readings of the kanji. You should be able to read this on your own.

meaningMnemonic: One’s <kanji>self</kanji> <kanji>part</kanji> is, after everything, still <vocabulary>oneself</vocabulary>. And the part that’s from my self is, well, <vocabulary>myself</vocabulary>. Everyone has their own part of their self. Pretty straightforward.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic:
By your<kanji>self</kanji>, you managed to <kanji>stand</kanji>. Nobody helped you. You stood up on your own, and you really showed your <vocabulary>independence</vocabulary> in the process!

自立 primarily refers to a person's <vocabulary>self-reliance</vocabulary> and <vocabulary>autonomy</vocabulary> — their ability to stand on their own, make decisions, and act independently (as opposed to the political independence of a country or something).
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it uses the kun'yomi reading. This is the reading you learned with the kanji, though, so enjoy.

meaningMnemonic:
The 々 character just repeats the kanji before it. In other words: <kanji>color</kanji>, <kanji>color</kanji>! But think figuratively, here — something with lots of different colors like this could also be described as <vocabulary>various</vocabulary>, which is exactly what this word means. 

色々 used to mean a variety of colors, but over time evolved into a more general word for a diverse variety of something. You'll often see it as an adjective, like 色々な人 (various people), but it also pops up when talking about mixed experiences or actions, as in 色々あったね (a lot happened) or 色々考えた (I thought about it in all kinds of ways). Kind of appropriate how this word has various uses, eh?
readingMnemonic: The reading for this kanji is just the kun'yomi reading twice. Since you've learned the kun'yomi reading already, you should be able to read this word as well. Nothing tricky about it.

meaningMnemonic: You'd think that <kanji>water</kanji> <kanji>color</kanji> would have something to do with watercolors, but it doesn't. No, it has to do with the color of water, which, ironically, is <vocabulary>sky blue</vocabulary> in English. <vocabulary>Light blue</vocabulary> works as well.
readingMnemonic: The reading for this word consists of the kun'yomi reading of <kanji>水</kanji> and the kun'yomi reading of <kanji>色</kanji>. For 水 you'll know this reading via your vocab studies. For 色 it just so happens to be the reading you learned with the kanji.

meaningMnemonic: This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>go</kanji> so the verb form of this is <vocabulary>to go</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn that reading with this kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you: 

You have to remember the い portion. To remember い we use the word "eagle." 

You want <vocabulary>to go</vocabulary> somewhere (anywhere!) but you can't. An <reading>ea</reading>gle (い) blocks your path. You try to go forward and it opens its wings and bites at you. You try to go back and it jumps at you. There's nowhere for you to go with this eagle standing there.

meaningMnemonic: This is a weird word. Call it exceptional. You'll see this at train stations, especially. You know the kanji means <kanji>go</kanji>. So, something is going somewhere. Now just use the き to help you to remember that this word means <vocabulary>train direction</vocabulary> (aka it tells you which direction a train is going), because this word is the key (き) to you knowing what direction you're going in so you don't get lost. You could also remember this word as "<vocabulary>bound for</vocabulary>" aka "Bound for Tokyo Station."
readingMnemonic:
The reading is also an exception. It can be いき, but instead we're focusing on the ゆき reading. You have to remember the ゆ portion, so think of it this way: 

Just think about the <vocabulary>train direction</vocabulary> that <reading>you</reading> (ゆ) need to go in. It's all about you. Not your friends or anyone else. You. You are the most important person at this train station.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading. You learned that reading with the kanji, but here's a mnemonic refresher just in case: 

You go off to the <vocabulary>west</vocabulary>. It's the Wild West, even. There aren't many people out here, making it easy to find a <reading>niche</reading> (にし) for your snake oil business.

meaningMnemonic:
<kanji>What</kanji> <kanji>thousand</kanji>? Could be five thousand, two thousand, or ten thousand, since you're asking. Let's generalize. How do you say all of those amounts at the same time? You say "<vocabulary>thousands</vocabulary>."

If this is used in a question, it can also mean <vocabulary>how many thousands</vocabulary>?
readingMnemonic: The reading is the kun'yomi なん for 何 and the rendaku'd on'yomi reading for 千 (ぜん) combined together. It's one of those weird words that mixes the on'yomi and kun'yomi readings.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic:
The reading for this word is the same as the reading you learned with the kanji. What? Yeah, that's what.

Heads up: The primary reading for 何 is なに, but it gets read as なん in certain situations. You'll eventually get the hang of which reading to use when as you encounter this word more, so don't worry about it too much and just keep learning!

meaningMnemonic: When you ask <kanji>what</kanji> <kanji>people</kanji> you're not actually asking "what people?" You're asking <vocabulary>how many people</vocabulary>. One thing you'll notice with Japanese and kanji is that 何, when added to other things, often means "how many _____" rather than "what ____." Just something you have to know and experience a bit to catch on to, but you will, I believe in you.
readingMnemonic:
This word uses the kun'yomi reading for 何 and the on'yomi reading for 人. Since 何 and 人 both have two readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which ones to use:

<vocabulary>How many people</vocabulary> eat <reading>naan</reading> at <reading>Nin</reading>tendo (なんにん)? You've been tasked with counting everybody eating naan for lunch at Nintendo, so you better get started!

meaningMnemonic: You have <kanji>what</kanji> <kanji>month</kanji> - as in: <vocabulary>what month</vocabulary> is it? It's １０月, you fool. Get a job, hippy.
readingMnemonic: This word uses the kun'yomi reading for 何 and the on'yomi reading for 月. You learned those readings with the kanji, but since 何 and 月 both have two readings, here's a mnemonic to help you out: What month will you choose to get naan guts (なんがつ) in? You've gotta pick a month to gorge yourself on naan until your guts are absolutely stuffed with it. But which month will you choose? Think about it! Note: 何月 isn't a specific month name, but as a question word, it is asking for a specific month on the calendar — which is why がつ gets used here.

meaningMnemonic: <kanji>What</kanji> <kanji>day</kanji> equals the fairly obvious translation "<vocabulary>what day</vocabulary>." But, it also means <vocabulary>how many days</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji, but 何 actually uses the kun'yomi reading here! And since 日 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember this reading:

<vocabulary>How many days</vocabulary> does it take you to bake a <reading>naan</reading> <reading>Nietzsche</reading> (なんにち)? It's not easy to make a Nietzsche entirely out of naan, so I wonder how many days it would take.

meaningMnemonic:
You have the kanji <kanji>what</kanji> and <kanji>year</kanji>, as in: "<vocabulary>what year</vocabulary> is it?" 

This also means <vocabulary>how many years</vocabulary> too, depending on the context. As in: "<vocabulary>How many years</vocabulary> did you play soccer?"
readingMnemonic: The readings are both the readings you learned with the separate kanji, meaning you should know them. How many years have you been studying kanji now? You should be able to read this one.

meaningMnemonic: <kanji>What</kanji> <kanji>times</kanji>? This doesn't make any sense. One thing you'll notice with Japanese and kanji is that 何, when added to other things, often means "how many _____" rather than "what ____." Just something you have to know and experience a bit to catch on to, but you will, I believe in you. So, for this one it's <vocabulary>how many times</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word with a slightly unique reading, where the first kanji uses the kun'yomi reading and the second uses the on'yomi reading. You've seen these readings before, so you should be able to read this alright.

meaningMnemonic:
This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>study</kanji> or <kanji>learning</kanji>. When you do these things, what ends up happening? You learn. That's why this word means <vocabulary>to learn</vocabulary>. 

There is another word that means "to learn" that is going to be more common, which is 習う. You'll learn this one later on. 学ぶ sounds a little more serious compared to 習う, so keep that in mind when you use it.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn that reading with this kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you: 

You have to remember the まな part to learn this word's reading. Think about a class you're taking. It's a manners class, and you're there <vocabulary>to learn</vocabulary> <reading>manner</reading>s (まな). Imagine this study of manners and imagine yourself practicing it.

meaningMnemonic: Who has a <kanji>study</kanji> <kanji>life</kanji>? Only one person that I know of, and that's a <vocabulary>student</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: Your <kanji>study</kanji> <kanji>year</kanji> is kind of like the years you've studied. Where do you study? School. What is a year of study in a school? It's your <vocabulary>school grade</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: You <kanji>enter</kanji> into a <kanji>study</kanji> (place). When does this happen? It happens when you get accepted into a school, aka <vocabulary>school admission</vocabulary>, <vocabulary>university admission</vocabulary>, or <vocabulary>entry into school</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: Someone with the <kanji>study</kanji> of <kanji>industry</kanji> is someone who creates things. That would be the study of <vocabulary>engineering</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: What's the <kanji>big</kanji> <kanji>study</kanji>? If I had to choose, I'd probably say this is <vocabulary>college</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>university</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Since 大 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use:

Your <vocabulary>university</vocabulary> is best known for its <reading>dye</reading> <reading>gawk</reading>ing (だいがく). It's a sport in which you gawk at dye, and your university is the best at it! Other universities might be known for their football or basketball teams, but yours is the best at dye gawking.

meaningMnemonic: When you <kanji>rest</kanji> from <kanji>school</kanji> you are taking an <vocabulary>absence from school</vocabulary>. This is only for very long absences though, not a day or a week, but months or years. Think about it like taking an extended leave of absence from school.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: Since this word is made up of a single kanji it uses the kun'yomi reading. You've already learned this reading with the kanji, though, so you should be able to read this.

meaningMnemonic:
The <kanji>sky</kanji> <kanji>energy</kanji>... once known as a mystical essence that kept us from flying into space. Now we know this is the <vocabulary>air</vocabulary>. It can also mean <vocabulary>atmosphere</vocabulary>, though, because that's what a bunch of air between the earth and space is called. ここの空気はいいね！

Figuratively, this can also refer to the <vocabulary>mood</vocabulary> that's in the air or room, but not a specific person's mood!
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading. When learning the kanji, you didn't learn that reading, so here's a mnemonic to help you with this word: 

In the sky, you are the ruler. You are known as "The <reading>soarer</reading>!" (そら). Imagine your theme song and make sure it includes "soarer of the sky" or something awesome like that.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: Since this word is made up of a single kanji you would normally think it uses the kun'yomi reading. Not this word, though. It still uses the on'yomi reading, which you learned with the kanji. Don't confuse this one with お金, though. That one uses kun'yomi which you know because it has a hiragana attached.

meaningMnemonic:
You have some <kanji>gold</kanji> <kanji>ball</kanji>s. What balls are more important than any other balls, at least for a dude? That would be his <vocabulary>testicles</vocabulary>. 

This word came about because tanuki scrotum skin would be used to pound down gold into sheets. Tanuki scrotums are apparently very good at this because they are very strong and very flexible. Strange, yeah? That's why Gold balls = testicles.

As you might have guessed, this word is slang and you'll probably want to avoid using it at the doctor's! It's a comical word that is used in casual situations.
readingMnemonic: This word uses the on'yomi for 金 and the kun'yomi for 玉. If you know the readings of your kanji, you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: The お is an honorific that makes this word a bit more fancy. When you're talking about money you gotta be fancy, after all. So, you're basically saying "gold" in a nicer way. What was gold back in the day? It was <vocabulary>money</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
The reading for 金 is different from the one you learned with the kanji. Makes sense since this is a single kanji with hiragana attached. Here's a mnemonic to help you: 

You've saved up a ton of money. With it you're going to buy something awesome. Then, suddenly someone hits you in the knees with a bat. Now you have to spend all your <vocabulary>money</vocabulary> buying a <reading>cane</reading> (かね). Sucks for you :(

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: Since this word is made up of a single kanji it uses the kun'yomi reading. Lucky for you, you learned this reading with the kanji.

meaningMnemonic: This is a single kanji with an い on the end, meaning you know it's probably an adjective. What's the adjective form of <kanji>blue</kanji>? It's still <vocabulary>blue</vocabulary>! It can also mean <vocabulary>unripe</vocabulary>, which might sound confusing, but it comes down to the fact that in Japanese there's some overlap between blue and green. So while in English we think of the "go" traffic light and unripe bananas as "green," in Japanese they're actually called "blue."
readingMnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn that reading with this kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you: 

You have to remember the あお portion to read this word. Think of <vocabulary>blue</vocabulary> things on your body. Those are bruises. Touch them. What would you say? <reading>OW!</reading> (あお).

meaningMnemonic:
Someone who is in their <kanji>young</kanji> <kanji>year</kanji>s is a <vocabulary>youth</vocabulary>, or <vocabulary>young man</vocabulary>. 

青年 refers to <vocabulary>young adult</vocabulary>s, usually in their late teens to early twenties. The word is gender-neutral in legal or official contexts, but in everyday use 青年 usually refers to young men.
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well. That said, this reading can be easy to confuse with other similar words, so here's a mnemonic to help you out:

The <vocabulary>youth</vocabulary> like to play with <reading>sa</reading>bers (せい) and <reading>nen</reading>doroids (ねん). Think about it: as you leave childhood and become a 青年, you want more mature and dangerous hobbies, right? So you move on from plastic swords and soft toys to playing with real sabers and nendoroid action figures. You're such a cool 青年.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it uses the kun'yomi reading. You learned this reading when you learned the kanji.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading. When learning the kanji, you didn't learn that reading, so here's a mnemonic to help you with this word: 

Did you hear that <vocabulary>sound</vocabulary>? It's barely <reading>audi</reading>ble (おと). Go ahead and say the word "audible" with the pronunciation of おと to help you to remember this.

meaningMnemonic: This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>make</kanji>. The verb form of that is <vocabulary>to make</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn that reading with this kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you: 

You have to remember the つく portion. In order to do that let's think about something that you're going to make. Imagine something really awesome. You complete it and look at your masterpiece. It's amazing. You realize right then and there that it's <reading>too coo</reading>l (つく) for the general public to see. You proceed to smash it. It's way too cool.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading. When learning the kanji, you didn't learn that reading, so here's a mnemonic to help you with this word: 

You have your body. It's nice - looking good! What would really mess up your body? It would be a <reading>car raider</reading> (からだ). I'm talking about a dude who is a raider of some kind driving a car. If he runs into your body, you're done for, so watch out for the car raider. 

Of course, this mnemonic is only meant to get you close to the reading. Hopefully it is close enough to get you to the actual reading, からだ.

meaningMnemonic: You have your <kanji>body</kanji> <kanji>power</kanji>. This is just another way to say <vocabulary>physical strength</vocabulary>. Watch out, if you use too much of your physical strength you may run out of physical <vocabulary>stamina</vocabulary> and <vocabulary>endurance</vocabulary> completely! They're tied together, so make sure to pace yourself.
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Since 力 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use:

Your <vocabulary>physical strength</vocabulary> and <vocabulary>stamina</vocabulary> are tied together with a <reading>tie</reading> <reading>lock</reading> (たいりょく). That's a lock made of ties that ties your strength and stamina up together, making it much easier to pace yourself.

meaningMnemonic:
The <kanji>body</kanji>'s <kanji>inside</kanji> means <vocabulary>inside the body</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>internal</vocabulary>.

体内 refers specifically to internal body stuff, so you'll often see it in medical and scientific contexts.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic:
When you <kanji>make</kanji> <kanji>writing</kanji>, what are you doing? Simply put, you're <vocabulary>writing</vocabulary>. If you want to be fancier about it, you could say this is <vocabulary>composition</vocabulary>.

Note that 作文 often refers to the end result of a writing process, like a finished essay, rather than the writing process itself. It’s also commonly associated with various writing tasks assigned to students in Japanese elementary or middle school.
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Since both 文 and 作 have two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which ones to use:

You can't stop writing <vocabulary>composition</vocabulary>s about <reading>sack</reading>s 'n' <reading>bun</reading>s (さくぶん). You're obsessed with them. You've been writing <vocabulary>essay</vocabulary> after essay about sacks 'n' buns. Stop <vocabulary>writing</vocabulary> about sacks 'n' buns!

meaningMnemonic:
What is the "<kanji>industry</kanji> of <kanji>make</kanji>"? Well, it's when you industriously make things, obviously. That can only be <vocabulary>handicraft</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>crafts</vocabulary>, often referring to craft activities or hands-on projects done in elementary or middle school!

But wait, 工作 isn't just about glue and popsicle sticks! It can also mean <vocabulary>maneuvering</vocabulary>, in the sense of pulling strings behind the scenes. So, whether you're crafting a masterpiece or scheming a clever plan, 工作 encompasses the wonders of crafting!
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Since 作 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use:

Your favorite <vocabulary>handicraft</vocabulary> is making <reading>こう</reading>いち <reading>sack</reading>s (さく). They're sacks that look exactly like こういち, and you're incredibly good at making them. Soon, the Tofugu offices will be decorated all over with your こういち sacks.

meaningMnemonic:
The <kanji>big</kanji> <kanji>make</kanji>! What is your big make? Bigger than anything else. It is your <vocabulary>major work</vocabulary>!

大作 means a large-scale, ambitious, or significant work. Depending on the context, it can refer to anything from an artistically accomplished <vocabulary>masterpiece</vocabulary> to a big-budget <vocabulary>blockbuster</vocabulary> movie.
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Since 大 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use:

Did you know that there's a <vocabulary>monumental work</vocabulary> about a <reading>tie</reading> <reading>sack</reading> (たいさく)? It's a <vocabulary>masterpiece</vocabulary> about a magic sack that produces tie after tie after tie after tie... A bit like The Magic Porridge Pot, but with a sack and ties instead.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: While words that are made up of a single kanji usually take the kun'yomi reading, this one takes the on'yomi reading. Luckily, this is the same reading you learned with the kanji, so it shouldn't be too hard to remember.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: The reading for this word is the same as the one you learned with the kanji making it easy to remember!

meaningMnemonic:
A <kanji>big</kanji> <kanji>voice</kanji> is a <vocabulary>loud voice</vocabulary>!

大声 usually just describes talking in a <vocabulary>big voice</vocabulary>, but it can also refer to shouting depending on the situation.
readingMnemonic: The reading for 声 is ごえ (rendaku'd version of the reading you know: こえ). The reading for 大 is the kun'yomi reading, like with the word 大きい (おおきい). Just imagine a loud voice saying "おお！."

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading. When learning the kanji, you didn't learn that reading, so here's a mnemonic to help you with this word: 

You look at your younger brother. He's a scheming one. He's smiling up at you. Why? Because he's lit a firecracker on your foot. It explodes, sending your toe flying. You yell <reading>"OH. Toe? TOE!"</reading> (おとうと) as you realize what's going on.

meaningMnemonic: You have an <kanji>older brother</kanji> and a <kanji>younger brother</kanji>. Wow! Both are here. These are <vocabulary>brothers</vocabulary>!
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word that uses the on'yomi readings of the kanji. You should be able to read this on your own.

meaningMnemonic: This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>come</kanji> so you know this word means <vocabulary>to come</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
The reading for this word is pretty strange. It changes depending on the type of verb form you use (it's a pesky exception verb). To remember the reading of this word, here's a mnemonic: 

"I want you <vocabulary>to come</vocabulary> to the <reading>coo</reading>l (く) side, Luke," says Darth Vader.
"What?" replies Luke.

Just replace this line into the famous line from Star Wars. Say it out loud. Do the Vader voice. It'll help you to remember.

meaningMnemonic: The <kanji>next</kanji> <kanji>month</kanji> is the <vocabulary>next month</vocabulary>! It isn't this month or the month before, it's the next one!
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. 月 has two on'yomi readings, but since this is NOT a specific month name, it's going to use the げつ reading. Here's a mnemonic to help you remember that:

<vocabulary>Next month</vocabulary> is when <reading>rye</reading> <reading>gets</reading> (らいげつ) into your diet. You're going to eat only rye bread and drink only rye beer for all of next month. Bet you can't wait!

meaningMnemonic: The <kanji>next</kanji> <kanji>year</kanji> is <vocabulary>next year</vocabulary>! It isn't last year or this year, it's the next one!
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic:
Things that are really <kanji>old</kanji> <kanji>come</kanji> <vocabulary>from ancient times</vocabulary>!

This is a relatively formal expression that’s used to talk about <vocabulary>age-old</vocabulary> things, like customs that have stood the test of time.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic:
When you have <kanji>year</kanji>s <kanji>com</kanji>ing and going, you know it is <vocabulary>for some years</vocabulary>. Don't confuse this one with 来年 which means "next year." The kanji 来 comes after the kanji for year, so instead of "coming year" it's "years that come (and go)." At least, you can think of it that way to differentiate. 

You can also add numbers to this word. For example ５年来 means something to the tune of "for five years." But this is a pretty formal word. Just keep that in mind.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: When you're doing something <kanji>inside</kanji> the <kanji>company</kanji> you're doing it <vocabulary>within the company</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>in-house</vocabulary>. For example, we do our kanji in-house. It gives it that artisan flavor.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: When you <kanji>enter</kanji> a <kanji>company</kanji> you're actually <vocabulary>joining a company</vocabulary>. Welcome, new recruit. Prepare to be hazed!
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: The <kanji>origin</kanji> <kanji>company</kanji> is where everything in your company comes from. This is your main base. This is your <vocabulary>headquarters</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: The place where your <kanji>company</kanji> <kanji>meet</kanji>s is your <vocabulary>company</vocabulary>. Sony is a company. Nintendo is a company. Etc.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic:
When the kanji for <kanji>angle</kanji> is used as a standalone word, it usually means <vocabulary>corner</vocabulary>. You can remember this because corners are pretty much the most common kind of angle you see in everyday life.

In math contexts, it can also mean <vocabulary>angle</vocabulary> — just like the kanji's meaning!
readingMnemonic:
This word has two different meanings and each one has its own reading.

When it's used for <vocabulary>corner</vocabulary>s, the reading is かど. Just picture a deck of <reading>card</reading>s (かど). What's the most important part of the card? The corner! The corners are where the numbers are. Now tape those cards into all the corners of your house. Why? Because it's artsy!

And the second meaning and reading pair is easy — <vocabulary>angle</vocabulary> and かく, which you already know from the kanji!

meaningMnemonic: When you have something with <kanji>three</kanji> <kanji>angle</kanji>s, what shape is that? It's a <vocabulary>triangle</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: What shape has <kanji>four</kanji> <kanji>angle</kanji>s? The most common one is a <vocabulary>square</vocabulary>, but it could also be a <vocabulary>rectangle</vocabulary> or even a <vocabulary>cube</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word that uses the on'yomi readings for the kanji. You've learned them both already, so you should be able to read this word!

meaningMnemonic: This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>say</kanji>, so the verb version of that is <vocabulary>to say</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn that reading with this kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you: 

To remember the reading い we use the word "eagle." 

You're trying <vocabulary>to say</vocabulary> something, but every time you try to say anything an <reading>ea</reading>gle (い) swoops down and pecks at your face then flies away. Just imagine yourself saying tiny bits of words, then an eagle crashing down on your face. Then, you wait in silence. Nothing happens. You try to say something again, and the eagle swoops down on you again.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading. This happens to be the reading you learned with the kanji, too, so don't feel like you're stuck in a valley of despair or anything. Rejoice!

meaningMnemonic: This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>run</kanji> so now you know the verb version of that is <vocabulary>to run</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn that reading with this kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you: 

You have to remember the はし portion. Think of yourself running. It's nice. As you run, though, you notice the ground changing into something weird and mushy. Everything is turning into <reading>hash</reading> (はし) browns. You start to get sucked in, and soon you can't run anymore. Smell the hash browns and taste them too. What a terrible run this was.

meaningMnemonic:
A vehicle that <kanji>run</kanji>-<kanji>go</kanji>es is <vocabulary>running</vocabulary> and it's <vocabulary>moving</vocabulary> you somewhere else. You're <vocabulary>traveling</vocabulary>!

This word is typically used to describe the movement of vehicles such as cars and trains. It carries a slightly formal or rigid tone, so keep an eye on our context sentences to make sure you’re using it right!
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: This is a single kanji with an い on the end, meaning you know it's probably an adjective. What's the adjective form of <kanji>near</kanji>? It's <vocabulary>close</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>nearby</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn that reading with this kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you.

Who do you want to keep <vocabulary>close</vocabulary> at all times? Your <reading>chica</reading> (ちか), of course. She's your strongest support, so when challenges come your way, you'll want her <vocabulary>nearby</vocabulary>, as close as possible.

meaningMnemonic:
The <kanji>near</kanji> <kanji>day</kanji> is a day coming very <vocabulary>soon</vocabulary>. It's a day coming <vocabulary>in a few days</vocabulary> or in the <vocabulary>coming days</vocabulary>. In other words, you wouldn't use this word for something that's going to happen in a few minutes, or a few months.

This is a relatively formal word that is commonly used in professional or official contexts.
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Since 日 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use:

<vocabulary>Soon</vocabulary>, the place where you live will have two <reading>kin</reading>gs. <reading>Gee, two</reading> (きんじつ) of them? Yep, yours is the first country ever to have two kings.

meaningMnemonic:
The <kanji>close</kanji> <kanji>year</kanji>s are years that aren't too long ago. They are the <vocabulary>recent years</vocabulary>.

This is a formal word that is often used in writing or formal speech.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic:
The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.

麦 refers to a group of European-style cereal grains, like wheat and barley but not rice and corn. To specify which 麦, you'll need to use compounds like 小麦 (wheat) or 大麦 (barley), though!
readingMnemonic: Since this word is made up of a single kanji that uses the kun'yomi reading which is the reading you learned with the kanji.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it should use the kun'yomi reading. Fortunately, this is the よる reading you learned with the kanji!

meaningMnemonic:
A <kanji>man</kanji> <kanji>child</kanji> is a <vocabulary>boy</vocabulary>.

There's a の particle stuck in the middle of this word. Here it just means the noun before の is modifying the noun after. 

But for now, all you really have to remember is that 男の子 means "boy."
readingMnemonic: If you know the kun'yomi readings of both of these kanji you should be okay. You have two separate single-kanji words put together by a の. That's why it's the kun'yomi reading - they're separate.

meaningMnemonic: When you think of a <kanji>fur</kanji> <kanji>insect</kanji> what do you think of? My mind immediately goes to a <vocabulary>caterpillar</vocabulary>. Imagine the furriest caterpillar you can and touch it. How does it feel? Furry?
readingMnemonic: The readings for this word are strange. Both use the kun'yomi readings - 虫 uses the reading you learned with the kanji, so that's easy. The 毛 part is like the vocab word 毛 you learned in the previous lesson.

meaningMnemonic: You have the <kanji>below</kanji> <kanji>town</kanji>. Does this sort of sound familiar? We have another word for it in English, but it's essentially the same thing: <vocabulary>downtown</vocabulary>. It isn't the downtown you might be used to though. In Japan, this is an old word that was used for the commercial districts literally, physically down (lower than) the main city. So picture an old, urban Japanese area!
readingMnemonic: The reading for this word is all kun'yomi despite being jukugo. Just think of sketchy things going on in the downtown, just like how there's sketchy kun'yomi readings for this word. You should know both the kun'yomi readings from the previous level, though, so if you do you know the reading for this word as well.

meaningMnemonic:
A <kanji>man</kanji> <kanji>person</kanji> is a <vocabulary>man</vocabulary>. 

There's a の particle stuck in the middle of this word. Here it just means the noun before の is modifying the noun after. 

Don't worry if that's hard to remember though. Just remember 男の人 means "man" (and that it's more polite than plain 男).
readingMnemonic: This word is actually two separate words combined with a particle. So, since you treat them like separate words, you read each of them with the kun'yomi readings (they are both single kanji words all alone). That being said, in level 4 you learned about the vocab readings for these separate words, so use that knowledge to remember the reading for this word as well.

meaningMnemonic: You know that 今 means now. What you probably don't know is that まで means "until." So, this word means <vocabulary>until now</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: The reading for this word is the kun'yomi reading which you learned previously when you learned the vocab word 今. You can tell this is a kun'yomi reading because it has hiragana attached. So if you know that, you should be able to read this word as well.

meaningMnemonic: When you have <kanji>fur</kanji> <kanji>string</kanji> (aka string made of fur) what do you think of? Hopefully <vocabulary>wool yarn</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: The readings for these kanji are the kun'yomi readings. With 毛 you should have learned this reading via the vocab word in the previous level. With 糸 it's actually the main reading for the kanji, so you should already know it.

meaningMnemonic: You'll have to learn this word in two parts. You already know the meaning of 今 because of the kanji, which means "now." The すぐ part, however, is a new word, meaning "soon." すぐ and "soon" even start with a similar sound, so maybe that can help you remember this! If you put 今 and すぐ together, you get "now soon." Put in better English, that's <vocabulary>at once</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>right now</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: Since you already know the vocab word 今 (read いま), you should be able to read this word as well.

meaningMnemonic:
This is the village you came from. This is where your parents live. This is your <vocabulary>hometown</vocabulary> (same meaning as the kanji you learned).

This word is old-fashioned and evokes a warm, nostalgic image of home. Often seen in folktales and traditional songs, it represents an idyllic, pastoral life.
readingMnemonic: The reading is the same as with the kanji you learned, so you should feel nice and "at home" with this one 😉

meaningMnemonic: What is <kanji>real</kanji> is <kanji>right</kanji>. It is the <vocabulary>reality</vocabulary>. It is also the <vocabulary>truth</vocabulary>. Don't believe anything that isn't <vocabulary>real</vocabulary> and <vocabulary>true</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well.
readingMnemonic: Reading is the same as what you learned with the kanji.

meaningMnemonic:
You learned 空 means "sky," but it can also mean "empty." An <kanji>empty</kanji> <kanji>car</kanji> literally means "empty car," but in practice, you'll usually see this lit up on a taxi to mean <vocabulary>open taxi</vocabulary>. If you spot it, feel free to hail it down!

You might also see 空車 at a parking lot. In that case, it means there's an <vocabulary>empty parking space</vocabulary> available.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word that uses the on'yomi readings of the kanji. You should be able to read this on your own.

meaningMnemonic: If you often <kanji>think</kanji> about <kanji>study</kanji>ing <kanji>old</kanji> things, it's called <vocabulary>archeology</vocabulary>. All archeology involves thinking about old things, at least a little bit.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word that uses the on'yomi readings of the kanji. You should be able to read this on your own.

meaningMnemonic: <kanji>One</kanji> <kanji>life</kanji> is all you get. It's your <vocabulary>whole life</vocabulary>. This can also mean <vocabulary>all through life</vocabulary>, and when it's used as an adjective it means <vocabulary>lifelong</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word that uses the on'yomi readings of the kanji. You didn't learn this reading for 生 though, so here's a mnemonic to help you:

Who has been in charge of you your <vocabulary>whole life</vocabulary>? The <reading>sho</reading>gun (しょう). He owns everyone and that's how it will stay for your <vocabulary>entire life</vocabulary>.

meaningMnemonic: Things you end up <kanji>doing</kanji> in the <kanji>grass</kanji> and not inside probably take a lot of big <vocabulary>gesture</vocabulary>s and <vocabulary>action</vocabulary>s. You have to do them outside on the grass because if you did them anywhere else you might break something.
readingMnemonic: The reading is a combination of on'yomi and kun'yomi. That being said, both readings for both kanji are the readings you learned with the kanji so you should be able to read this. Just watch out because rendaku makes くさ become ぐさ.

meaningMnemonic: The kanji and the word share one of the same meanings: <vocabulary>form</vocabulary>. It doesn't just mean form though, it means <vocabulary>tense</vocabulary> too. Like verb tense.
readingMnemonic: This has the same reading as the kanji you learned, meaning you know the reading!

meaningMnemonic: You want to <kanji>mix</kanji> opinions from <kanji>outside</kanji> your country into the way you handle things. That's called <vocabulary>diplomacy</vocabulary>. If you mix your country with the ones outside of your own, you'll all profit!
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word that uses the on'yomi readings of the kanji. You should be able to read this on your own.

meaningMnemonic: Remember how you learned that 生まれる means "to be born?" Well once you're born that place where it happened becomes your <vocabulary>birthplace</vocabulary>!
readingMnemonic: Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You learned this reading when you learned 生まれる. Just drop the る off the end and you have your reading!

meaningMnemonic: If all you can see is the <kanji>big</kanji> <kanji>body</kanji> of something, then you'll only get a <vocabulary>general</vocabulary> idea of what it is. You'll be able to say that it's <vocabulary>generally</vocabulary> a certain way, but that's <vocabulary>about</vocabulary> all. You'll get the <vocabulary>gist</vocabulary>, but no more.
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Since 大 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use:

<vocabulary>Generally</vocabulary>, it's a bad idea to <reading>dye</reading> your <reading>tie</reading>s (だいたい). The tie police isn't a big fan of it, and you wouldn't wanna get arrested!

meaningMnemonic: If you <kanji>study</kanji> <kanji>writing</kanji>, it's called <vocabulary>literature</vocabulary>. You'll get to study lots of things other people wrote before you!
readingMnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Since 文 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use:

All the great works of <vocabulary>literature</vocabulary> are about <reading>bun</reading>s (ぶん). That's the subtle thread running through all literature: buns.

meaningMnemonic: You want to talk about the <kanji>origin</kanji> where something <kanji>come</kanji>s from. That's where it is from <vocabulary>originally</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word that uses the on'yomi readings of the kanji. You should be able to read this on your own.

meaningMnemonic: This has the 〜 for counters, so you can bet this shares the <vocabulary>bread loaf counter</vocabulary> meaning with the kanji.
readingMnemonic: This has the same reading as the kanji you learned, meaning you know the reading!

meaningMnemonic: その means "that" or "the", and その plus <kanji>other</kanji> means things like <vocabulary>the others</vocabulary>, <vocabulary>other</vocabulary>, and <vocabulary>other than that</vocabulary>, depending on the context.
readingMnemonic: This has two readings, but luckily you learned one of them when you learned the kanji, and the other one when you learned the word 他. The two readings are usually interchangeable, too.

meaningMnemonic: Remember 北口 from a couple levels ago, and how 入り口 means "entrance" and 出口 means "exit?" Combine that 口 meaning with the kanji for <kanji>west</kanji>, and you get <vocabulary>west exit</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>west entrance</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This follows the same pattern as 入り口, 出口, and 北口, so it uses kun'yomi readings. And just like with those, make sure to watch out for the rendaku that changes くち to ぐち!

meaningMnemonic: What's a "third day moon"? It's a <vocabulary>crescent moon</vocabulary>. A crescent moon is the newly visible moon that appears on the third day of the month in the lunar calendar that Japanese people used back in the day.
readingMnemonic:
This word combines the vocab words 三日 and 月, so it uses their readings, except they're slightly different here: there's no small っ in 三日, and 月 has rendaku. Sounds complicated, but the changes actually make the word easier to pronounce. (Try it yourself — みかづき is easier to say than みっかつき, right?) Here's a mnemonic to remember the みか and づ parts, just in case:

You're out with your friend Mika one night when a <vocabulary>crescent moon</vocabulary> appears. What do you say to her? "<reading>Mika</reading>, <reading>du</reading>de (みかづ)! It's a crescent moon!" 

Hint: To get the character づ, type "du" on your keyboard.

meaningMnemonic: Remember how 上手 means that you're <vocabulary>good at</vocabulary> something? Well, this word uses the exact same kanji, and means the exact same thing!
readingMnemonic:
That い on the end is your clue that the reading is different, and you can forget about the readings you learned for each kanji. This is a native Japanese word, and the kanji were assigned to it based on the meaning alone. That means you just have to memorize the reading, so here's a mnemonic to help you:

When you see that someone's <vocabulary>good at</vocabulary> something, you say, "<reading>Ooh</reading>, <reading>my</reading> (うまい)!" at the top of your lungs. Try it! Think of something that impresses you and say "ooh my!" out loud.

meaningMnemonic: You have the number "thirty", and <kanji>age</kanji>. So this refers to <vocabulary>one's thirties</vocabulary>.
readingMnemonic: This uses on'yomi for all the kanji, so you should be good to go here!

meaningMnemonic:
When you buy something, you basically <kanji>substitute</kanji> it with <kanji>money</kanji>. That substitute money is the <vocabulary>cost</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>price</vocabulary> of the thing you're paying for.

There are a lot of money-related synonyms in Japanese, but keep in mind this one refers to the money you pay to get some stuff in return (usually goods), not to an expense, price, or value. It's "substitute money" after all!
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which means it uses on'yomi readings for both of the kanji. You already know those, so you should be good to go!

meaningMnemonic: Find a <kanji>flat</kanji> surface and <kanji>go</kanji> along it. Now you are running <vocabulary>parallel</vocabulary> to it.
readingMnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

meaningMnemonic:
When do things begin to go in the <kanji>evening</kanji> <kanji>direction</kanji>? That would be in the <vocabulary>early evening</vocabulary> or simply the <vocabulary>evening</vocabulary>.

夕方 refers to the period of the day between afternoon and nighttime, when the sun is starting to go down and it's gradually beginning to get darker outside.
readingMnemonic:
This word uses the kun'yomi readings for both kanji. You learned 夕's kun'yomi reading with the kanji, so you should be okay there. The 方 reading wasn't the main one learned, though, and plus it's rendaku'd, so here's a mnemonic to help you remember it:

<vocabulary>Early evening</vocabulary> is your curfew — you <reading>gotta</reading> (がた) be home by then. If you're out past <vocabulary>evening</vocabulary>, you'll miss dinner and get in trouble, so you gotta head home as soon as it starts getting dark. You GOTTA!
